claims.evidence: Research into Noom, Duolingo, Grammarly, Calm, Spotify, Future, and Flo

claims.text: Duolingo built a massive user base through word of mouth and no paid marketing by offering all learning content for free, because limiting free experience heavily slows organic growth since only payers can tell friends.
claims.evidence: Cem Kansu, VP of Product at Duolingo, explaining their free-first business model creating an organic growth engine

claims.text: Noom targeted one-month payback on paid acquisition which allowed immediate reinvestment into their performance marketing engine, making them less reliant on raising money to grow.
claims.evidence: Nikhil Jhunjhnuwala, VP of Growth at Noom, describing how they scaled from $3-4M to $60M+ revenue with a team under 10

claims.text: Future deliberately fired perfectly happy paying members in early cohorts to keep costs low and iterate on retention, only expanding operations once three-month retention consistently hit their anomalously high bar.
claims.evidence: Rishi Mandal, CEO of Future, describing their cohort-based iteration approach to achieving high retention

claims.text: Companies underestimate the power of experimentation volume; Noom ran up to six tests per week per PM, with 90% failing, because high velocity of learning beats high effort per test.
claims.evidence: Nikhil Jhunjhnuwala at Noom describing their experimentation culture of fast learning with large minimum detectable effects

newsletters.title: How to validate your startup idea
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons learned from Flexport, Vanta, Rec Room, LaunchDarkly, Pinwheel, Snackpass, Good Dog, and Cocoon—a guest post by Todd Jackson

claims.text: There are three common approaches to coming up with a startup idea: market first, experience ripe for improvement, and problem first, and these methods correlate with specific company categories.
claims.evidence: Todd Jackson's research into 8 companies: Vanta, Flexport, Cocoon, Good Dog, Snackpass, Rec Room, LaunchDarkly, and Pinwheel

claims.text: Vanta's founder knew she was onto something when people she had not even spoken to started reaching out directly to ask about using the product, indicating genuine market pull.
claims.evidence: Christina Cacioppo's experience validating Vanta by first building a spreadsheet MVP then seeing unprompted inbound interest

newsletters.title: What is a good growth rate
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks and insights from the world’s best investors

claims.text: For early-stage B2B businesses, investors focus on how quickly they ramp to $1M ARR after going live rather than month-over-month growth percentages, with reaching $1M ARR within 12 months considered good and 9 months considered great.
claims.evidence: Consensus from two dozen investors including partners at a16z, Sequoia, Founders Fund, Lightspeed, and others

claims.text: Best-in-class SaaS companies follow a triple-triple-double-double-double ARR growth pattern after reaching $1M in revenue.
claims.evidence: Multiple investors cited this pattern including Nina Achadjian at Index and Jonathan Golden at NEA: $1M to $3M to $9M to $18M to $36M

claims.text: For early-stage consumer businesses, investors focus on intensity of engagement, virality, and retention rather than revenue, with greater than 50% DAU/MAU or greater than 50% D30 retention considered excellent.
claims.evidence: Mike Vernal at Sequoia and other consumer investors describing their evaluation criteria

claims.text: It is a huge mistake to focus on the growth number before getting retention because you can hit great early growth by brute force with small numbers, but it will not translate to continued growth.
claims.evidence: Ellen Chisa of Boldstart warning against premature growth focus

newsletters.title: Preparing for a PM interview
newsletters.subtitle: My favorite resources for getting ready for product interviews

claims.text: PM interview preparation has three parts: research the product and company, practice with real people through mock interviews, and study frameworks and core PM skills.
claims.evidence: Based on interviewing hundreds of PMs; practice section placed before study because too many people study without practicing

claims.text: Preparing for PM interviews gives candidates a big leg up even with just a few hours of effort, despite interviewers believing they can see through preparation to a candidate's true PM nature.
claims.evidence: Author's experience interviewing hundreds of PMs over the years

newsletters.title: How to know when to stop
newsletters.subtitle: A guide to avoiding burnout and establishing balance in your life—by guest author Andy Johns

claims.text: Professionals should define their personal range of tolerance using three lists: intolerables to completely avoid, self-imposed boundaries that promote health, and a plan to flourish with items that put you in your optimal zone.
claims.evidence: Andy Johns developed this after heart issues at 35 while president of Wealthfront, borrowing from biological range of tolerance concept

claims.text: Mind hours, the time your nervous system is preoccupied with work stress outside of office hours, are the most emotionally taxing part of career progression and most people underestimate how many they accumulate.
claims.evidence: Andy Johns's personal experience showing that nights and weekends used for recovery early in career become consumed by mind hours at executive levels

claims.text: Nearly 50% of tech professionals surveyed had thought about changing careers at least three times because it was not worth it, and another 50% had cried because of work at least twice.
claims.evidence: LinkedIn surveys run by Andy Johns

claims.text: A career is better modeled as a series of S-curves stacked on top of each other, with acceleration phases followed by necessary recovery periods, rather than a single continuous upward trajectory.
claims.evidence: Andy Johns's career included three major acceleration phases each followed by burnout and 6+ months off for recovery

newsletters.title: The most important marketplace metrics to track
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I’m building a marketplace and am wondering, what KPIs should I focus on and track?

claims.text: The four most important marketplace metrics are fill rate (percentage of intentful sessions converting), bookings growth (completed transactions), supply growth (new active supply), and GMV growth (dollars through the system).
claims.evidence: Based on Airbnb experience and conversations with a dozen marketplace founders and investors including leaders at Whatnot, TaskRabbit, Peerspace, and Snackpass

claims.text: Fill rate is the ultimate measure of marketplace health because it bakes in supply quality, availability, and booking conversion, measuring whether people can consistently find what they want.
claims.evidence: Alex Taussig of Lightspeed evaluates every new marketplace startup primarily on this metric; TaskRabbit uses it as their north-star marketplace performance metric

claims.text: The founding insight for most great marketplace businesses is principally a 'liquidity hack' such as Uber paying drivers to circle neighborhoods, Airbnb paying for professional photos, and Faire guaranteeing items would sell.
claims.evidence: Alex Taussig of Lightspeed describing how the prime directive of a marketplace is to generate liquidity where none existed before

claims.text: 80% of marketplaces start out supply-constrained and about half stay that way, making supply growth a major priority for most marketplace businesses.
claims.evidence: Based on Lenny's prior marketplace research series

newsletters.title: How to interview product managers
newsletters.subtitle: Interview structure, skills to test, questions to ask, project examples, and more

claims.text: You need to talk to an average of 23 PM candidates to hire one great one, with only 13% of inbound applicants making it to the first screen.
claims.evidence: Data from Ashby platform analyzing over 75,000 recent PM applications across high-growth tech companies

claims.text: The six core IC PM skills to interview for are communication, collaboration, execution, strategy, impact, and product sense.
claims.evidence: Distilled from the 10 core skills of product management; product sense is not on career ladders but is tested in interviews

claims.text: The project portion of PM interviews is the most informative component because it is the only time you see a candidate tackle a chunky new problem, watch them in action, and discuss their approach.
claims.evidence: Merci Grace of Slack found project performance closely related to years-long success at the company

claims.text: Companies should treat closing a candidate almost exactly the same as closing an investor, because candidates are investing something even more valuable than capital: their time.
claims.evidence: Siqi Chen, CEO of Runway, uses the same pitch and demo for Series A investors and for candidates

newsletters.title: How to develop product sense
newsletters.subtitle: With guest author Jules Walter

claims.text: Product sense is not something you need to be born with; it is a learned skill that can be developed through deliberate practice in empathy and creativity.
claims.evidence: Jules Walter defines product sense as consistently crafting products with intended impact, built on empathy and creativity, and outlines four concrete practices to develop it.

claims.text: Observing users interacting with products two to four times per month, paying attention to micro details like facial expressions and hesitations, builds stronger product intuition than reading research reports.
claims.evidence: Jules Walter's experience at Slack showed that firsthand user observation revealed insights about mobile churn that led to experiments significantly increasing retention.

claims.text: Removing many small annoyances from a product adds up to something meaningfully valuable, even when no single fix moves metrics significantly.
claims.evidence: At Slack, Stewart Butterfield's obsession with details led to positive customer tickets thanking the team for thoughtfulness, even though users couldn't articulate exactly why they loved Slack.

claims.text: Spending time understanding and reframing user problems with strong constraints is more valuable than jumping to solutions, because good framing naturally narrows the solution space.
claims.evidence: Stewart Butterfield taught that proper problem framing sets opinionated constraints so only a few solutions remain, streamlining decision-making.

claims.text: Keeping track of emerging technology trends at both macro and micro levels helps PMs develop creative product solutions by understanding what is newly possible.
claims.evidence: Cash App's decision to support Bitcoin drove 76% of its 2020 revenue; Uber became possible due to smartphone GPS and Google Maps APIs; Figma leveraged performant WebGL.

newsletters.title: Freemium vs. trial
newsletters.subtitle: Going freemium, adding a trial, business model disruption, and more

claims.text: Making a product free is an acquisition strategy, not a monetization strategy; it lowers CAC, increases virality, and gets attention in crowded markets.
claims.evidence: Analysis of ~50 SaaS pricing models shows nearly every SaaS product offers some form of free offering to drive growth.

claims.text: Nearly 90% of freemium SaaS products also offer a free trial of their paid tier, meaning most companies do not need to choose between freemium and trial.
claims.evidence: Survey of ~50 SaaS companies found about 50% do both freemium and trial, and nearly all freemium products also offer a trial.

claims.text: Companies should go with a trial instead of freemium only if their product requires significant hand-holding, has complex integration needs, or has a high price point.
claims.evidence: Products like Okta, ServiceNow, and Snowflake use trials because they require complex integration or many stakeholders.

claims.text: Freemium should not be implemented until a company truly understands how to convert leads to customers, typically 2-3 years into the business.
claims.evidence: Patrick Campbell of ProfitWell advises that premature freemium increases noise and false positives when trying to find segment beachheads.

newsletters.title: What is good waitlist conversion
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks and advice for making the most of your waitlist

claims.text: Waitlist-to-free-signup conversion averages around 50% if users are admitted within a month, but drops below 20% if they wait over three months.
claims.evidence: Data collected from multiple founders across Twitter, email, and in-person conversations about their waitlist experiences.

claims.text: Time on the waitlist is by far the biggest factor in conversion rate, as people quickly lose interest and forget what they signed up for.
claims.evidence: Linear saw 30-50% conversion in the first few months dropping to 10% at 12 months; multiple founders confirmed this pattern.

claims.text: Running waitlist emails through a bounce filter before sending invites prevents spam folder issues that can significantly reduce conversion rates.
claims.evidence: Henrique Cruz at Rows found that releasing 5K invites at once caused hundreds of bounces leading to spam issues.

newsletters.title: What is good monthly churn
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks for good and great monthly churn, broken down by segment and price point

claims.text: Good monthly churn for B2C SaaS is 3-5%, for B2B SMB/Mid-Market is 2.5-5%, and for B2B Enterprise is 1-2%, with great being roughly half those figures.
claims.evidence: Consensus from 16 growth experts plus data from ProfitWell's dataset of 13,000 anonymized SaaS companies.

claims.text: The higher the price point, the lower you should expect target churn, because smaller businesses are more likely to go out of business and high-CAC companies cannot survive high churn.
claims.evidence: ProfitWell data shows acceptable churn at $5K+ monthly price points is below 0.35%; experts confirm the correlation.

claims.text: Churn should be decomposed into intentional vs involuntary and soft vs hard churn to identify actionable improvement areas like failed credit cards and forgotten passwords.
claims.evidence: Crystal Widjaja (CPO at Kumu, ex-Gojek) explains that overlooking involuntary churn causes companies to lose meaningful percentage points.

newsletters.title: How to get promoted
newsletters.subtitle: 7 concrete actions you can take to increase your odds

claims.text: People do not get promoted for doing their jobs well; they get promoted by demonstrating their potential to do more through taking on increased scope.
claims.evidence: Lenny's own experience: proactively mentoring junior PMs led to management promotion; leading cross-departmental projects led to senior PM promotion.

claims.text: The most underappreciated and often most powerful ingredient in getting promoted is having an influential champion who fights for you in the calibration process.
claims.evidence: Lenny's fastest career ramp-up occurred after moving to an influential manager who fought for him cycle after cycle.

claims.text: If you have been stuck at the same level for over two years despite actively working on promotion strategies, it is probably time to explore a new job.
claims.evidence: Lenny's observation across his 16-year career with approximately nine promotions across engineering and product management roles.

newsletters.title: Lessons from 140+ angel investments
newsletters.subtitle: Biggest surprises, how to get started, what to look for when evaluating companies, plus a ton of advice from many smart investors

claims.text: Angel investors are usually wrong about which investments will do best; only a third of the biggest winners were rated as high-conviction at the time of investing.
claims.evidence: Lenny's review of 140+ investments found that if he only invested in high-conviction deals, he would have missed two-thirds of his best returns.

claims.text: Angel investing is more about access to deals than picking ability; getting into hot rounds matters more than trying to find hidden gems.
claims.evidence: Over two-thirds of Lenny's biggest winners were hot deals at the time; AngelList data shows broad indexing into credible deals outperforms selective picking.

claims.text: Power laws are extremely real in angel investing: 70% of paper gains can come from a single company, and 80% from just four companies out of 140+.
claims.evidence: Lenny's direct portfolio data showing extreme concentration of returns across 140+ angel investments.

claims.text: The best way to build deal flow as an angel investor is to share great deals with other investors, as great deals function as currency in the investor community.
claims.evidence: Most of Lenny's investments shifted from friends' companies to deals shared by other angels and solo capitalists over time.

newsletters.title: The inside story of Facebook Marketplace
newsletters.subtitle: A guest post by Deb Liu

claims.text: Facebook Marketplace solved the classic two-sided marketplace cold start problem not by recruiting new supply, but by cross-posting existing items from commerce Groups into the new Marketplace tab.
claims.evidence: Deb Liu describes the one-checkbox cross-posting experience that seeded supply by leveraging items already listed in Facebook Groups.

claims.text: Facebook Marketplace users initially never used the search bar because years of Facebook usage trained them to scroll, requiring the team to add a prominent search button to change behavior.
claims.evidence: Research showed users instinctively scrolled on Facebook while searching on competitor apps; adding a visible search button permanently increased search participation.

claims.text: Trust through real identity was Facebook Marketplace's key differentiator over anonymous platforms like Craigslist, especially for high-consideration categories like cars and rentals.
claims.evidence: Deb Liu's husband reviewed buyer profiles before meeting for a minivan sale; Facebook identity and tenure on platform provided comfort for in-person transactions.

newsletters.title: Differentiating your product
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I know differentiation is important, but why is that, and what are ways we can differentiate our product?

claims.text: There are only two paths to winning a market according to Michael Porter: performing activities better than rivals (operational effectiveness) or performing different activities (differentiation), and differentiation is more sustainable long-term.
claims.evidence: Porter's research shows competitors copy operational improvements and erode margins, making differentiation the more durable strategy.

claims.text: There are seven common ways to differentiate a product: be cheapest, highest quality, most convenient, safest, proprietary, feel-good to buy, or focused on a niche underserved market.
claims.evidence: Analysis of hundreds of companies including Apple, Robinhood, DuckDuckGo, Patagonia, and DoorDash mapped to these seven categories.

claims.text: Combining multiple differentiators creates an even stronger competitive position than relying on a single one.
claims.evidence: Patagonia combines highest quality plus feel-good; Apple combines highest quality plus safest; Airbnb combined cheapest plus proprietary supply.

newsletters.title: Prioritizing at startups
newsletters.subtitle: A guide for prioritizing at an early-stage B2B startup

claims.text: The single goal of an early-stage pre-PMF startup should be to make 10 customers very happy, and everything prioritized should serve this goal.
claims.evidence: Founders from Ashby, Vanta, Stytch, Writer, Zip, and Mage confirm this approach; if you cannot make 10 customers very happy, you likely do not have a business.

claims.text: Early-stage startups should not try to be data-driven because they simply do not have enough data for statistical significance; a 10% improvement at 20% conversion requires over 5,000 users.
claims.evidence: Sample size calculator shows the data requirements are far beyond what most early-stage startups have.

claims.text: Startups should not build everything users ask for but instead focus on understanding user pain points and independently designing the best solutions.
claims.evidence: Julianna Lamb at Stytch explains that building what customers ask for without understanding the root problem leads to one-off solutions instead of universally applicable products.

newsletters.title: What is a good payback period?
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks for good, great, and exceptional payback periods based on guidance from 16 growth experts

claims.text: For B2C businesses a payback period under 1 month is great and under 6 months is good; for B2B SMB under 6 months is great and under 12 months is good; for B2B Enterprise under 12 months is great and under 18 months is good.
claims.evidence: Consensus from 16 growth experts including Andrew Chen, Casey Winters, Elena Verna, and Dan Hockenmaier.

claims.text: Startups should focus on payback periods over LTV/CAC ratios because accurately calculating LTV for an early-stage company is highly suspect due to limited data.
claims.evidence: Casey Winters explains that early-stage startups have very little data to predict lifetime value, making long payback periods especially risky.

claims.text: One of the highest-ROI optimization hacks for mid-stage companies is running in-app promos and emails to convert monthly subscribers to annual plans, effectively getting customer loans to fund growth.
claims.evidence: ChenLi Wang explains that annual plans create positive cash cycles, with some companies pricing annual at 6x monthly just to fuel growth.

newsletters.title: The Atomic Network
newsletters.subtitle: Exclusive excerpt from Andrew Chen’s new book, The Cold Start Problem

claims.text: The atomic network is the smallest network needed that can stand on its own, and if you can build one, you can build many more by repeating the same playbook.
claims.evidence: Andrew Chen's research shows Slack's atomic network was under 10 people on a team, while credit cards needed an entire city; the concept scales from there.

claims.text: A product's first atomic network is probably smaller and more specific than expected, often on the order of hundreds of people at a specific moment in time rather than a city or segment.
claims.evidence: Uber's earliest focus was '5pm at the Caltrain station at 5th and King St' rather than all of San Francisco.

claims.text: The next big thing will start out looking like it is for a niche network, which is why new networked products are easy to underestimate.
claims.evidence: Andrew Chen extends Clayton Christensen's Disruption Theory: products look like toys AND target weird niches, making market-size objections misleading.

newsletters.title: The most common pitfalls of new product managers
newsletters.subtitle: The Coordinator, The Dictator, The Dreamer, The Feature Factory, and The Busted Umbrella

claims.text: The five most common pitfalls for new PMs are: being a Coordinator (no point of view), a Dictator (micro-managing), a Dreamer (too much strategy), a Feature Factory (shipping without strategy), and a Busted Umbrella (failing to shield the team).
claims.evidence: Lenny's observations across his PM career and from working with many PMs, mapped to five distinct failure modes.

claims.text: Shipping is not success; success is shipping work that has meaningful business impact, high ROI relative to alternatives, and moves you closer to your strategy.
claims.evidence: The Feature Factory pitfall: teams without strategy ship constantly but go nowhere because they lack a filter for what matters.

newsletters.title: A product manager’s guide to web3
newsletters.subtitle: How PM'ing in web3 is different, the pros and cons of going web3, how to break in, and how to be successful—by guest author Jason Shah, head of product at Alchemy

claims.text: Web3 PMs are responsible for the success of communities rather than increasing acquisition, engagement, or revenue metrics; the role prioritizes execution over vision.
claims.evidence: Jason Shah at Alchemy describes how web3 PM emphasizes building, marketing, and iterating rather than planning, managing, and measuring, with 95/5 execution/vision split.

claims.text: Product managers are not yet common or well understood in web3 because the 0-1 phase depends more on technical infrastructure, cryptoeconomics, and community than on traditional PM skills.
claims.evidence: Nearly half of 20+ web3 teams Jason Shah explored roles with in 2021 did not understand what PMs were for; the Ethereum Foundation has 97 employees with virtually zero PMs.

claims.text: In web3, open-source code and portable user data mean companies cannot rely on proprietary code, trade secrets, or data moats as competitive advantages, forcing continuous product improvement.
claims.evidence: Sushiswap forked Uniswap and conducted a vampire attack; any competitor can fork your product and compete instantly.

newsletters.title: Become a more technical product manager
newsletters.subtitle: Technical foundations for product managers

claims.text: A technical background is a superpower for product managers because it enables better decisions, understanding of trade-offs, more accurate estimates, and more confident communication with engineers.
claims.evidence: Lenny and Colin Matthews explain that with AI increasingly infused into products, it is even more important that PMs understand technical basics.

claims.text: Almost every software product is built with the same three fundamental parts: a client, a server, and a database, and understanding this architecture helps PMs collaborate more effectively.
claims.evidence: Colin Matthews' breakdown of application architecture shows how these three components interact through APIs in products like Airbnb and OpenAI.

claims.text: Testing is a high-leverage area for PMs to quickly improve product quality; a quick round of end-to-end testing with engineering before a major release catches issues before production.
claims.evidence: Colin Matthews explains that engineers often do not fully understand the whole codebase, making PM-led E2E testing especially valuable.

newsletters.title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
newsletters.subtitle: Celebrating 10 million downloads 🎉

claims.text: Instead of trying to make something a lot of people like, create something that 100 people love, because once you have that foothold, growth follows through word of mouth and better guests.
claims.evidence: Lenny applied this advice to launch a niche PM podcast that became a top 10 global technology podcast.

claims.text: YouTube is the single best channel for podcast discovery because over half of new listeners come from YouTube's algorithms, a growth channel unmatched by any other platform.
claims.evidence: Lenny's data showing over half of podcast views come from YouTube sending new people to his content.

claims.text: Aim for B+ quality when starting a podcast and work up from there, because putting too much pressure on perfection prevents you from starting at all.
claims.evidence: Lenny shows early episodes of Lex Fridman, Huberman Lab, and other now-popular podcasts that were clearly not A+ quality on day one.

newsletters.title: What is product management
newsletters.subtitle: My take on the PM role

claims.text: A PM's job is to deliver business impact by marshaling the resources of their team to identify and solve the most impactful customer problems.
claims.evidence: Lenny's synthesis from analyzing dozens of PM career ladders and surveying ~1,000 PMs across hundreds of companies.

claims.text: The PM role varies dramatically across companies along two axes: which skills matter most and how much influence a PM has, with Zynga at the mini-CEO end and Apple at the project-manager end.
claims.evidence: Survey data and career ladder analysis across companies like Google, Meta, Stripe, Netflix, and Airbnb showing wide variation.

newsletters.title: Six rules of hiring for growth
newsletters.subtitle: How to hire and build your growth team—guest post by Elena Verna

claims.text: Founding teams should create the first growth model themselves rather than outsourcing it to a Head of Growth hire, because only they understand the DNA of the product and market deeply enough.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna argues that a new Head of Growth will likely copy-paste from past experience rather than build an authentic model aligned with product-market fit.

claims.text: A growth model consists of growth levers (acquisition, retention, monetization) and growth motions (product-led, sales-led, marketing-led), and the unique combination creates a defensible growth strategy.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna shows how SurveyMonkey and Qualtrics competed with nearly the same product but won different segments by applying different growth motions.

claims.text: The first growth hire should be a Builder generalist who proves the growth model's validity, not an Innovator who thinks big or an Optimizer who squeezes marginal gains.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna's framework of three growth profiles (Innovators, Builders, Optimizers) with sequencing advice based on whether the initial growth model hypothesis proves correct.

newsletters.title: The top 5 things PMs should know about engineering
newsletters.subtitle: Guest post by Justin Gage, writer of Technically and growth at Retool

claims.text: PMs should learn their company's tech stack, sensitive points in the codebase, the build and deploy process, how to contribute small code changes, and technical basics.
claims.evidence: Justin Gage's five areas map to practical PM effectiveness: understanding trade-offs, avoiding delays, and building engineer trust.

claims.text: Being able to make small frontend changes like copy and color fixes yourself will result in more polished products shipped faster and win you points with engineers.
claims.evidence: Justin Gage suggests PMs learn to open PRs for small self-contained changes rather than relying on engineers for typo fixes.

newsletters.title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
newsletters.subtitle: Issue 76

claims.text: Great PMs take pride in the clarity and conciseness of their documents, emails, presentations, and meetings because people judge the quality of thinking by the quality of communication.
claims.evidence: Lenny's observation over years of watching the best PMs, noting that effective communication is the most fundamental PM skill.

claims.text: Great PMs build an aura of 'I have got this' by rarely dropping balls, coming prepared, and ensuring colleagues know that assigned tasks will get done.
claims.evidence: Practical suggestions include being selective with commitments, maintaining a Waiting For list, and communicating priority of new requests.

newsletters.title: Picking a wedge
newsletters.subtitle: When, why, and how to pick a wedge for your product

claims.text: A wedge is a strategy to win a large market by initially capturing a tiny part of a larger market or a large part of a small adjacent market, then expanding from that foothold.
claims.evidence: Twilio started with SMS APIs and became a $60B communication platform; Airbnb started with room rentals and captured 20% of the $90B vacation rental market; Amazon started with books.

claims.text: A wedge is most essential when going after a market that is entrenched or crowded; companies like Zoom, Slack, and Notion won large markets straight-on without a wedge by having an amazing product.
claims.evidence: Datadog CEO Olivier Pomel confirms that some product categories like infrastructure management require broad initial capability and do not work with a wedge.

newsletters.title: Finding your distribution advantage
newsletters.subtitle: Seven ways to break through the noise

claims.text: Poor distribution, not product, is the number one cause of startup failure, and getting your product in front of the right people efficiently is increasingly what separates winners from losers.
claims.evidence: Peter Thiel quote supported by data showing free channels like SEO are getting crowded, CPMs are rising, and privacy features are making paid acquisition harder.

claims.text: There are seven early-stage distribution advantages: pre-existing audience, unique viral loop, being first on an emerging platform, remarkable story, pre-existing relationships, strategic partnerships, and extraordinary hustle.
claims.evidence: Examples include Kylie Cosmetics (audience), Tesla (Elon's audience), DoorDash (hustle in Tier 2/3 markets), and Clubhouse (first on audio social).

newsletters.title: Why now
newsletters.subtitle: Why it matters for startups to have a strong 'why now'—and also why it doesn't

claims.text: A strong 'why now' is helpful but not essential for building a large business; companies like SpaceX, Airbnb, Pinterest, DoorDash, and Facebook had little or no why-now at founding.
claims.evidence: Research with investors including Sarah Tavel, Ann Miura-Ko, and Garry Tan shows why-now creates advantages but is not mandatory.

claims.text: A strong why-now creates one of two advantages: it unlocks the ability to provide a 10x better product (like smartphones enabling Uber) or it creates a new untapped market need (like remote work enabling Zoom).
claims.evidence: Mapped dozens of companies to their why-now sources including technology shifts (Figma/WebGL), regulatory changes (Eaze/marijuana legalization), and social changes (remote work).

newsletters.title: Types of business models
newsletters.subtitle: Issue 77

claims.text: There are eight fundamental business model types: sell a thing, rent a thing, take a cut, charge a subscription, charge for usage, sell access, sell attention, and charge per transaction.
claims.evidence: Lenny's original taxonomy mapping all known business models to eight categories with examples like Stripe (take a cut), Netflix (subscription), AWS (usage).

newsletters.title: The most important consumer metrics to track
newsletters.subtitle: Which metrics matter most when building a B2C product

claims.text: The most important metrics to track vary significantly by consumer business type: trial-based subscriptions, freemium subscriptions, ad-based, marketplaces, and DTC each require different metric sets.
claims.evidence: Framework developed with Sriram Krishnan and Yuriy Timen mapping specific metrics to five consumer business types.

claims.text: Greater focus on fewer metrics leads to greater impact; teams should narrow to the two or three metrics that most directly drive business success.
claims.evidence: Lenny's repeated experience across multiple companies finding that excessive metric tracking dilutes effort.

newsletters.title: On taking time off
newsletters.subtitle: By special guest contributor DJ DiDonna, founder of The Sabbatical Project

claims.text: Sabbaticals are a peak life experience ranked alongside having a baby or getting married, and not a single person out of 250+ interviewed has regretted taking extended time off.
claims.evidence: DJ DiDonna's research at The Sabbatical Project surveying 250+ people from all walks of life, including those who got divorced or cared for dying loved ones during their sabbatical.

claims.text: The two most important principles for an effective sabbatical are taking enough time off and actually disconnecting from work.
claims.evidence: DJ DiDonna's research showing that partial disconnection undermines the transformational benefits of extended leave.

newsletters.title: Three myths about sabbatical programs
newsletters.subtitle: With return guest DJ DiDonna

claims.text: The three biggest myths about corporate sabbatical programs are that everyone will leave, the company will fall apart, and that people do not really need them.
claims.evidence: DJ DiDonna's research across law, consulting, fintech, and nonprofit organizations showing all three fears are unfounded.

claims.text: Nearly one in seven US companies now provide extended leave to employees, and sabbatical programs have become prevalent in the employee benefits arms race for top talent.
claims.evidence: DJ DiDonna's research data on corporate sabbatical adoption trends.

newsletters.title: Winning at early-stage hiring
newsletters.subtitle: Hiring advice from founders who are crushing it

claims.text: The most important factor in early-stage hiring success is the effectiveness of the company's mission or vision in compelling top candidates.
claims.evidence: Founders from Snackpass, Runway, Vori, and others all independently cite captivating vision as the primary reason they attract great talent.

claims.text: Hiring compounds: great early hires create a snowball effect where the quality of the existing team organically attracts more exceptional people.
claims.evidence: Multiple founders confirm the compounding effect, including Siqi Chen at Runway noting that raising the bar becomes easier over time.

newsletters.title: How to know if you're doing a good job as a product manager
newsletters.subtitle: The three things you need to get right

claims.text: An IC product manager's performance can be evaluated with three questions in priority order: is the team delivering business impact, are you personally contributing, and do stakeholders regard you highly.
claims.evidence: Lenny's synthesis from his 16-year career, applied as a practical day-to-day self-evaluation tool.

newsletters.title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
newsletters.subtitle: Growth lessons from over a dozen of today's fastest-growing labor marketplaces

claims.text: The most popular channel for early supply growth in labor marketplaces is posting generic roles to existing job boards like Indeed, ZipRecruiter, and Craigslist.
claims.evidence: Research across a dozen fast-growing labor marketplaces including Clipboard Health, Thumbtack, and Staffy confirms job boards as the dominant early supply channel.

newsletters.title: Breaking into growth
newsletters.subtitle: ## ✨ Announcement: Applications for my PM course now open ✨

claims.text: The approach to transitioning into a growth PM role is simple: ask your manager for it, learn growth fundamentals through structured study, and then do actual growth work to demonstrate capability.
claims.evidence: Lenny's own transition from generalist PM to growth role at Airbnb by telling his manager, studying, and demonstrating results over six months.

newsletters.title: Why marketplaces fail
newsletters.subtitle: The top five marketplaces failure modes — lessons from 30+ marketplaces

claims.text: Marketplaces are roughly 2x as hard as non-marketplace businesses because you must find product-market fit for both supply and demand.
claims.evidence: Author's direct assertion based on experience evaluating 30+ marketplaces

claims.text: The five most common reasons marketplaces fail are lack of demand-side PMF, lack of supply-side PMF, lack of liquidity, bad unit economics, and scaling too fast.
claims.evidence: Categorization from analysis of 30+ failed marketplace examples including Homejoy, Shyp, Fab, and others

claims.text: Marketplaces beat non-marketplace alternatives on demand by delivering a much cheaper product, a much better product via exclusive supply, or a much better experience via aggregating disaggregated supply.
claims.evidence: Three demand-side advantages mapped to examples: DoorDash (cheaper), Airbnb (better product), GOAT/Uber (better experience)

claims.text: There is generally one fatal issue that kills a marketplace rather than a combination of equal factors.
claims.evidence: Pattern observed across dozens of failed marketplace case studies

newsletters.title: Choosing a take rate
newsletters.subtitle: Issue 72

claims.text: Take rate is determined by the formula: Convenience + Demand - Competition, where platforms generally charge 5-15% and marketplaces charge 10-50%.
claims.evidence: Analysis of 35+ company take rates showing clear distinction between platforms and marketplaces

claims.text: In winner-take-all markets, charging a lower take rate often beats a higher one because higher fees create friction and opportunity for competitors.
claims.evidence: Bill Gurley's argument in 'A Rake Too Far' plus Jeff Bezos quote 'your margin is my opportunity'

claims.text: OnlyFans can charge a 20% take rate despite not driving demand because it solves a major pain point (accepting payment for sex work) with very low competition.
claims.evidence: Anomaly analysis from take rate dataset showing it charges marketplace rates as a platform

newsletters.title: The Minto Pyramid Principle and the SCR Framework
newsletters.subtitle: Issue 71

claims.text: In business communication you should start with the answer first and then provide supporting arguments, the opposite of how you naturally arrive at conclusions.
claims.evidence: Barbara Minto's Pyramid Principle developed at McKinsey: 'you think from the bottom up, but you present from the top-down'

claims.text: The SCR (Situation-Complication-Resolution) framework combined with the Minto Pyramid is an effective structure for any business proposal, email, strategy doc, or presentation.
claims.evidence: Applied examples showing before/after of a bug-fix proposal, plus templates for product 1-pagers and strategy docs

claims.text: In a business context you should spend minimal time on Situation and Complication to get to the Resolution as quickly as possible, since colleagues want to be informed not entertained.
claims.evidence: Contrast with storytelling in movies where tension builds; business audiences are busy and want efficiency

newsletters.title: The PM 🤝 Design Partnership
newsletters.subtitle: Five tips for PMs working with designers — guest post by Katie Dill

claims.text: PMs should trust the designer's expertise on user experience decisions, defer to them in their area of expertise, and protect uninterrupted maker time for creative work.
claims.evidence: Katie Dill's experience leading design at Airbnb, Lyft, and Stripe; Paul Graham's maker vs manager schedule concept

claims.text: Including designers early in the product development process saves time and money because they inform with user voice, catalyze with creative ideas, and gain context for better execution.
claims.evidence: Katie Dill's experience seeing PMs change their tune after including designers in planning

claims.text: A north star vision is essential for product teams to avoid building a disjointed Frankenstein product, and the investment can range from a single-screen whiteboard session to a months-long high-fidelity prototype.
claims.evidence: Lyft's 6-month north star for reimagining the rider app covering multiple transportation modes

claims.text: So-called 'design goals' like consistency, usability, and accessibility are actually business goals because they directly drive conversion, loyalty, and efficiency.
claims.evidence: Katie Dill argues these are sub-goals of business metrics; users notice quality gaps and infer broader failures

newsletters.title: Defending your big bets
newsletters.subtitle: Issue 69

claims.text: To protect long-term big-bet projects at large companies, spend 80% of team time on short-term incremental wins and 20% on high-risk long-term bets to create 'cover fire' of consistent results.
claims.evidence: Author's repeated personal experience at Airbnb; supported by advice from PMs at Twitter, Snap, GitHub, Shopify, and Gusto

claims.text: Consistently shipping small wins builds trust, earns the right to take bigger bets, and prevents your team from becoming a target when new fires arise and leaders look for non-essential projects to cut.
claims.evidence: Author's experience plus advice from Sriram Krishnan (keep it small until ready) and Tomer London (small hungry entrepreneurial team)

newsletters.title: Content-driven growth
newsletters.subtitle: Advice from HubSpot, Ahrefs, Intercom, Slidebean, and Webflow

claims.text: There are five distinct content-driven growth strategies mapped on a 2x2 of SEO vs virality and user-generated vs editorially-generated content: UGSO, EGSO, DGSO, UGVO, and EGVO.
claims.evidence: Author's taxonomy with examples: Quora/Reddit (UGSO), HubSpot/Ahrefs (EGSO), Thumbtack/Zapier (DGSO), TikTok (UGVO), Spotify Wrapped (EGVO)

claims.text: Content marketing does not generate returns as quickly as other forms of marketing, but once you find a format that works you should double down on it rather than trying to do everything at once.
claims.evidence: Intercom's John Collins: 'content is a supertanker, not a speedboat'; Slidebean took 18 months of trial and error on YouTube

claims.text: SEO content is much easier to pursue than viral content because keyword research provides a straightforward source of topic ideas, while viral content depends on creativity.
claims.evidence: Tim Soulo of Ahrefs contrasting the two approaches based on years of content production

claims.text: In HubSpot's early days, over 30% of customers came from demand generated by blog posts, and they think of competitors as other media outlets rather than other SaaS brands.
claims.evidence: HubSpot's head of content Kieran Flanagan sharing internal data

newsletters.title: Becoming a senior Product Manager
newsletters.subtitle: by Jackie Bavaro

claims.text: The three most important differentiators of senior PMs versus junior PMs are strategy, autonomy, and nuance rather than better day-to-day execution.
claims.evidence: Jackie Bavaro's research for Cracking the PM Career; most of the senior PM's strategic work is invisible to teammates

claims.text: A product strategy has three components -- vision, strategic framework, and roadmap -- and newer PMs prioritize by incremental value while senior PMs prioritize by progress toward long-term vision.
claims.evidence: Jackie Bavaro's framework with toy-game examples illustrating each component

claims.text: The median time to progress from entry-level PM to senior PM is about 3 years, making it the longest single milestone in the PM career path.
claims.evidence: Lenny's estimate based on experience: 3 years to Senior, 1-2 to GPM, 2-3 to Director

newsletters.title: How to increase virality
newsletters.subtitle: > # Q: Is it possible to increase the virality of my product?

claims.text: Most companies find the vast majority of growth from just one of four engines -- virality, performance marketing, content, or sales -- and optimizing a secondary engine before scaling the core one is rarely time well spent.
claims.evidence: Author's research for First Round and Reforge on growth engines

claims.text: There are seven distinct types of virality across three categories: word-of-mouth (offline and online), invitation (social, incentivized, utility), and experiential (passive and active).
claims.evidence: Taxonomy built from dozens of examples including Airbnb, Clubhouse, Dropbox, TikTok, Calendly, and Citizen

claims.text: Virality is a natural fit for products that are better with friends, innately fun to share, or remarkable enough to talk about, while performance marketing fits revenue-generating products and content fits those generating public UGC.
claims.evidence: Heuristic mapping product characteristics to appropriate growth engines

newsletters.title: Positioning
newsletters.subtitle: By April Dunford

claims.text: Positioning defines how your product is a leader at delivering something that a well-defined set of customers cares a lot about, and a shift in positioning can mean the difference between success and failure.
claims.evidence: April Dunford's story of repositioning a failed 'Microsoft Access killer' as an 'embeddable database for mobile devices' which spawned hundreds of millions in revenue

claims.text: The five components of positioning must be evaluated in a specific order: competitive alternatives, then differentiated capabilities, then value, then target customers, then market category.
claims.evidence: April Dunford's methodology refined across seven startups; starting with competitive alternatives prevents positioning that sounds good internally but fails with customers

claims.text: Companies that create new market categories often lose in the long run to companies that gain a foothold after the hard category-creation work is done, which is why 90% of recent tech IPOs positioned in existing markets.
claims.evidence: Google vs Ask Jeeves, Facebook vs Myspace; Salesforce and Gainsight positioned in existing markets until having hundreds of millions in revenue

newsletters.title: The most important consumer subscription metrics to track
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I read your post about the most important bottom-up SaaS metrics to track, and I was wondering – do you have the same kind of advice

claims.text: Consumer subscription businesses must nail six things in sequence: acquire users sustainably, get them to experience value quickly, keep them finding value, convert to paid, retain paid users, and deliver profitably.
claims.evidence: Framework applied across three sub-types (content, software, physical goods) with specific metric recommendations for each

claims.text: For consumer software subscriptions, the four most important metrics are activation rate, L7/L30 engagement intensity, free-to-paid conversion, and cohort retention at 1 month and 1 year.
claims.evidence: Synthesis from growth experts and benchmarks including Mixpanel data

newsletters.title: Managing up
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I’m finding it tough to work with my manager

claims.text: The most effective tactic for managing up is sending a weekly 'State of The Me' email to your boss covering current priorities, things on your mind, and blockers.
claims.evidence: Author's personal experience; template provided with format for priorities, thoughts, and blockers

claims.text: When asked to take on new work, you should reprioritize against existing work and share updated priorities with your manager rather than just absorbing the new task.
claims.evidence: Author's experience as PM and manager; highlights that managers rarely know your full plate

newsletters.title: Pricing your SaaS product
newsletters.subtitle: By Patrick Campbell, CEO of ProfitWell

claims.text: If you get everything else wrong in pricing but get your value metric right you will do okay, because a value metric bakes in lower churn and higher expansion revenue.
claims.evidence: Patrick Campbell of ProfitWell; value metric pricing captures more area under the demand curve than flat monthly fees

claims.text: The most successful companies optimize monetization every quarter, but this goes far beyond changing the actual price to include target segments, packaging, and positioning.
claims.evidence: Patrick Campbell's experience at ProfitWell working with thousands of SaaS companies

newsletters.title: What it feels like when you've found product-market fit
newsletters.subtitle: Stories from the founders of Netflix, Uber, Airbnb, Substack, Stripe, Datadog, GitHub, Segment, Dropbox, Superhuman, Instacart, Nextdoor, and many more

claims.text: About half of iconic companies found product-market fit immediately after launch while the other half spent months or years iterating, including Netflix (18 months), Airbnb (2 years), and Superhuman (3 years).
claims.evidence: First-hand accounts from 25 founders including Netflix, Uber, Airbnb, Stripe, Dropbox, and Segment

claims.text: The intensity of PMF pull is a factor of both fit (how good your product solves the problem) and initial market size -- broad markets create sudden pull while niche markets create steady compounding pull.
claims.evidence: Dropbox/Netflix/Tinder had sudden broad pull vs Instacart/Superhuman/Substack had steady niche pull

claims.text: True product-market fit requires three things: making a product people want, making a profit delivering it at scale, and finding and keeping those people sustainably.
claims.evidence: Cherry, Luxe, and Exec cited as cautionary tales that had demand but failed on economics

newsletters.title: Getting better at product strategy
newsletters.subtitle: Each week I tackle reader questions about product, growth, working with humans, and anything else that’s stressing you out at the office

claims.text: Product planning follows the sequence Mission, Vision, Strategy, Goals, Roadmap, where your strategy is your plan to win that creates alignment, clarity, and focus.
claims.evidence: Author's experience at Airbnb with Instant Book strategy; Tesla example with mission/vision/strategy breakdown

claims.text: At Airbnb, transitioning from a request-to-book to instant-book model grew instant bookings from 5% to over 80% in two and a half years by setting an ambitious 100% north-star goal.
claims.evidence: Author's direct experience leading the Instant Book team at Airbnb

newsletters.title: How the biggest consumer apps got their first 1,000 users
newsletters.subtitle: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business 3

claims.text: Just seven strategies account for every major consumer app's early growth, and most found success from just a single strategy.
claims.evidence: Research across dozens of apps including Tinder, Uber, DoorDash, Netflix, and Product Hunt through founder interviews

claims.text: The most popular early growth strategies for consumer apps involve going directly to users -- offline, online, and through friends -- embodying the principle of doing things that don't scale.
claims.evidence: Tinder went to USC sororities, DoorDash put flyers on Stanford campus, Lyft did ice cream dropoffs at startup offices

newsletters.title: What is good retention?
newsletters.subtitle: Hello, and welcome to the free monthly editionof my weekly newsletter

claims.text: Good and great 6-month user retention benchmarks vary dramatically by business type: consumer social (25%/45%), consumer transactional (30%/50%), consumer SaaS (40%/70%), SMB SaaS (60%/80%), enterprise SaaS (70%/90%).
claims.evidence: Research with 20 top growth practitioners including experts from Pinterest, Uber, Slack, Dropbox, and Grammarly

claims.text: Retention is the best indicator of product-market fit, the most important factor in lifetime value, and drives all the best acquisition strategies.
claims.evidence: Casey Winters (former head of growth at Pinterest and Grubhub, CPO at Eventbrite)

newsletters.title: How today's fastest growing B2B businesses found their first ten customers
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from Figma, Stripe, Airtable, Shopify, Carta, Gusto and many many more

claims.text: Only three strategies account for every B2B company's earliest growth: tapping your personal network, seeking customers where they are, and getting press -- and almost every company uses the first two simultaneously.
claims.evidence: Stories from Slack, Stripe, Figma, Shopify, Airtable, Carta, Gusto and 15+ other companies

claims.text: Having a strong personal network is an even bigger advantage in B2B than B2C because early customers need to trust you, which is why connector investors or incubators like YC are so valuable.
claims.evidence: Pattern across 20+ B2B companies; personal network especially important for non-bottom-up sales models

newsletters.title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea
newsletters.subtitle: The top seven things to look for when evaluating a marketplace business idea – or any business idea

claims.text: Most marketplaces fail not because of marketplace-specific issues but because of fundamental business problems like not building something people want, too small a market, or inability to acquire users.
claims.evidence: Pattern from evaluating 100+ marketplace businesses

claims.text: To evaluate a marketplace idea, first assess seven business fundamentals (PMF, market, timing, distribution, team, moat, business model) and then seven marketplace-specific factors (demand PMF, supply PMF, scalability with quality, frequency/AOV, platform stickiness, repeat need, fragmentation).
claims.evidence: Author's framework developed from evaluating 100+ marketplace businesses

newsletters.title: Magical growth loops
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: Can you share examples of companies that found innovative growth loops, like marketplaces convincing their supply to drive their

claims.text: Magical growth loops where existing users recruit new users come in four types: supply driving demand, demand driving supply, demand driving demand (virality), and supply driving supply.
claims.evidence: 30+ examples including DoorDash (restaurants tell customers), Cameo (celebrities bring fans), Faire (vendors bring retailers)

claims.text: DoorDash, Cameo, and Faire found that recruiting supply effectively drives demand for free because supply has a natural motivation to bring customers to the platform.
claims.evidence: Specific growth loop diagrams: restaurants tell customers about DoorDash, celebrities share Cameo profiles, vendors encourage retailers to use Faire

newsletters.title: Flywheels, flywheels, flywheels
newsletters.subtitle: Each week I tackle reader questions about product, growth, working with humans, and anything else that’s stressing you out at the office

claims.text: A business flywheel identifies which elements of your business feed off each other to accelerate growth, and you create one by listing core assets, actions, user needs, outputs, and optimizations then connecting them in loops.
claims.evidence: Amazon's flywheel (lower prices -> more customers -> more sellers -> lower costs -> lower prices) and Uber's supply-driven flywheel

newsletters.title: The Transition: Layering sales onto a bottom-up self-serve product
newsletters.subtitle: By Pete Kazanjy

claims.text: 100% of bottom-up B2B companies eventually layer on a sales team, and waiting too long to do so often leads to competitors firewalling you out of enterprise segments.
claims.evidence: Pete Kazanjy citing Zoom, Slack, Datadog as IPO exemplars; Dropbox as cautionary tale of waiting too long

newsletters.title: When NOT to run an experiment – Issue 54
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: Should I always run an experiment when I make a change to my product?

claims.text: You should skip running an A/B experiment when it will take too long to get actionable results, when the downside risk is low and setup effort is high, or when the change only has long-term effects.
claims.evidence: Example: detecting a 5% conversion change on a 10% baseline requires 60,000+ users per variation; most startups cannot wait that long

newsletters.title: How to run SEO experiments
newsletters.subtitle: And it makes sense — SEO is one of the few remaining “free” growth channels, and still somewhat mysterious

claims.text: SEO experiments must be bucketed at the page level rather than the user level because Google recrawls pages as different 'users' each time, and the only metric to measure is incoming organic traffic.
claims.evidence: Brian Ta's experience running SEO experiments at Airbnb, Strava, and AngelList

claims.text: Title tag experiments led to a 15-20% aggregate increase in organic traffic at Airbnb, making them the most impactful type of SEO experiment to run.
claims.evidence: Brian Ta's direct experience leading SEO at Airbnb; a single minor title tag change was the most impactful SEO experiment ever run there

newsletters.title: A comprehensive survey of Product Management
newsletters.subtitle: 👋 Hello, I’m Lenny and welcome to a ✨ once-a-month-free-edition✨ of my newsletter

claims.text: Communication, execution, and product sense are the three most frequently valued PM skills, while design/UX, empathy, and raw intelligence are least frequently valued.
claims.evidence: Survey of nearly 1,000 respondents from 600+ companies

claims.text: PMs believe they have more influence than their teammates think they do -- 80% according to PM respondents vs 70% from non-PM respondents.
claims.evidence: Survey comparing PM self-assessment to engineer/designer/CEO assessments across hundreds of companies

newsletters.title: Increasing team velocity
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I’m constantly feeling like my team is moving too slowly

claims.text: The single most effective way to increase team velocity is to narrow the team's focus, as fewer concurrent priorities dramatically improves outcomes on the remaining work.
claims.evidence: Author's experience as founder, PM, and engineer; validated by polling founders and Twitter community

newsletters.title: Should I become a product manager
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I'm considering venturing into product management after being a software engineer for 3 years

claims.text: A PM's work breaks down into three categories: shaping the product (customer research, strategy, specs), shipping the product (timelines, tradeoffs, QA), and synchronizing the people (alignment, buy-in, communication).
claims.evidence: Author's personal experience transitioning from engineering to PM at Airbnb

claims.text: Engineers should pursue product management if they are more excited about business and customer challenges than technical ones, often have strong UX opinions teammates agree with, and could see themselves never coding professionally again.
claims.evidence: Author's personal experience switching from 10 years of engineering to PM

newsletters.title: Li Jin launches Atelier Ventures, her debut fund to invest in the passion economy
newsletters.subtitle: A special edition of Lenny's Newsletter

claims.text: The passion economy is not limited to social media influencers but encompasses an entirely new economic movement empowering individuals over institutions across education, media, money, and every industry.
claims.evidence: Li Jin's thesis with examples: CopyAI for marketers, Dumpling for grocery delivery solopreneurs, plus Substack proving the model

claims.text: What kids do for fun today will become the new creator professions of the future, and some form of Universal Creative Income will become prevalent across social platforms as the creator arms race heats up.
claims.evidence: Li Jin's predictions based on her childhood blogging, painting, and fanfiction now being monetizable; platforms already competing on creator comp packages

newsletters.title: The most important bottom-up SaaS metrics to track (and how to best visualize them)
newsletters.subtitle: Metrics to focus on, what tools to use, and how to best visualize and share these metrics

claims.text: For pre-revenue bottom-up SaaS startups, the three most important metric categories in priority order are retention (user and logo), virality within an organization (invite rate and conversion), and top-of-funnel growth (traffic and activation).
claims.evidence: Crowdsourced from experienced SaaS founders and primary research

newsletters.title: Startup PM vs. big company PM
newsletters.subtitle: > ## Q: I’m transitioning from being the first (and only) Product Manager at a small company to being a PM at a big company

claims.text: The three most important PM skills to develop when transitioning from startup to big company are communication, influence without authority, and data-driven decision making.
claims.evidence: Author's experience at Airbnb growing from a few PMs to nearly 200; specific reading recommendations for each skill

newsletters.title: Generating buzz
newsletters.subtitle: Ten strategies and 49 examples of successful buzz making

claims.text: The key to generating buzz at launch is doing something remarkable -- literally worth remarking about -- because no one cares about your new product and everyone's time is already allocated.
claims.evidence: Ten strategies with 49 examples including Dollar Shave Club (remarkable video), Robinhood ($0 commissions), Gmail (1GB free), Snapchat (vending machine Spectacles)

newsletters.title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
newsletters.subtitle: What tips do you have for managing your time and workload?

claims.text: Blocking off dedicated deep work time on your calendar and defending it aggressively is one of the most effective ways for PMs to increase productivity.
claims.evidence: Lenny recommends scheduling two-hour deep work blocks on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, citing Cal Newport's Deep Work concept that distraction-free concentration creates new value and is hard to replicate.

claims.text: When asked to take on more work, the best response is to prioritize the new ask among existing work and communicate that prioritization back to your manager rather than saying yes or no.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes a 'Prioritize and Communicate' technique taught by his last manager: slot the new request among existing priorities and share the updated list, letting the manager adjust if they disagree.

claims.text: Having a trusted TODO system with concrete actions and a list of people you are waiting on eliminates the time wasted re-figuring out what to work on.
claims.evidence: Lenny credits the Getting Things Done system for clearing mental distractions and making it easy to choose what to work on next, using a simple physical notebook rather than an app.

newsletters.title: Strategy and tactics for increasing conversion
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions and in return, I’ll humbly offer actionable real-talk advice

claims.text: Increasing conversion is one of the highest ROI investments a growth team can make because the wins make existing channels more efficient and last indefinitely.
claims.evidence: Lenny states this is particularly true once top-of-funnel engines like paid, SEO, and referrals are running, based on his experience at Airbnb.

claims.text: Funnel optimization has three core levers: maintaining user focus, maintaining user motivation, and reducing user friction.
claims.evidence: Lenny presents this as his framework for approaching funnel optimization, with specific Airbnb examples for each lever including opening listings in new tabs (focus), scarcity messaging (motivation), and Instant Book (friction).

claims.text: At Airbnb, opening listings in a new tab so users wouldn't lose their search results turned out to be one of the single biggest conversion wins.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes this as a surprisingly impactful change that helped users avoid losing track of options, keeping them exploring if the first listing didn't work out.

claims.text: The three core re-engagement strategies for bounced users are reminders of unfinished actions, notifications that something has changed, and providing additional information that addresses why they stopped.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes these as the primary content strategies for re-engaging users, with Airbnb examples including reminding hosts of waiting guests and notifying visitors of price changes.

newsletters.title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions (just reply to this email or DM me) and in return, I’ll humbly offer some real-talk advice

claims.text: During a crisis, companies should first determine which of three buckets they fall into: seeing growth, falling apart, or chugging along, and adopt a corresponding strategy of acceleration, survival, or pivoting.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna's framework shared by Lenny, supported by Kevin Barry's data showing roughly 20% of B2B SaaS clients were in growth markets, 20% in hard-hit markets, and 60% doing OK.

claims.text: Before knowing which recovery scenario you're in, the priority should be preserving optionality by cutting spend to extend runway while keeping as much of the team in place as possible.
claims.evidence: Dan Hockenmaier's advice to build a worst-case scenario (6 months of zero revenue, 18 months without profitable acquisition) while also understanding the true upside case.

claims.text: Every company should create a small incident response team with a single DRI, full authority to change anything, blown-up OKRs, and daily standups to move fast during a crisis.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna's operational recommendation, echoed by HBR's advice to assemble a small trusted team with enough leeway for rapid tactical decisions and beware of bureaucracy.

newsletters.title: Leading a PM team meeting - Issue 19
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions (just reply to this email or DM me) and in return, I’ll humbly share free real-talk advice

claims.text: PM team meetings should occur one to two times a month; weekly meetings become redundant with 1:1s, while less than monthly loses alignment benefits.
claims.evidence: Lenny advises that bigger teams should meet less frequently, and suggests the three typical goals are understanding, alignment, and connection.

claims.text: Before creating a PM team meeting, you should explicitly write out what problem you are trying to solve and whether there are more efficient ways to address it.
claims.evidence: Lenny warns against doing meetings just to do them and says to regularly check in on whether you're achieving the stated goals.

newsletters.title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
newsletters.subtitle: Hello and welcome to another edition of my weekly newsletter

claims.text: When joining a turnaround situation, set clear expectations with your manager that the next 6-12 months will likely involve less business impact and some team member dissatisfaction.
claims.evidence: Lenny emphasizes this is critical so that when you succeed it's all the more impressive, and to protect yourself during the transition period.

claims.text: The Observe-Identify-Share-Act framework, adapted from Nonviolent Communication, is an effective approach for making necessary changes on an underperforming team.
claims.evidence: Lenny recommends collecting facts first, identifying root causes vs symptoms, communicating changes carefully to peers and higher-ups before the team, and acting only after alignment.

claims.text: To motivate an underperforming team to embrace change, connect the proposed changes to each key member's personal motivations whether that is driving impact, getting promoted, or achieving work-life balance.
claims.evidence: Lenny provides three specific examples of connecting issues to individual motivations: showing how issues slow shipping, hold back careers, or cause unnecessary work.

newsletters.title: Building a referrals program
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions (just reply to this email or DM me) and in return I’ll humbly share actionable free real-talk advice

claims.text: Referral programs work best when strong word-of-mouth already exists; the added incentive simply adds fuel to an existing fire.
claims.evidence: Lenny states he has not seen referrals work when there wasn't strong existing WOM, based on Airbnb experience where host referrals became the single biggest driver of attributable supply growth.

claims.text: Referral programs are most effective when customers are consumers or small business owners who know many other potential users, and the product requires a lot of trust to use.
claims.evidence: Lenny notes Airbnb hosts know other folks with homes and Dropbox users have friends who need cloud storage, while enterprise heads of HR may not know many peers. Trust-dependent products benefit most from friend recommendations.

claims.text: At Airbnb, testing a doubled referral bonus in some markets occasionally showed no significant improvement, indicating incentives plateau at a point where more money won't motivate additional referrals.
claims.evidence: Lenny shares this as a direct learning from Airbnb's host referral program optimization.

newsletters.title: This Week #15: When should a SaaS startup raise money 💰
newsletters.subtitle: Hello and welcome to another edition of my weekly newsletter 👋

claims.text: Hiring before product-market fit slows you down, while hiring after product-market fit speeds you up, so startups should raise meaningful money only after finding PMF.
claims.evidence: Sam Altman quote cited by Lenny, supported by Andreessen Horowitz's observation that premature scaling is one of the most common ways startups die.

claims.text: A profitable company should raise capital only when confident it can use that capital to attain a higher growth rate worth the tradeoff in dilution.
claims.evidence: Tomasz Tunguz quote cited by Lenny, framing the fundraising decision around whether higher growth rates increase business value enough to justify dilution.

newsletters.title: This Week #14: Transitioning from startup founder to product manager
newsletters.subtitle: Hello and welcome to another edition of my weekly newsletter 👋

claims.text: As a founder, your job is to win at all costs and chaos gets swept under the rug, but as a PM your job is to avoid chaos, avoid changing course, and keep the team running smoothly.
claims.evidence: Lenny draws from his personal experience transitioning from CEO of Localmind to PM at Airbnb, noting it took him a long time to adjust to listening to his boss and focusing on soft skills.

claims.text: Companies acquire startups because they want to move faster, so acquired founders should maintain their entrepreneurial spirit while also learning to collaborate within the larger organization.
claims.evidence: Lenny advises balancing two tensions: being the change you want to see while also respecting the larger company's processes and your boss's decisions.

newsletters.title: This Week #13: Balancing outcome-thinking with design and technical requirements ⚖️
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions (just reply to this email or DM me) and in return I’ll give you free actionable real-talk advice 🤝

claims.text: At Airbnb there was a very strong correlation between teams that drove significant impact and teams that were organized around outcomes rather than product surface areas.
claims.evidence: Lenny cites examples like having a team oriented around getting hosts more bookings rather than owning host products, and a team focused on trip quality rather than owning the reviews product.

claims.text: To balance outcome-thinking with engineering and design needs, extend your outcome thinking further into the future by evaluating how much the technical work would benefit your outcome 1+ years out.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes how Airbnb decided on a monolith-to-SOA migration by laying out scenarios of what would happen with different levels of investment, looking at timeline, resources, short-term and long-term goals, and risks.

newsletters.title: This Week #12: Expanding your business internationally 🌏
newsletters.subtitle: Send me your questions and in return I’ll give you actionable real-talk advice 🤝

claims.text: When expanding internationally, start by making two sorted lists of potential markets: one ranked by short-term signals like existing demand and localization ease, and one by long-term TAM and execution ease, then merge them.
claims.evidence: Christopher Cederskog and Georg Bauser's framework from their experience building Airbnb's European business, noting UK, Germany, France, China, Canada, and Australia typically top such lists.

claims.text: A divide-and-conquer cycle for international expansion works by constraining to a geo, achieving liquidity (e.g. $10k GMV over a rolling 30-day period), then splitting or expanding to adjacent markets.
claims.evidence: Cederskog and Bauser recommend starting with a city like London, achieving liquidity, then splitting into subregions or expanding to Birmingham, Manchester, Berlin, Paris, and Amsterdam.

newsletters.title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from Marc Andreessen, Elad Gil, Steve Blank, Andy Rachleff, Michael Seibel, and many more

claims.text: If you plot the percentage of active users over time for various cohorts and the retention curve flattens off at some point, you have probably found product-market fit.
claims.evidence: Brian Balfour and Casey Winters both describe this as the key quantitative signal, with Winters adding that the ability to acquire users at less than the money earned from that curve represents true PMF.

claims.text: If you survey users asking how they would feel if they could no longer use the product and over 40% say 'very disappointed,' you have product-market fit.
claims.evidence: Sean Ellis / Rahul Vohra's metric, cited alongside Doug Leone's advice that if enterprise customers don't scream when you pull a free trial, you don't have PMF.

claims.text: If your product is broken and people are still using it, that is a clear sign of product-market fit.
claims.evidence: Elad Gil cites Twitter's fail whale era where no one left despite constant downtime, and Don Valentine's formulation that PMF means customers want it so badly you can screw everything up and still succeed.

claims.text: Product-market fit is not a one-time discrete event; competitors arrive, markets evolve, and what felt like PMF can fade as patterns change.
claims.evidence: Ben Horowitz quote emphasizing PMF is ongoing, supported by Lenny's examples of Yo app and CryptoKitties as fool's gold that looked like PMF but faded.

newsletters.title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
newsletters.subtitle: With guest contributor Jackie Bavaro - author of Cracking the PM Interview

claims.text: Early in your PM career, the four things to focus on are shipping wins, learning best practices, getting baseline credentials, and starting your network.
claims.evidence: Jackie Bavaro's advice that shipping wins drive your career forward, best practices are learned at strong PM orgs, credentials help pass resume screening, and early networking pays off when peers become senior.

claims.text: Wherever the majority of your growth comes from determines who is driving the ship: product-led companies like Dropbox, engineering-led like Stripe, sales-led like Salesforce, or marketing-led like DTC brands.
claims.evidence: Lenny's rule of thumb for identifying company culture, with the recommendation that PMs will have the best experience at product-led organizations.

newsletters.title: This Week #10: Keeping designers and engineers excited about metrics + Transitioning from DS to PM 🕺
newsletters.subtitle: With guest contributor Lex Roman - Growth Designer, Writer, Speaker

claims.text: Designers and engineers are most engaged with metrics when they are involved in goal setting, metric definition, regular metric reviews, and post-launch reflections.
claims.evidence: Lex Roman's four-part framework: define metrics together, review metrics together, prioritize together, and reflect on releases together. She argues goal-setting sessions are the most valuable time to spend together.

claims.text: An internal transfer program is by far the best way to break into product management because it is the easiest, least risky, and provides the most support.
claims.evidence: Lenny's assessment based on the four most common paths into PM, noting it is very normal to move down one level when making the transition and that a DS background is both a superpower and liability.

newsletters.title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
newsletters.subtitle: With special guest contributor Brian Balfour – Founder and CEO of Reforge
