claims.text: When seeking a growth role, focus on demonstrating foundational tools like data analysis, user psychology, and experiment-driven methodology rather than listing tactical ideas.
claims.evidence: Brian Balfour advises that listing tactical ideas will fail because candidates are at a massive information disadvantage about what has been tried; great teams want to see strong foundations applied to problems.

claims.text: When pursuing an initiative in an ambiguous area of ownership, communicate your intent and proceed rather than blocking on approval, creating a culture of impact over ownership.
claims.evidence: Lenny recommends communicating to leadership and associated teams with a specific date to disagree by, then proceeding if no pushback, while keeping interested parties updated on progress.

claims.text: Organizing teams around outcomes rather than features was a critical ingredient in Airbnb's success because it breaks the idea of clean divisions of product surface-area ownership and prevents over-optimizing features that aren't driving impact.
claims.evidence: Lenny cites this as one of the best decisions the Airbnb founders made early on, referencing Jonathan Golden's First Round Review post for details.

newsletters.title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: TaskRabbit scaled before achieving product-market fit by focusing on top-line growth instead of fill rate, and people who didn't get matched never came back.
claims.evidence: Brian Rothenberg's admission that TaskRabbit should have focused on fill rate (% of tasks completed) which was well under 100%, before scaling. They eventually addressed it by vetting supply and raising fill rate to 86%.

claims.text: At Etsy, nearly the entire product and engineering team's activity had nothing to do with actual growth for years, with hundreds of people working on pages selling 700 items per day while only three worked on search which sold 100,000 items per day.
claims.evidence: Dan McKinley's account of Etsy's resource misallocation, describing it as a failure that increased in scale through multiple CEO changes, each a missed opportunity to acknowledge the disconnect.

claims.text: For marketplace companies, if you have conviction in an adjacent opportunity with clear PMF, expand into it as soon as the organization can support it rather than waiting too long.
claims.evidence: David Rosenthal's regret that Rover waited too long to expand into dog walking, allowing Wag to capture the exact same demand base with better economics.

newsletters.title: Maintaining Quality 🏅 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: The search ranking algorithm is one of the cheapest yet most powerful levers for maintaining marketplace quality because it promotes the best supply without requiring direct intervention.
claims.evidence: Rover's David Rosenthal credits search ranking as the lever that controlled everything in the business and the reason Rover pulled ahead of competition, while Airbnb deliberately avoided sort-by-price for years because it lowered quality.

claims.text: Rover found that asking sitters 'Where does the dog sleep at night?' was the single most predictive signal of being good supply; those who answered 'In my bed' were boosted in search rankings.
claims.evidence: David Rosenthal describes how Rover identified early predictive signals of quality and used them to seed supply appropriately in search results.

claims.text: Almost all marketplace quality issues are supply-side, and you never fully solve quality; it is an ongoing battle with unforeseen bumps and shifting expectations that only gets harder as you scale.
claims.evidence: Lenny's meta-learning from interviews across 17 marketplace companies, noting nearly every company ended up with full-time quality teams with ever-increasing sophistication.

newsletters.title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: At scale, the number of effective growth levers narrows from sixteen in the early days to only eight: performance marketing, geographic expansion, conversion optimization, SEO, direct sales, referrals, loops, and PR.
claims.evidence: Lenny's research across 17 marketplace companies, with performance marketing being key for 70%, geographic expansion for 65%, and conversion optimization and SEO each for 50%.

claims.text: Uber's mature growth mix was approximately 50% paid, 15% referrals, and 35% word-of-mouth, compared to early days of 30% referrals, 50-60% WOM, and the rest PR.
claims.evidence: Andrew Chen's direct account of how Uber's acquisition mix evolved over time from organic-dominant to paid-dominant.

newsletters.title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: About 40% of marketplace companies start supply-constrained and remain supply-constrained throughout their entire history, while very few are always demand-constrained.
claims.evidence: Lenny's research across 17 marketplaces found Uber, Lyft, OpenTable, and DoorDash were always supply-constrained, while only Rover, TaskRabbit, and AngelList were always demand-constrained.

claims.text: Lyft found that a 3-minute ETA was the inflection point below which the ride felt instantaneous; above 3 minutes, users were incentivized to shop around or take alternatives.
claims.evidence: Benjamin Lauzier describes finding this through data analysis of app-open-to-ride conversion and long-term retention, which gave local teams a north star metric.

newsletters.title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
newsletters.subtitle: Rare insights from today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: Word-of-mouth was the most important early demand growth channel for over half of the biggest marketplace companies, with Uber seeing 50-60% WOM and Airbnb over 50% on the guest side.
claims.evidence: Direct quotes from leaders at Lyft, Airbnb, OpenTable, Uber, AngelList, TaskRabbit, and Instacart all identifying WOM as a huge early growth driver.

claims.text: Supply directly driving its own demand was the second most common early demand growth lever for marketplaces, effective for over 40% of companies studied.
claims.evidence: DoorDash restaurants printed placards without being asked, Etsy sellers did grassroots marketing, Eventbrite organizers brought built-in audiences, and Patreon creators marketed their pages to followers.

claims.text: SEO was a vital early demand growth driver for over 40% of marketplaces, with Thumbtack getting 80-90% of growth from SEO and GrubHub getting 30% of new users from it.
claims.evidence: Sander Daniels describes Thumbtack's SEO journey starting with a chance bar meeting with an SEO expert, while Casey Winters describes GrubHub creating landing pages for each restaurant.

newsletters.title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
newsletters.subtitle: Rare insights from 17 of today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: One-on-one direct sales was a crucial supply growth lever for about 60% of early marketplace companies, twice as common as any other lever.
claims.evidence: Lenny's research across 17 companies found direct sales was the top supply lever, with examples from Airbnb, OpenTable, Etsy, Caviar, Uber, DoorDash, and AngelList all using door-to-door or direct outreach.

claims.text: The median number of growth levers that the biggest marketplace companies relied on to kickstart supply was just two, suggesting teams should focus rather than spread across many channels.
claims.evidence: Lenny's meta-analysis across 17 marketplaces found the average was 2.5 levers, with the lesson being to find what works and double down.

newsletters.title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 2: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Supply vs. Demand
newsletters.subtitle: Rare insights from 17 of today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: The vast majority (80%) of successful marketplaces focused almost all of their early resources on growing supply, because supply either drove its own demand or word-of-mouth handled demand.
claims.evidence: 14 out of 17 companies interviewed concentrated on supply, including Eventbrite, Lyft, DoorDash, Etsy, Caviar, Instacart, and GrubHub, with only Rover, TaskRabbit, and Zillow focusing on demand.

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from 17 of today's biggest marketplaces, including Airbnb, DoorDash, Thumbtack, Etsy, Uber and many more

claims.text: With the exception of one company (Thumbtack), every successful marketplace studied constrained their initial marketplace either by geography or by category to more quickly reach critical mass.
claims.evidence: Lenny's research across 17 marketplaces found location-based services always started with a geographic constraint (e.g. Uber targeting 30 drivers and sub-15-min ETA), while others used category constraints (e.g. Etsy with only three categories).

claims.text: The primary reason most of these marketplace companies became successful was great product-market fit, not amazing growth insights; the growth levers helped accelerate and control growth but were secondary.
claims.evidence: Lenny's explicit disclaimer that many interviewees were first to admit this, with DoorDash's Micah Moreau noting that whenever they made a mistake it was because they forgot first principles of selection, delivery quality, and pricing.

newsletters.title: This Week #7: Effectively communicating about a failure to execs, managing founder expectations, and hiring a Director of PM
newsletters.subtitle: ### Q: What are your thoughts on communicating failure, like missing our team’s set goals?

claims.text: When communicating a project failure to leadership, be up front about it, stay positive, separate the idea from the execution, and most importantly have a clear plan for next steps.
claims.evidence: Lenny's seven-step framework for delivering bad news including describing the journey, sharing what worked, and showing you care, emphasizing that turning failure into opportunity through clear next steps is where to spend most of your time.

claims.text: To change a founder's mind about their core idea, bring them on the same journey of evidence you went on, align on key hypotheses and test them one by one, and give the founder a chance to save face.
claims.evidence: Lenny provides seven tactics including aligning on tests, showing irrefutable data, having a trusted advisor help convince, and framing changes as evolution based on new learnings rather than failure.

newsletters.title: This Week #6: Cultivating good relationships with distributed co-workers, building trust to accelerate growth, and leveling up as a PM when you have extra time
newsletters.subtitle: With special guest contributor Hiten Shah!

claims.text: Remote work leads to increased miscommunication, and the key to good relationships with remote engineers and designers is maintaining clarity through writing things down, screensharing, and postmorteming everything shipped.
claims.evidence: Hiten Shah's advice based on years of running distributed companies, emphasizing documentation of decisions, collaborating in tools during calls, and constantly assessing processes.

claims.text: The six key strategies for building user trust are social proof, authority signals, guarantees, reputation systems, great UX, and consistent delivery on your promise.
claims.evidence: Lenny's framework citing Airbnb Host Guarantee, Yelp/Uber reviews, and the Amazon Flywheel as examples, with the advice to show trust signals early and often in the user experience.

newsletters.title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
newsletters.subtitle: What helped me most during that period was actually talking to a professional coach

claims.text: Impostor syndrome is normal and generally a sign of success; if you were in an unchallenging role, you wouldn't experience it.
claims.evidence: Executive coach Kate Hosie's perspective that impostor syndrome can also be a valid signal to look for areas where you're not taking responsibility, or a symptom of burnout from over-caring, lack of appreciation, or not working to your strengths.

claims.text: To introduce a growth team to a traditional sales-and-marketing org, bring in external growth experts, get CEO buy-in, and propose a six-month experiment with a small cross-functional team to demonstrate measurable impact.
claims.evidence: Lenny suggests inviting growth leaders from admired companies for fireside chats, benchmarking against other companies, and warning that without top-level support you'll face friction, politics, and tension.

claims.text: Having a data scientist on your cross-functional team is like having a superpower, but to get the most value you should treat them as a partner by sharing the what and why, not just queries to run.
claims.evidence: Lenny's seven tips including prioritizing asks together, knowing what you'll do with the answer before asking, sharing findings widely, and being data-informed rather than data-driven.

newsletters.title: This Week #4: Motivating engineers to hit deadlines, PM career ladders, and aligning with execs
newsletters.subtitle: I’ll tackle three reader questions each week (keeping your name and company anonymous) until you quit sending me questions

claims.text: The most effective way to motivate engineers is to connect their daily work to their personal goals such as wanting to become a manager, start a company, or get promoted.
claims.evidence: Lenny provides a 10-point framework that also includes connecting work to higher mission, partnering with their manager, ensuring buy-in on timelines, and aligning incentives to performance reviews.

claims.text: All you really need for your team to excel is smart and motivated people; if engineers consistently take too long to ship, either intelligence or motivation is the issue to diagnose.
claims.evidence: Lenny's high-level conclusion after presenting ten tactical approaches to improving team velocity.

newsletters.title: This Week #3: Finding product/market fit, interviewing for a Director of PM role, and structuring discussions with senior leaders
newsletters.subtitle: I’ll answer three questions each week (keeping your name and company anonymous) until you stop sending me questions

claims.text: When validating a startup idea pre-product, the three things to look for are visible passion in potential customers, willingness to pay now, and a clear articulation of why this problem matters to them.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes passion as people getting visibly excited and offering money, skin in the game as literally sending invoices for early access, and a clear why as repeatedly asking 'why' to get to the core pain point.

claims.text: As you progress from IC PM to Director, your gaze rises from week-to-week to years out, and the five most critical traits become long-term strategic thinking, people leadership, stakeholder management, impact orientation, and communication.
claims.evidence: Lenny's framework for what's different about a Director PM role, emphasizing that impact increasingly comes from the output of people you lead rather than your own hands-on work.

newsletters.title: The Secret to a Great Planning Process — Lessons from Airbnb and Eventbrite
newsletters.subtitle: An excerpt from a new essay about yearly and quarterly planning just published by First Round Review

claims.text: The root cause of nearly all bad planning processes is a lack of understanding of roles -- who is responsible for what, when.
claims.evidence: Lenny and Nels Gilbreth's observation from dozens of planning cycles across 10 years at Eventbrite and 7 at Airbnb, leading to their W Framework with four steps: Context, Plans, Integration, Buy-in.

newsletters.title: This Week #2: Tackling the chicken-and-egg problem, building a growth team from scratch, and addressing overlap with PM peers 🤔
newsletters.subtitle: Thank you for all of the feedback and kind words so far

claims.text: The five most common solutions to the marketplace chicken-and-egg problem are single-player mode, subsidizing one side, employees as early supply, focusing on one market, and focusing on one niche.
claims.evidence: Lenny synthesizes examples like OpenTable's reservation software (single-player), Uber/Lyft paying early drivers (subsidy), Airbnb founders hosting (employees as supply), Rover in Seattle (one market), and eBay with Beanie Babies (one niche).

newsletters.title: This Week #1: Must reads on growth, what PMs should focus on when joining a new team, and the value of an MBA
newsletters.subtitle: I was half-worried no one would take me up on the offer — instead, we now have a backlog of over two dozen intriguing questions

claims.text: A new PM joining a team should focus on building trust in the first 30 days by asking questions and finding quick wins, improving execution in days 30-60, and developing strategy and vision in days 60-90.
claims.evidence: Lenny's 30-60-90 day plan for new PMs, comparing a new PM to a new roommate where the team is skeptical and not sure they want to trust you with the keys.

claims.text: For most PMs who already have a PM job, an MBA is not worth the investment; better alternatives include working with your manager on a plan, finding PM mentors, and working on a variety of products and teams.
claims.evidence: Lenny advises that nothing beats actual experience when evaluating PM candidates, though acknowledges an MBA can help break into PM or unstick a career.

newsletters.title: The Power of Performance Reviews: Use This System to Become a Better Manager 🤝
newsletters.subtitle: You owe your reports a better performance review

claims.text: Performance reviews done well improve performance, align expectations, and accelerate careers; done poorly, they accelerate departures.
claims.evidence: Lenny's assertion based on experience with great managers at Airbnb, presenting a three-step system of Prepare, Deliver, Follow-up with peer feedback using start/continue/stop questions.

claims.text: Most performance reviews over-index on development areas, but individuals will have just as much or more impact by flexing what they are really good at, so reviews should highlight a person's superpower.
claims.evidence: Lenny cites research showing focusing on strengths is more effective than obsessing over weaknesses, and warns to watch for gender bias where the same behavior is described as strength for men and weakness for women.

newsletters.title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
newsletters.subtitle: What I learned about growing supply at Airbnb that you can apply to your marketplace business

claims.text: There are 28 distinct tactics for growing supply in a marketplace, organized across seven strategies: direct outreach, piggyback existing networks, referrals, subsidize supply, events, single-player mode, and community building.
claims.evidence: Lenny's comprehensive taxonomy from his Airbnb experience and Andrew Chen blog collaboration, providing examples from Airbnb, Uber, Lyft, Etsy, and other major marketplaces for each tactic.

newsletters.title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
newsletters.subtitle: Create strong culture, stay laser-focused on problems, and set wildly ambitious goals

claims.text: Nailing the problem statement is the single most important step in solving any problem, and simple projects with vague problem statements go in circles while complex projects with strong problem statements sail smoothly.
claims.evidence: Lenny's core lesson from seven years at Airbnb, supported by a 1-pager template, the SCR framework for communication, and the jobs-to-be-done framework for customer needs.

claims.text: At Airbnb, CEO Brian Chesky was famous for doubling proposed goals or pushing for 10x, and teams were often shocked at how close they came to hitting these wildly ambitious targets.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes five ingredients: uncomfortable goals, individual accountability, long-term thinking, cross-functional ownership, and celebrating success without punishing failure.

claims.text: The most important product to get right in a product org is how you organize your people; the ideal is dedicated cross-functional teams with a clear mandate that can move autonomously, and any missing resource or added dependency cuts impact immensely.
claims.evidence: Lenny's principle from Airbnb about org design as product, noting he went through almost a dozen reorgs and there was never a perfect design, but minimizing cross-team dependencies was key.

claims.text: Some of the biggest guest conversion gains at Airbnb came from simple tweaks that gave users fewer things to think about, such as opening listings in new tabs, extending session length, and removing links within the payments flow.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes similar focus wins on the host side including recommended tags, persona-based defaults, and inline tips, concluding 'Don't underestimate the power of focus.'

newsletters.title: What Buddhism Taught Me About Product Management
newsletters.subtitle: We think we’ll be happy when we finally get what we want (e.g

claims.text: Find your fulfillment in solving problems, not in forever keeping problems from arising, because fires will always burn, people will get upset, and things will go wrong as a natural order.
claims.evidence: Lenny applies the Buddhist concept of Dukkha (suffering/dissatisfaction) to product management, noting that the illusion of a problem-free state is counterproductive for leaders.

claims.text: Nothing, no matter how well it's working, is going to last, so welcome change, anticipate it, and use it to your advantage rather than clinging to existing ideas, products, or strategies.
claims.evidence: Lenny applies the Buddhist concept of Anicca (impermanence) to product management, noting that the hardest things to let go of are often your own ideas and that killing bad ideas quickly benefits both you and the business.

claims.text: The most effective leaders place the team above themselves by deflecting credit and giving presentation opportunities to other team members, which strengthens the team's efficacy.
claims.evidence: Lenny connects the Buddhist concept of Anatta (no-self) to leadership, citing Good to Great's finding that the most successful companies were led by Level 5 Leaders whose ambition was first for the organization, not themselves.

newsletters.title: How to get into product management
newsletters.subtitle: Figure out if this role is for you, how to make the move, and what skills you’ll need to build in order to be successful

claims.text: The seven core PM skills in order of importance are strategic thinking, execution, communication, leadership through influence, data-informed decision making, product taste, and always being prepared.
claims.evidence: Lenny's comprehensive guide with curated resources for each skill, noting that a PM good at nothing else but execution is valuable while a team with a PM that can't execute is better off without that PM.

claims.text: The four most common paths into product management are internal transition at a large company, finding a junior PM role, joining a startup with a burning need, and starting your own company.
claims.evidence: Lenny's assessment based on his own transition from engineering to PM at Airbnb and helping many others transition from Operations, Data Science, and Finance.

newsletters.title: A Three-Step Framework For Solving Problems 👌
newsletters.subtitle: We work long and hard to design an amazing experience for travelers to discover fun local things to do with other travelers

claims.text: Nothing is more certain to cause a project to fail than a misunderstanding of the problem you are solving, and the three steps to avoid this are crystallize the problem, align your team on it, and keep coming back to it throughout execution.
claims.evidence: Lenny shares the story of building a social travel feature at Airbnb that failed because they solved 'travelers want to hang out together' instead of the real problem 'travelers want to find quality non-touristy things to do,' which Airbnb Experiences later solved.

claims.text: Problem statements are like silver burritos: everyone on the team has a unique version of the problem in their heads, and the PM's job is to eradicate this misalignment early and often.
claims.evidence: Lenny uses the Chipotle billboard analogy where everyone pictures their ideal burrito inside the wrapper, applying it to how team members each imagine different versions of the project scope.

newsletters.title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
newsletters.subtitle: With special guest contributor Hunter Walk!

claims.text: A late-arriving technical cofounder should receive common shares with standard vesting, with equity ranging from around 10% for a true cofounder up to 1-5% for a senior founding team member.
claims.evidence: Hunter Walk's advice based on working with over a hundred early-stage startups at Homebrew, noting these are averages and the CTO might reasonably ask for a termination-without-cause protection before vesting cliff.

podcasts.title: Jenny Wen
podcasts.description: It's not just designers who are feeling like, "Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers." I think even engineers are like, "How do we keep up with ourselves?", covering product design, AI product work, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
moments.summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
moments.summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
moments.summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
moments.summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
moments.summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
moments.summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

podcasts.title: Jeetu Patel
podcasts.description: And the reason we pulled this thing together, the goal was, what is happening in the industry and how can we help customers make sure that they can make the most of it, covering AI product work, team leadership, and product design.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
moments.summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
moments.summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
moments.summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
moments.summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
moments.summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
moments.summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
moments.summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

podcasts.title: Boris Cherny
podcasts.description: This is the world now." What's the next big shift to how software is written that either your team is already operating in or you think will head towards, covering engineering tradeoffs, product design, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
moments.summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
moments.summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
moments.summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
moments.summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

podcasts.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
podcasts.description: Brian Halligan co-founded HubSpot, ran it as CEO for about 15 years, and now coaches Sequoia’s fastest-growing founders as their in-house CEO coach.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
moments.summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.
moments.summary: AI makes starting companies trivially easy, but the resulting explosion of competition makes scaling harder than ever.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
moments.summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
moments.summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
moments.summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
moments.summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

podcasts.title: Sherwin Wu V2
podcasts.description: If you talk to a lot of engineers, the thing that annoys them the most is after you've written your beautiful code, how do you get it into production, covering engineering tradeoffs, AI product work, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.
moments.summary: Software engineers are transforming from code writers into tech lead managers who orchestrate 10-20 parallel AI agent threads.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The one person billion dollar startup implies that to enable it, there might be a hundred other small startups building bespoke software. We might actually enter into a golden age of B2B SaaS.
moments.summary: The one-person billion-dollar startup will spawn a golden age of B2B SaaS with tens of thousands of $10M micro-companies.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
moments.summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: Companies where AI really works have top-down buy-in combined with bottoms-up adoption. A lot of AI deployments fail because it's an exec mandate, extremely top-down, divorced from what actual work looks like.
moments.summary: Successful AI adoption requires both executive buy-in and bottoms-up evangelism from an internal tiger team.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.
moments.summary: AI models keep consuming the scaffolding built around them, so builders should target where model capabilities are heading.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
moments.summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.
moments.summary: Startups fail from lack of customer resonance, not from being crushed by OpenAI.

podcasts.title: The rise of the professional vibe coder (a new AI-era job) | Lazar Jovanovic (Professional Vibe Coder)
podcasts.description: Lazar Jovanovic is a full-time professional vibe coder at Lovable. His job is to build both internal tools and customer-facing products purely using AI, while not having a coding background.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: I don't have a coding background. My job is to build both internal tools and customer-facing products purely using AI. The key is knowing what to build, not how to build it.
moments.summary: Professional vibe coder with no coding background builds production tools for Lovable using only AI.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: The biggest mistake people make is starting too big. Start with something you actually need. My first project was a simple dashboard for tracking my own metrics.
moments.summary: Start vibe coding with personal needs, not ambitious projects — the learning compounds from real usage.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: Internal tools are the sweet spot for vibe coding. The bar for quality is lower, the feedback loop is faster, and the value is immediate.
moments.summary: Internal tools are the ideal use case for vibe coding due to lower quality bar and faster feedback loops.

podcasts.title: A child psychologist’s guide to working with difficult adults | Dr. Becky Kennedy
podcasts.description: Working with difficult adults, covering product design, team leadership, and practical product lessons.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Repair is the number one relationship strategy we have. Secure attachment isn't defined by getting it right all the time — it's defined by an adult who's willing to repair.
moments.summary: Repair — taking responsibility after you mess up — is the most powerful relationship strategy.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: The idea of being good inside inherently requires us to separate behavior and identity. When someone's late to work a lot, start with: 'This is a good person who is late.'
moments.summary: Separate behavior from identity to have productive conversations.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Picture a turbulent flight. The sturdy pilot says: 'I hear you screaming. It makes sense. I know what I'm doing. This turbulence doesn't scare me. I'll see you when we land.' That's sturdy leadership.
moments.summary: Sturdy leadership means acknowledging others' emotions as real without being overwhelmed by them.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Boundaries are what you tell someone else you will do, and it requires the other person to do nothing. Too often people confuse requests with boundaries.
moments.summary: True boundaries define what you will do and require nothing from the other person.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Optimizing for happiness in childhood is the quickest way to build anxiety and fragility in adulthood. When we're thinking about a resilient work culture, we want people who can say, 'This is hard and I can do hard things.'
moments.summary: Optimizing for happiness over resilience creates fragility.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Dealing with anxiety is a combination of 'I believe you and I believe in you.' Picture someone in a hole — they need you to have one foot in the hole with them and one foot outside.
moments.summary: Effective leadership combines validation with confidence in someone's capability.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: The most generous interpretation — MGI. The story you tell yourself about your organization at night becomes the leader you are the next morning.
moments.summary: The Most Generous Interpretation (MGI) transforms how leaders respond to difficult behavior.

podcasts.title: Marc Andreessen: The real AI boom hasn’t even started yet
podcasts.description: Marc Andreessen is a founder, investor, and co-founder of Netscape, as well as co-founder of the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). In this conversation, we dig into why we’re living through a unique and one of the most incredible times in history, and what comes next.

moments.speaker: Marc Andreessen
moments.text: Every PM in the world should be learning to build with AI tools. Not because they'll replace engineers, but because the ability to prototype changes the entire product development cycle.
moments.summary: All PMs should learn AI building tools — not to replace engineers, but to fundamentally change the product development cycle.

moments.speaker: Marc Andreessen
moments.text: The companies that are going to win are the ones where AI isn't a feature — it's the operating system. It changes how you build, how you sell, how you support.
moments.summary: Winning companies will use AI as their operating system — transforming building, selling, and support — not just adding it as a feature.

podcasts.title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
podcasts.description: Jason’s five-step framework: logo retention, pricing, NRR, marketing channels, target market.

moments.speaker: Jason Cohen
moments.text: When growth stalls, 90% of the time it's a retention problem, not an acquisition problem. But teams always want to fix acquisition first because it's more fun.
moments.summary: Stalled growth is almost always a retention issue, but teams default to fixing acquisition because it's more engaging.

moments.speaker: Jason Cohen
moments.text: Logo retention is the most honest metric in B2B. Net revenue retention can mask churn with expansion. Logo retention tells you if customers are actually staying.
moments.summary: Logo retention is more honest than NRR for B2B — it reveals real customer retention without expansion masking churn.

podcasts.title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
podcasts.description: The complete AI workflow that lets non-technical people build real products in Cursor.

moments.speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
moments.text: I'm a PM at Meta with zero coding background. I built a full internal tool using Cursor in three days. It's now used by my entire team daily.
moments.summary: Non-technical Meta PM built a team-wide internal tool in 3 days using Cursor with no coding background.

moments.speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
moments.text: Start with your own pain point. Don't try to build a startup. Build something you need. The motivation to finish is completely different when you're the user.
moments.summary: Start AI building with personal pain points — self-use motivation is the key to finishing projects.

podcasts.title: How to show up in any room with a low heart rate: Silicon Valley’s missing etiquette playbook | Sam Lessin
podcasts.description: Silicon Valley’s missing etiquette playbook, covering startup building, product design, and operator lessons.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: There has to be a deeper truth to it. There's a serious real narrative to why etiquette matters in 2025 for founders.
moments.summary: Etiquette is a critical business skill for founders because building trust matters more than ever.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Etiquette is a skill for how to show up in a room with a low heart rate. You kind of want to show up with the self-confidence and the calm of abundance.
moments.summary: Etiquette is fundamentally about showing up with an abundance mindset and low heart rate.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Coming in and saying 'Hey let me give you my four-minute startup spiel' is so self-centered. Think of it like ping pong — hit the ball back.
moments.summary: The best networking conversations are balanced exchanges like ping pong, not one-sided monologues.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Vibe coding is exactly good for this — Cursor and DigitalOcean and Cloudflare will get you a long way. But vibe coding also doesn't really scale.
moments.summary: Vibe coding tools are great for building personal projects and MVPs but don't scale.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Seed VCs who invest in companies branded as AI companies are going to lose an impossibly large amount of money. There's a difference between a great business using AI versus an 'AI company.'
moments.summary: AI-branded startups are terrible seed investments because they are too capital intensive and commoditizing.

podcasts.title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
podcasts.description: How to set up and use Codex in VS Code and terminal environments for both simple and complex coding tasks.

moments.speaker: Alexander Embiricos
moments.text: Context engineering is the new prompt engineering. It's not about the single prompt — it's about what information you give the AI over the entire session.
moments.summary: Context engineering (session-level information management) is replacing prompt engineering as the key AI skill.

moments.speaker: Alexander Embiricos
moments.text: The trick is parallelization. Run five agents at once on different parts of the problem. Your job becomes orchestration, not execution.
moments.summary: Parallelizing AI agents transforms the developer role from executor to orchestrator.

podcasts.title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
podcasts.description: But if you make a bunch of practitioners sit together and ask them, "Is it important to build an actionable feedback loop for AI products?" I think all of them will agree, covering AI product work, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti
moments.text: If you make a bunch of practitioners sit together and ask them, 'Is it important to build an actionable feedback loop for AI products?' All of them will agree. But almost nobody does it well.
moments.summary: Everyone agrees AI products need feedback loops, but almost no one implements them well in practice.

podcasts.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
podcasts.description: Molly Graham has worked for some of tech’s most effective leaders, including Mark Zuckerberg, Sheryl Sandberg, Chamath Palihapitiya, and Bret Taylor. Today she leads Glue Club, a community for leaders navigating rapid scale, growth, and change.

moments.speaker: Molly Graham
moments.text: In high-growth, you have to hire for the role people will grow into, not the role they're perfect for today. The job changes every 6-12 months.
moments.summary: High-growth hiring: recruit for growth potential, not current-role fit — the job changes every 6-12 months.

podcasts.title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
podcasts.description: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened, covering B2B products, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: However you get those first 10, 15, 20 customers, be honest. If they need a sales type motion and you say 'I don't like sales, so I'm going to do a PLG motion,' you'll fail.
moments.summary: Founders must be honest about whether their customers need a sales-led motion rather than forcing PLG.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Almost all founders are A+ middlers. Any founder should be there by day one. And prospects love it — they get to talk to you. It's magical for early adopters.
moments.summary: Founders are naturally great at the middle part of sales conversations because of their deep product knowledge.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Those first couple reps have to be people you would buy your own product from. We're looking for pirates and romantics in the early days.
moments.summary: Your first sales hires must be people you would personally buy your own product from.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: You need two sales reps hitting quota before you're ready to hire a manager for them. If you hire a VP of sales before then, it's approaching 100% chance of failure.
moments.summary: Hiring a VP of Sales before you have two reps hitting quota is almost guaranteed to fail.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: What do you want to do your first 30 days? In B2B, if I don't hear 'I'm going to go meet customers,' I'm out.
moments.summary: The ultimate screening question for any VP hire is whether they want to meet customers in their first two weeks.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Trust me, hire someone whose last product was harder to sell. If you hire someone from something easier, they will have none of the skills.
moments.summary: Always hire salespeople whose previous product was harder to sell than yours.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: In sales, there's rules of eight. Eight SDRs need one manager, eight AEs need a director above them. You can build your whole org with eights.
moments.summary: Scale your sales org using the rule of eight and concentrate leads on fewer, better closers.

podcasts.title: 10 contrarian leadership truths every leader needs to hear | Matt MacInnis (Rippling)
podcasts.description: Matt MacInnis is the chief product officer and former longtime COO at Rippling, a unified workforce management platform valued at over $16 billion.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: It is really important to me that we feel that we've deliberately understaffed every project at the company.
moments.summary: Deliberate understaffing prevents teams from working on low-priority items and forces focus.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: You don't really learn from your mistakes, you learn from your successes. As an early career PM, you should join a winning team.
moments.summary: You learn far more from being part of a winning team than from failure.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: Processes exist for the sole purpose of lowering beta — decreasing volatility. The downside is they suppress alpha.
moments.summary: Use the alpha/beta framework: processes lower volatility but suppress outperformance.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: Product market fit is a sort of thing where you absolutely know it when you see it. The Silicon Valley 'never quit' mindset is not pro-entrepreneur, it's pro-venture capitalist.
moments.summary: If you don't obviously have product-market fit, you don't have it — and 'never quit' advice serves VCs, not founders.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: The market is immutable. No amount of tweeting, advertising is going to change whether the market wants your product.
moments.summary: Product-market fit is like drug receptor binding — the market wants your product or it does not.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: Point solutions don't have enough data in the age of AI to be useful. If you own the mine with the data, you can make money.
moments.summary: In the AI era, point SaaS solutions lack sufficient data context — only companies owning first-party data capture value.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: The purest form of ambition and most intense source of energy in the business is the founder CEO. Every next concentric circle of management has the potential to be an order of magnitude drop off in intensity.
moments.summary: Executive leadership intensity drops exponentially with each management layer.

podcasts.title: Elena Verna 4.0
podcasts.description: Who would've thought that a head of growth, who is traditionally seen as data, metrics, spreadsheets, drive KPIs is like, "Okay, how do we make this more lovable, covering growth systems, product design, and pricing decisions.

moments.speaker: Elena Verna
moments.text: The best growth teams I've seen all have one thing in common: they measure retention before acquisition. If your bucket is leaky, pouring more water in doesn't help.
moments.summary: Top growth teams prioritize retention measurement over acquisition — fix the leaky bucket first.

moments.speaker: Elena Verna
moments.text: Gamification gets a bad rap because people think of it as badges and points. But the good kind — leaderboards, streaks, social proof — those are fundamentally about making progress visible.
moments.summary: Good gamification makes progress visible through mechanics like leaderboards and streaks, not superficial badges.

moments.speaker: Elena Verna
moments.text: Notifications are the most underinvested growth lever. Most companies treat them as marketing spam. The companies that win treat every notification as a product decision.
moments.summary: Notifications should be product decisions, not marketing — most companies underinvest in notification quality.

podcasts.title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
podcasts.description: Edwin Chen is the founder and CEO of Surge AI, the company that teaches AI what’s good and bad, powering frontier labs with elite data, environments, and evaluations. Surge surpassed $1 billion in revenue with under 100 employees last year, completely bootstrapped—the fastest company in history to reach this milestone.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game. We could fire 90% of the people and we would move faster.
moments.summary: Surge rejected the Silicon Valley playbook and built to a billion in revenue with under 100 people.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: Imagine you wanted to train a model to write an eight line poem about the moon. Most people check: Is this a poem? Does it contain eight lines? But we are looking for Nobel Prize-winning poetry.
moments.summary: The difference between mediocre and exceptional AI training data is the difference between checking boxes and demanding Nobel Prize-level quality.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: Instead of building AI that will actually advance us as a species, we are optimizing for AI slop instead. We're basically teaching our models to chase dopamine instead of truth.
moments.summary: AI labs are optimizing for engagement and flashy leaderboard metrics rather than truth and accuracy.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: An RL environment is essentially a simulation of the real world. We might build a world where you have a startup with Gmail, Slack, Jira, GitHub. And then suddenly AWS goes down. Model, what do you do?
moments.summary: RL environments simulating messy real-world scenarios are the next frontier of AI training.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: Do you want a model that says 'There are definitely 20 more ways to improve this email' or one that's optimizing for your time and just says 'Your email's great, just send it'?
moments.summary: AI models will differentiate based on whether they optimize for engagement or for user productivity.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: The standard playbook is to get product market fit by pivoting every two weeks, chase growth with dark patterns, blitz scale. I've always disagreed. Just build the one thing only you could build.
moments.summary: Reject the Silicon Valley playbook — build the one company that only you could build based on your unique insight.

moments.speaker: Edwin Chen
moments.text: Vibe coding is over-hyped. People don't realize how much it's going to make your systems unmaintainable in the long-term.
moments.summary: Vibe coding is overhyped because short-term gains mask long-term technical debt.

podcasts.title: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered "full stack builders” | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
podcasts.description: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered "full stack builders”, covering product design, team leadership, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Tomer Cohen
moments.text: We're calling it the 'full-stack builder.' A PM who can prototype, run evals, analyze data, and ship small features independently. This is the new bar at LinkedIn.
moments.summary: LinkedIn's new PM standard: 'full-stack builders' who can prototype, eval, analyze, and ship independently.

moments.speaker: Tomer Cohen
moments.text: The PMs who are thriving right now are the ones who see AI as expanding their capabilities, not threatening their role. They're building things they never could before.
moments.summary: PMs who embrace AI as capability expansion (not threat) are thriving by building things previously impossible.

podcasts.title: What world-class GTM looks like in 2026 | Jeanne DeWitt Grosser (Vercel, Stripe, Google)
podcasts.description: Jeanne DeWitt Grosser built world-class GTM teams at Stripe, Google, and, most recently, Vercel, where she serves as COO and oversees marketing, sales, customer success, revenue operations, and field engineering. She transformed Stripe’s early sales organization from the ground up and advises founders on GTM strategy.

moments.speaker: Jeanne DeWitt Grosser
moments.text: With AI, it's just intensified because you have 10 players pursuing the same market opportunity and so your ability to actually bring the product to market has become more strategically important.
moments.summary: AI has intensified competition, making GTM execution a strategic differentiator.

moments.speaker: Jeanne DeWitt Grosser
moments.text: 80% of customers buy to avoid pain or reduce risk as opposed to increased upside. For enterprises, you're avoiding the risk of not making your revenue target next quarter.
moments.summary: Enterprise buyers are primarily motivated by avoiding pain and reducing risk, not by upside potential.

podcasts.title: A guide to difficult conversations, building high-trust teams, and designing a life you love | Rachel Lockett
podcasts.description: A guide to difficult conversations, building high-trust teams, and designing a life you love, covering team leadership, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: Most leaders, especially technical leaders, assume they have to have all the answers. But great leaders know that you're training your team to come to you with all of the hard problems.
moments.summary: Leaders who always provide answers inadvertently train their teams to be dependent.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: There's actually three levels to listening. Level one is internal. Level two is focused. Level three is global listening — you hear what they're communicating, not just saying.
moments.summary: The three levels of listening framework distinguishes between self-focused, word-focused, and deep empathetic listening.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: I use a GROW model. G is goal, R is reality, O is options, W is way forward.
moments.summary: The GROW coaching model provides a simple framework for leaders to coach rather than dictate.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: We operate in tech. We're supposed to give all of ourselves purely logically. That's not at all true. The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.
moments.summary: Tech work is deeply emotional despite its logical veneer, and the goal of conflict should be mutual understanding.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: My litmus test is 80%. If you're 80% of the time in your gifts, how much energy you have to give to the world.
moments.summary: Aim to spend 80% of your work time in your natural strengths and gifts to maximize energy and impact.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: 65% of startups fail because of co-founder conflict. Just like couples need a date night, co-founders need regular check-ins.
moments.summary: Co-founder relationships need intentional maintenance through regular check-ins.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? That's the question we always asked at Stripe.
moments.summary: The 'enthusiastic rehire' test provides binary clarity for evaluating whether someone is right for their role.

moments.speaker: Rachel Lockett
moments.text: The one-page plan puts your vision and values on the first column, strategic intentions and KPIs on the second, annual goals on the third, and quarterly goals on the fourth.
moments.summary: A one-page plan aligning vision, strategy, annual goals, and quarterly goals creates clarity across the organization.

podcasts.title: Slack founder: Mental models for building products people love ft. Stewart Butterfield
podcasts.description: Mental models for building products people love ft. Stewart Butterfield, covering product design, B2B products, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: I feel like what we have right now is just a giant piece of shit. If you can't see almost limitless opportunities to improve, then you shouldn't be designing the product.
moments.summary: A perpetual dissatisfaction with your product is essential for great product design.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: Along with reduced friction, 'reduce the number of clicks' is almost always exactly the wrong thing. If it takes eight clicks but every one is trivially easy, that's great.
moments.summary: Reducing clicks is the wrong goal — instead focus on reducing cognitive load per interaction.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: At more than one company all hands, I made everyone repeat: In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value that we create for customers.
moments.summary: Slack's company mantra was that long-term success is measured solely by value created for customers.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: Hyper-realistic work-like activities are superficially identical to work but actually fake. Every executive will do it.
moments.summary: Organizations fill up with 'hyper-realistic work-like activities' that look identical to real work but produce no value.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: The overwhelming majority of people you hire want to hire more people who report to them because headcount correlates with career trajectory.
moments.summary: Parkinson's Law: nearly everyone wants to hire more reports because headcount correlates with career trajectory.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: You're not just responsible for creating the product, but also creating the market. It's almost impossible to create a new idea in someone's head.
moments.summary: Founders must create both the product and the market, positioning through familiar concepts.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: The owner's delusion is when you think your thing is so important that you don't recognize users are regular human beings who will bounce in a fraction of a second.
moments.summary: The 'owner's delusion' is when builders overestimate user attention and investment.

moments.speaker: Stewart Butterfield
moments.text: You have to be coldly rational about pivoting because it's humiliating. It feels better to keep doing it until it dies of suffocation.
moments.summary: Pivoting requires cold rationality because emotional cost causes most founders to ride a failing idea to its death.

podcasts.title: The Godmother of AI on jobs, robots & why world models are next | Dr. Fei-Fei Li
podcasts.description: Jobs, robots & why world models are next, covering AI product work, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
moments.text: Whatever AI does, currently or in the future, is up to us. Every technology is a double-edged sword.
moments.summary: AI's impact on humanity is determined by human choices.

moments.speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
moments.text: We curated 15 million images on the internet, created a taxonomy of 22,000 concepts. That combination of big data, neural network, and GPU was the golden recipe for modern AI.
moments.summary: ImageNet's combination of big data, neural networks, and GPUs created the 'golden recipe' that sparked the deep learning revolution.

moments.speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
moments.text: In the middle of 2015, 2016, some tech companies avoided using the word AI because they were not sure if AI was a dirty word.
moments.summary: As recently as 2015-2016, major tech companies avoided the term 'AI' entirely.

moments.speaker: Dr. Fei-Fei Li
moments.text: I realized the North Star challenge of AI is visual intelligence. Our human brains use about 50% of our neurons on visual processing. This is fundamentally a big data problem.
moments.summary: The ImageNet breakthrough came from recognizing that AI visual intelligence was fundamentally a data problem.

podcasts.title: “Dumbest idea I’ve heard” to $100M ARR: Inside the rise of Gamma | Grant Lee (CEO)
podcasts.description: Inside the rise of Gamma, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Grant Lee
moments.text: We grew to $100M ARR largely through product-led growth. The key was making the creation experience so fast that people would share their Gamma decks, and viewers would sign up to make their own.
moments.summary: Gamma reached $100M ARR through product-led growth driven by a fast creation experience and viral sharing.

moments.speaker: Grant Lee
moments.text: We use gamification elements — streak-like engagement mechanics, progress indicators — but we never call them that internally. We call them 'momentum features.'
moments.summary: Gamma uses gamification-style mechanics internally called 'momentum features' to drive engagement.

podcasts.title: "Sell the alpha, not the feature": The enterprise sales playbook for $1M to $10M ARR | Jen Abel
podcasts.description: Jen Abel is GM of Enterprise at State Affairs and co-founded Jellyfish, a consultancy that helps founders learn zero-to-one enterprise sales. She’s one of the smartest people I’ve ever met on learning enterprise sales, and in this follow-up to our first chat two years ago (covering the zero to $1 million ARR.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: A lot of early stage founders get tripped up taking late stage sales advice. The founder is the product.
moments.summary: Early-stage founders fail by following late-stage sales advice — the founder IS the product.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: One of the worst things someone can say is 'we're better than X product.' Better is a dangerous place. You need to be different, not better.
moments.summary: Pitching as 'better' is a trap — position as fundamentally different and counterintuitive.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: Founder led sales is not about revenue on day one. It is about learning as fast as humanly possible.
moments.summary: Founder-led sales has two phases: first 'sales for research' then 'sales for revenue'.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: On the first call, the demo is the only card you control. Once they see it, that dreaminess is gone. Leave them wanting more.
moments.summary: Withhold the product demo on the first sales call to preserve mystery.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: Everyone says they have a bottom of funnel problem. It's never a bottom of funnel problem. It's always qualification.
moments.summary: Sales problems that appear bottom-of-funnel are almost always top-of-funnel qualification issues.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: 40 to 50% of B2B SaaS startups have to sell some form of service before they can sell a technology.
moments.summary: Nearly half of B2B SaaS startups need to sell services first to educate buyers.

moments.speaker: Jen Abel
moments.text: Trust is the number one currency in sales. Don't try to sell something just to prove to investors you sold something.
moments.summary: Trust is the ultimate sales currency.

podcasts.title: The woman behind Canva shares how she built a $42B company from nothing | Melanie Perkins
podcasts.description: The woman behind Canva shares how she built a $42B company from nothing, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: Investors gave really helpful feedback, often in the form of rejection. I would add a new page showing how big the market was.
moments.summary: Melanie Perkins treated each of her 100+ investor rejections as product feedback.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: The thing that I love about a crazy big goal is that you feel completely inadequate before it. You want to work really hard to will it into existence.
moments.summary: Crazy big goals work because they create a sense of inadequacy that drives relentless effort.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: I don't have emails on my phone. When I shut my laptop, I actually tune out. Sometimes you can miss the forest from the trees when you're just working harder.
moments.summary: Creating hard boundaries between work and rest provides the perspective to work on the right things.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: We get more than a million requests from our community every year. This year we've closed more than 200 loops.
moments.summary: Canva processes over a million community requests annually, treating user feedback as half of their product strategy.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: Step one, build one of the world's most valuable companies. Step two, do the most good we can do. They fuel each other.
moments.summary: Canva's two-step plan combines building a valuable company with doing maximum good, and they are mutually reinforcing.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: We didn't worry about competitors at all. It's better to solve a small number of people's problem really well.
moments.summary: Canva succeeded by ignoring competitors and deeply solving a small group's pain point.

moments.speaker: Melanie Perkins
moments.text: There's many times we took bricks from someone else's house, and they didn't match. Confidence in how we take what is authentic to us and do it at the next level of scale is constant work.
moments.summary: Importing practices from other companies often fails — authenticity at each stage of scale matters more.

podcasts.title: How Block is becoming the most AI-native enterprise in the world | Dhanji R. Prasanna
podcasts.description: Dhanji R. Prasanna is the chief technology officer at Block (formerly Square), where he’s managed more than 4,000 engineers over the past two years. Under his leadership, Block has become one of the most AI-native large companies in the world.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: We find engineering teams that are very AI forward are reporting about eight to 10 hours saved per week. This is the worst it will ever be. This is now the baseline.
moments.summary: AI-forward engineering teams at Block save 8-10 hours per week, and this represents the floor of AI productivity gains.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: He built this system where Goose is essentially just watching everything he does all the time. A few hours later Goose has already tried to build that feature and opened a PR.
moments.summary: A Block engineer built a system where Goose watches his screen and Slack, autonomously building features and opening PRs.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: Vibe coding is highly limiting. We're trying to push Goose to work not just for five minutes at a time but for hours.
moments.summary: Block is pushing beyond vibe coding toward autonomous agents that work for hours overnight.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: One of the things I do regularly is just throw away huge amounts of code. What would our world look like if every release deleted the entire app and rebuilt from scratch?
moments.summary: AI enables a radical new workflow where engineers throw away entire systems and rebuild from scratch.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: The non-technical people using AI agents and programming tools to build things is really what's been surprising and amazing.
moments.summary: The biggest surprise in AI adoption at Block is non-technical people using coding agents.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: A lot of engineers think code quality is important to building a successful product. The two have nothing to do with each other. YouTube was storing videos as blobs in MySQL.
moments.summary: Code quality and product success are unrelated — YouTube beat Google Video despite terrible architecture.

moments.speaker: Dhanji R. Prasanna
moments.text: The power of Conway's Law — how difficult it is to change outcomes without changing the structure of relationships between people.
moments.summary: Conway's Law is viscerally real at scale — you cannot change outcomes without changing org structure.

podcasts.title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
podcasts.description: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix), covering AI product work, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Chip Huyen
moments.text: Product managers don't need to understand backpropagation. But they need to understand what a model can and can't do, what evals mean, and how to think about AI reliability.
moments.summary: PMs need AI literacy at the decision-making level — not technical depth, but capability and reliability understanding.

moments.speaker: Chip Huyen
moments.text: Evals are the most important thing in AI engineering right now. More important than model selection, more important than prompt engineering. Get your evals right and everything else follows.
moments.summary: Evals are the most critical AI engineering practice — more important than model or prompt selection.

podcasts.title: How to measure AI developer productivity in 2025 | Nicole Forsgren
podcasts.description: Nicole Forsgren created the most widely used frameworks for measuring developer productivity—DORA and SPACE. She wrote the foundational book Accelerate and is about to release her newest book, Frictionless , a practical guide for helping teams move faster in the AI era.

moments.speaker: Nicole Forsgren
moments.text: The biggest mistake in measuring AI productivity is looking at lines of code or PRs per day. You need to measure outcomes: cycle time, change failure rate, deployment frequency.
moments.summary: Measure AI productivity by outcomes (cycle time, failure rate, deployment frequency), not output volume.

moments.speaker: Nicole Forsgren
moments.text: DORA metrics still work in the AI era, but you need to add new dimensions. We're adding 'AI augmentation rate' — what percentage of work is AI-assisted vs. purely human.
moments.summary: DORA metrics remain valid but need AI-era additions like 'AI augmentation rate' for assisted vs. human work.

podcasts.title: Inside Google's AI turnaround: The rise of AI Mode, strategy behind AI Overviews, and their vision for AI-powered search | Robby Stein (VP of Product, Google Search)
podcasts.description: Robby Stein is VP of Product at Google, where he oversees the core products of Google Search—including the new AI Overviews, AI Mode, search ranking, Google Lens, and more.

moments.speaker: Robby Stein
moments.text: AI is expansionary. There's actually just more and more questions being asked and curiosity that can be fulfilled now with AI.
moments.summary: AI is not replacing traditional search but expanding it — people are asking more questions than ever.

moments.speaker: Robby Stein
moments.text: You need to be the physical manifestation of relentlessness. My wife described me in one word: dissatisfied. It's not unhappiness — you want to make it better.
moments.summary: The best product builders embody relentless dissatisfaction — an inability to accept the status quo.

moments.speaker: Robby Stein
moments.text: For Stories, we knew we needed to do something new. We made a bunch of decisions that made it Instagram — letting people upload from camera roll, adding pause.
