podcasts.title: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI | Eli Schwartz (SEO advisor, author)
podcasts.description: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI, covering AI product work, go-to-market execution, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: Think of SEO as a product. The product managers are the people that should be thinking about this SEO question because it's a product question. This is a user that's doing their own self-discovery journey. If you can't answer the question about what is it that someone's going to do a search on, then don't do SEO.
moments.summary: SEO should be treated as a product problem, not a marketing trick -- PMs should own it because it is about serving users on a self-discovery journey.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: Transparently, I thought this was going to be an apocalypse. Up until AI Overviews, whoever won on that long form piece of content would get that first click. But now that doesn't exist anymore.
moments.summary: AI Overviews in search have fundamentally changed SEO by eliminating the first-click advantage that long-form content used to enjoy.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: In their documentation, they say that AI itself is not the problem. It's the helpfulness, the usefulness of the content that would be a problem.
moments.summary: Google's stance on AI content is that AI authorship itself is not penalized -- what matters is whether the content is genuinely useful to users.

podcasts.title: The things engineers are desperate for PMs to understand | Camille Fournier (author of “The Manager’s Path,” ex-CTO at Rent the Runway)
podcasts.description: The things engineers are desperate for PMs to understand, covering team leadership, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Camille Fournier
moments.text: Hoarding credit. PMs, they tend to be the front-facing person for initiative. Engineers sometimes think that they don't get the credit for their work because the PM takes all the glory and all the credit for the project that they really worked very hard on.
moments.summary: The number one thing PMs do that annoys engineers is hoarding credit -- being the front-facing person while engineers feel invisible for the real work.

moments.speaker: Camille Fournier
moments.text: The next thing that engineers really get annoyed about with PMs, when they just don't understand the details and act like they don't matter, it just shows a real lack of empathy for the work that engineers are doing and I think it really can be very off-putting.
moments.summary: PMs who dismiss technical details signal a lack of empathy for engineering work, which is deeply off-putting to their engineering partners.

podcasts.title: Land your dream job in today’s market: negotiation tactics, job search councils, and more | Phyl Terry (Author, “Never Search Alone”)
podcasts.description: Negotiation tactics, job search councils, and more, covering strategic decision-making, career development, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: When you're looking for a job, you need a spear and not a net. What happens when we're building a product? Same thing, right? We want this product to be for everyone, but we've learned with product market fit that doesn't work. We need a narrow, clear focus.
moments.summary: Job searching requires a spear, not a net -- just like product-market fit, you need narrow focus rather than trying to appeal to everyone.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: While it's hard to figure out your candidate market fit, it's also a relief to know it's not about you. So what I ask people to do is I ask them to think about what they want and what they don't want. Now, you might not think that that's a radical step, but most people don't do that. When they get laid off, they spray and pray.
moments.summary: Most people skip the fundamental step of defining what they want and do not want in a job -- instead they spray and pray, which rarely works.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: There's no I in team. Well, there is an I in village, and the I in village is that when you start to interview and negotiate, you've got to be in charge. I want you to play to win, not not to lose.
moments.summary: In job negotiations, shift from playing not-to-lose to playing to win -- take charge of the interview and negotiation process.

podcasts.title: The original growth hacker reveals his secrets | Sean Ellis (author of “Hacking Growth”)
podcasts.description: The original growth hacker reveals his secrets, covering growth systems, strategic decision-making, and product design.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: The question is, how would you feel if you could no longer use this product? Once you got a high enough percentage of users saying they'd be very disappointed, most of those products did pretty well. If you felt too low, those products tended to suffer.
moments.summary: The Sean Ellis test -- asking users how disappointed they would be without the product -- reliably predicts product-market fit when 40%+ say very disappointed.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: Just ignore the people who say they'd be somewhat disappointed. They're telling you it's a nice to have. If you start paying attention to what your somewhat disappointed users are telling you and then you start tweaking onboarding and product based on their feedback, maybe you're going to dilute it for your must have users.
moments.summary: Focus on 'very disappointed' users, not 'somewhat disappointed' ones -- optimizing for nice-to-have feedback risks diluting the experience for your core fans.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: It's usually much more function of onboarding to the right user experience than it is about the tactical things that people try to do to improve retention.
moments.summary: Retention is driven more by onboarding users to the right experience than by tactical retention hacks.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: Before the referral program, Dropbox had amazing referral rate. Companies that are trying to copy it are like, 'Why isn't anyone talking about a product? Let's add a referral program with incentives.' To me, I think it's a great accelerant when it's already working, but it can't fix it if people don't want to talk about your product.
moments.summary: A referral program is an accelerant for existing word of mouth -- it cannot create organic sharing that does not already exist.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: I start with the value that's uncovered through the test. So with a company, I'll say, 'This is what the must have value is according to our most passionate customers, and we want to think about a metric that reflects us delivering that value.'
moments.summary: Your North Star Metric should reflect the must-have value identified by your most passionate users, not revenue -- revenue should be a byproduct.

podcasts.title: How to consistently go viral: Nikita Bier’s playbook for winning at consumer apps (co-founder of TBH, Gas, advisor, investor)
podcasts.description: Nikita Bier’s playbook for winning at consumer apps (co-founder of TBH, Gas, advisor, investor), covering product design, consumer products, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: I looked on the App Store and the number one app in the United States was an app called Surah, but the entire app was in Arabic, like the strongest signal that you could ever have that people want something.
moments.summary: Finding an app in Arabic that was number one in the US App Store was the ultimate signal of latent demand -- the insight that led to building TBH.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: We launched this app, it immediately took off, servers started crashing. I looked at our numbers and I'm like, 'We will be number one in the United States in six days.'
moments.summary: TBH went from launch to number one in the US in six days, validating the power of building for pre-existing demand.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: Honored to be on a product management podcast for a person who doesn't believe product management is real.
moments.summary: Nikita Bier's provocative stance that product management as a discipline is not real reflects his builder-first, ship-fast philosophy.

podcasts.title: Unorthodox PM wisdom: Automating user insights, unselling job candidates, logging every decision, more | Kevin Yien (Stripe, Square, Mutiny)
podcasts.description: Automating user insights, unselling job candidates, logging every decision, more, covering product design, product strategy and execution, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: The PM job can become a little too internal, influencing my stakeholders and getting alignment and all these things. But if you can't sell or support your own product, I don't trust you to build the product.
moments.summary: PMs who cannot sell or support their own product have no business building it -- the role must stay grounded in customer reality, not internal politics.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: We all talk about product sense. To me, it's just a fancy way of saying you can make good decisions with insufficient data. PMs need as many reps as possible in making decisions, documenting the rationale behind those decisions, and then crucially seeing the outcome of them.
moments.summary: Product sense is the ability to make good decisions with insufficient data, developed by logging decisions, rationale, and outcomes over many reps.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: When you get to offer stage, I send an email and I say all the terrible things that are probably going to reinforce their fears. If you can tell them that upfront and they can read that whole email and still be equally excited to join you, find yourself a A+ hire.
moments.summary: The unsell email -- sharing all the terrible things about the role at offer stage -- filters for candidates who are truly committed and becomes a powerful hiring tool.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: I use AI to essentially do a voice-of-the-customer pipeline automatically. It processes all customer calls, tags them with themes, and then I can go pull up exactly what people said about a given topic.
moments.summary: AI can automate the voice-of-the-customer pipeline by processing all customer calls and tagging them by theme, giving PMs instant access to real feedback.

podcasts.title: Improve strategy, influence, and decision-making by understanding your brain | Evan LaPointe (founder of CORE Sciences)
podcasts.description: Improve strategy, influence, and decision-making by understanding your brain, covering product design, team leadership, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: The brain is like a college campus that has different departments in it. Most people rely on their history department way too much. If you instead send things to the more experimental, open-minded science department, the more creative art department, you get dramatically better answers.
moments.summary: Most people over-rely on their brain's history department (past patterns) instead of engaging the science department (experimentation) or art department (creativity).

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: It's critical to ask what kind of experience am I? Not how good am I at my job, how much do I know, how critical am I to this process, but am I a miserable experience? If the answer is yes, don't worry too much about the other pieces yet. You got to fix that first.
moments.summary: Before worrying about skills or knowledge, ask whether you are a pleasant experience for others to work with -- fix that first because everything else depends on it.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: If mission, vision, values was an airline, you would not allow any family to fly on that airline. It does not arrive at most of its intended destinations.
moments.summary: Traditional mission-vision-values has a terrible track record for shaping culture -- logical deduction from your role in the world works far better than performative inspiration.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: Alpha is quite simply daydreaming. Beta is productivity mode. And then gamma is your brain's intense focus. We generally spend too much time in beta in work.
moments.summary: Teams over-index on beta (productivity) mode and under-invest in alpha (daydreaming) and gamma (deep thinking), which is where breakthrough ideas and innovations emerge.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: If 25% of your year is spent in gamma and alpha, you're probably a lot better off than the teams who spend less than 25% of their year thinking deep, and being in this more daydreaming mode.
moments.summary: Teams should aim for roughly 25% of their time in deep thinking and daydreaming modes -- most teams spend less than 5%, severely limiting innovation.

podcasts.title: How embracing emotions will accelerate your career | Joe Hudson (executive coach, Art of Accomplishment)
podcasts.description: How embracing emotions will accelerate your career, covering measurement and analysis, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: It's the fact that they have emotions that they are not sitting, feeling, or expressing. Whatever emotion that you're trying to avoid, you are inviting into your life in exactly the way that you're trying to avoid it.
moments.summary: The emotions you avoid are the ones that dominate your life -- suppressed feelings manifest in the exact situations you are trying to prevent.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: What most people try to do is they try to stop it, and that doesn't work very well. I think the best way to work with the voice in the head is to pick an experiment every day and respond to the voice in the head in a new way every day. One of my favorite responses is, 'Oh, I see that you're really scared. Don't worry. I'm right here with you. I got you.'
moments.summary: Instead of fighting your inner critic, try responding to it differently each day -- acknowledging its fear with compassion rather than suppressing it.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: A lot of the people in my circles may have spent hundreds of millions of dollars to try to arrange a life that they enjoy, and it doesn't fucking work.
moments.summary: No amount of money can compensate for unprocessed emotions -- external arrangements cannot create internal satisfaction.

podcasts.title: Lessons from scaling Uber and Opendoor | Brian Tolkin (Head of Product at Opendoor, ex-Uber)
podcasts.description: Lessons from scaling Uber and Opendoor, covering product design, team leadership, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: Uber always has this mentality and Opendoor does two of the product operations, twin turbine jet plane where you can fly the plane on one engine for a little bit if you need to, but it's operating most efficiently and effectively if both are working together.
moments.summary: Product and operations function like twin turbine engines -- you can run on one briefly, but peak performance requires both working in concert.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: Gave a really deep understanding of how the business actually works. It's a pretty good foundation for then going on to say, okay, what do we actually want to build in a more scalable technology way.
moments.summary: Working in operations gives product leaders a deep understanding of how the business actually works, which becomes the foundation for building scalable technology.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: I've slept on the floor in China before launching uberPOOL, and when you reflect the stress onto your teams, everybody tenses out. It counterintuitively doesn't produce better outcomes.
moments.summary: Reflecting stress onto your team counterintuitively produces worse outcomes -- staying calm under pressure unlocks better performance from everyone.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to performance marketing | Timothy Davis (Shopify)
podcasts.description: Performance marketing, covering strategic decision-making, growth systems, and product design.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Hot take, paid is for everyone. If you look at the way each platform is doing, Google, you have to scroll pretty far down to get to an organic listing. Meta, it's almost a pay for play now.
moments.summary: Paid marketing is no longer optional -- organic reach on Google and Meta has shrunk so much that every company needs some performance marketing.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Instead of thinking about being on top of the page, and that's like ego marketing, I want to be number one. I want to be there all the time. It's about showing to the right person as often as possible.
moments.summary: Stop chasing top-of-page vanity positioning and focus on showing your ad to the right person as often as possible -- that is what drives actual conversions.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Way underestimating the power of creative. Do you remember Dollar Shave Club? There you go. You remember it. That was creative.
moments.summary: Marketers massively underestimate the power of creative in ad performance -- great creative like Dollar Shave Club's video creates lasting impact beyond any targeting strategy.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: I'm always forward thinking, backwards planning. 'Where do you want to be and ultimately how do you think you're going to get there?' Because say your goal is to be on all platforms... forward think, that's where you want to be. Now let's backwards plan.
moments.summary: Forward think your destination, then backwards plan the path -- this prevents overwhelm and creates clear milestones from current state to ambitious goals.

podcasts.title: 5 essential questions to craft a winning strategy | Roger Martin (author, advisor, speaker)
podcasts.description: 5 essential questions to craft a winning strategy, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Roger Martin
moments.text: What's taught now in business schools generally sucks. People aren't prepared educationally, and they sure don't get prepared for it in companies. It's intellectually challenging and it's emotionally intimidating.
moments.summary: Most people are bad at strategy because business schools teach it poorly and companies fail to develop strategic thinking -- it is both intellectually hard and emotionally scary.

moments.speaker: Roger Martin
moments.text: You have to have answers to five questions. What's your winning aspiration? Where to play? How can you win? What capabilities do you have to have that your competitors don't? And then, what enabling management systems do you have to put in place?
moments.summary: The strategy choice cascade requires answering five questions: winning aspiration, where to play, how to win, required capabilities, and enabling management systems.

moments.speaker: Roger Martin
moments.text: You have to be either differentiated or low cost, there's no way to protect yourself if you're not one of those two.
moments.summary: Strategy boils down to being either differentiated or low cost -- without one of those two positions, you have no sustainable competitive protection.

podcasts.title: Building a world-class data org | Jessica Lachs (VP of Analytics and Data Science at DoorDash)
podcasts.description: Building a world-class data org, covering team leadership, measurement and analysis, and product design.

moments.speaker: Jessica Lachs
moments.text: For me, analytics is a business impact driving function and not purely a service function, not just answering the why, but answering the, 'What do we do now that we know this?'
moments.summary: Analytics should be a business-impact-driving function, not just a service that answers 'why' -- the real value is in answering 'what do we do now?'

moments.speaker: Jessica Lachs
moments.text: Retention is a terrible thing to goal on. It's almost impossible to drive in a meaningful way in a short term. Ultimately, you want to find a short-term metric you can measure that drives a long-term output.
moments.summary: Do not goal on retention directly -- it is nearly impossible to move short-term. Instead find a measurable short-term metric that drives long-term retention.

moments.speaker: Jessica Lachs
moments.text: Yes, you are a data scientist, but your goal is to figure out what's happening. And if that means that you're going to pick up the phone and call customers, then that is what you're going to do to roll up your sleeves.
moments.summary: Data scientists should not hide behind dashboards -- if understanding the problem requires calling customers directly, that is exactly what they should do.

podcasts.title: Building product at Stripe: craft, metrics, and customer obsession | Jeff Weinstein (Product lead)
podcasts.description: Craft, metrics, and customer obsession, covering product design, engineering tradeoffs, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: The moment the customer felt compelled enough to go out of their way to talk about some problem, that's an unbelievable gift. I will leave a meeting to just get one message back to them.
moments.summary: Customer complaints are gifts -- Jeff Weinstein will leave meetings to respond because that moment of compelled feedback is priceless signal.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: What was the value that we're trying to produce for the customer and can we measure it from their perspective? And okay, how do you know you have product market fit? Charts that showcase things are going up into the right on one hand and then tweets on the other.
moments.summary: Measure value from the customer's perspective, and validate product-market fit with two signals: metrics going up and to the right, plus organic customer enthusiasm.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: We show up four to eight people total pretend to be some company with some outcome problem. Rule one is you do not work at Stripe and rule two is we're not here to solve any problems. This is just about practicing empathy for the customer.
moments.summary: Stripe's study groups practice pure customer empathy -- team members pretend to be customers with real problems, with explicit rules against jumping to solutions.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: Not having things be your idea I think is really powerful. I just talked to 50 customers who all yelled the same thing. Here they are in varieties of quotes and forms... Cool. What else could we want to do? The majority failure mode is we do nothing.
moments.summary: The best way to drive change at a big company is to let the customer be the source of ideas rather than claiming them as your own -- the real failure mode is doing nothing.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: I find proof of existence to be an incredibly powerful proof, rather than proof by theory or proof by debate. It's like, 'Look, we did it one time.'
moments.summary: Proof of existence beats proof by theory or debate -- showing one working example creates more momentum than any amount of argument.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: I remember writing the first draft and sending it to him because it was... and he wrote back, 'This doesn't sound like you yet.' The willingness to entrust a new person to provide their own perspective... that really spoke to me, and I rewrote it completely.
moments.summary: Patrick Collison told a one-month-old employee 'This doesn't sound like you yet' -- great leaders encourage people to bring their authentic perspective from day one.

moments.speaker: Jeff Weinstein
moments.text: You are one of the best people I've ever worked with at solving problems three through 100, but I need you stuck on problems one and two.
moments.summary: John Collison's gut-punch feedback: being great at solving problems 3 through 100 is a way of avoiding the hardest problems that matter most.

podcasts.title: Pattern Breakers: How to find a breakthrough startup idea | Mike Maples, Jr. (Founding Partner at Floodgate, ex-Product at Silicon Graphics)
podcasts.description: Finding a breakthrough startup idea, covering startup building, product design, and career development.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: The three are inflections, insights and then the founder future fit. Business is never a fair fight. What inflections let the founder do is wage asymmetric warfare on the present.
moments.summary: Breakthrough startup ideas require three elements: inflections, insights, and founder future fit, enabling asymmetric warfare against incumbents.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: The way inventions happen is people get their hands dirty, being awake to the possibility that secrets are there. If you're living in the future and you notice what's missing, your intuition about what to build is far more likely to be right.
moments.summary: Great founders discover startup secrets by living in the future and noticing what is missing rather than analyzing the present.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: The number one thing that I've learned is that you have to be willing to have your idea change, but you can't lose touch with the insight. 80% of our biggest returning investments came from a pivot.
moments.summary: Most top-returning venture investments came from pivots, so founders must stay flexible on the idea while holding onto the core insight.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: A startup never beats a big company by executing better than the big company. Startup wins over the big company because it proposes a radically different future, disorients the incumbent, and sort of chaotically moves people to that different future.
moments.summary: Startups beat incumbents not through superior execution but by proposing a radically different future that disorients the established players.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: The mistake that most companies make is they say, 'I've got this new pattern-breaking idea. It needs to be a third of my business in 24 months.' Bad strategy... Pattern-breaking products have different types of leadership, different types of go-to-market motion, different risk profiles.
moments.summary: Companies fail at internal innovation by applying established-business metrics and expectations to pattern-breaking products that require entirely different approaches.

moments.speaker: Mike Maples, Jr.
moments.text: It's a lonely existence, and it's okay if most people don't like your idea. It doesn't mean you're right, but it sure as heck doesn't mean you're wrong either. There's not enough people in this world willing to stick their neck out for things that they believe that they see before the rest of us.
moments.summary: Founders must embrace the loneliness of having an unpopular idea because consensus disapproval does not invalidate a breakthrough vision.

podcasts.title: Dylan Field live at Config: Intuition, simplicity, and the future of design
podcasts.description: Intuition, simplicity, and the future of design, covering product design, team leadership, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Dylan Field
moments.text: I think of intuition as a hypothesis generator. Intuition is not always right, but it gives you a starting point. And then you have to go validate it.
moments.summary: Intuition should be treated as a hypothesis generator that provides starting points requiring validation, not as a source of truth.

moments.speaker: Dylan Field
moments.text: We have this thing called a simplicity review. Every quarter we look at things that have gotten more complex and we ask, can we simplify this? It's a forcing function to keep things simple.
moments.summary: Figma runs quarterly simplicity reviews as a forcing function to combat the natural tendency of products to accumulate complexity.

moments.speaker: Dylan Field
moments.text: The future of PMs, I think, is going to be much more about taste and judgment and less about process. The PMs who are going to thrive are the ones who can look at something and say, this is good or this is not good.
moments.summary: The future of product management will prioritize taste and judgment over process, rewarding PMs who can evaluate quality directly.

moments.speaker: Dylan Field
moments.text: There's this tool called websim where you can just type in a URL that doesn't exist and it will generate the entire website for you. It's wild. I think it shows you where things are going.
moments.summary: AI tools like websim that generate entire websites from imaginary URLs hint at a future where creation barriers collapse dramatically.

moments.speaker: Dylan Field
moments.text: In the early days of Figma, everyone told us the browser would never be fast enough. We just kept pushing. Sometimes you have to have conviction in a technical bet that everyone else thinks is wrong.
moments.summary: Figma succeeded because its founders maintained conviction in the browser-based technical bet despite universal skepticism.

podcasts.title: The social radar: Y Combinator’s secret weapon | Jessica Livingston (co-founder of Y Combinator, author, podcast host)
podcasts.description: Y Combinator’s secret weapon, covering startup building, consumer products, and product design.

moments.speaker: Jessica Livingston
moments.text: My three co-founders were deeply technical, but I would look at other things about founders. All these little social cues.
moments.summary: Jessica Livingston brought a unique evaluative lens to YC by reading social cues and interpersonal dynamics that her technical co-founders missed.

moments.speaker: Jessica Livingston
moments.text: I would look at, do the co-founders get along? Are these people committed? And if a founder would get defensive, that was always a bad sign.
moments.summary: Key signals for evaluating founders include co-founder chemistry, commitment level, and whether they get defensive under questioning.

moments.speaker: Jessica Livingston
moments.text: He hated their idea, and Paul tried to get Brian and Joe and Nate to change it. But I remember specifically Joe brought out the cereal boxes, the Obama O's and Cap'n McCain's, and I just thought, oh my God, they're going to work hard to do whatever they can to make this company succeed.
moments.summary: The Airbnb founders' cereal box hustle convinced Jessica Livingston they had the resourcefulness and determination to succeed despite a questionable idea.

moments.speaker: Jessica Livingston
moments.text: The most important thing we did at YC was create an environment where founders could be honest about their problems. Most founders are performing for investors and the outside world. At YC dinners, they could let their guard down.
moments.summary: YC's most important contribution was creating a safe space where founders could drop the performance act and be honest about their struggles.

moments.speaker: Jessica Livingston
moments.text: One of the things I noticed is that the founders who struggled the most were the ones who were not resilient. It wasn't about the idea. It was about whether they could take a punch and keep going.
moments.summary: Founder resilience matters more than the initial idea because every startup journey involves absorbing blows and continuing forward.

podcasts.title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
podcasts.description: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity, covering product design, engineering tradeoffs, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Ami Vora
moments.text: I really enjoy being right and then it turns out in the working world, that did not serve me so great. I think the hard part is sublimating your ego a little bit and saying it's more important to get to the outcome than to be right.
moments.summary: Effective leadership requires sublimating the ego and prioritizing reaching the right outcome over personally being right.

moments.speaker: Ami Vora
moments.text: If we all agree that the feeling of something should be, I'm sitting in Dolores Park with my friends on a sunny Saturday, then people will just naturally build something that feels more consistent.
moments.summary: Aligning teams on the desired emotional experience of a product, using vivid metaphors, naturally drives design consistency.

moments.speaker: Ami Vora
moments.text: I think the hill climb metaphor is really useful. You might be on a local maximum, but if you want to get to the global maximum, you sometimes have to go downhill first. That's terrifying for a company.
moments.summary: Reaching a global maximum sometimes requires deliberately going downhill from a local maximum, which is terrifying but necessary for breakthrough growth.

moments.speaker: Ami Vora
moments.text: When I joined WhatsApp, one of the most striking things was the discipline of saying no. The product had like 50 engineers serving a billion users. That level of focus is extraordinary.
moments.summary: WhatsApp's extreme focus, with only 50 engineers serving a billion users, demonstrates the extraordinary power of disciplined prioritization.

moments.speaker: Ami Vora
moments.text: Curiosity is a superpower in product. When someone says something you disagree with, instead of arguing, try saying 'fascinating, tell me more.' You will learn things you never expected.
moments.summary: Responding to disagreements with genuine curiosity rather than argument unlocks unexpected learning and better outcomes.

podcasts.title: Hard-won lessons building 0 to 1 inside Atlassian | Tanguy Crusson (Head of Jira Product Discovery)
podcasts.description: Why large companies with so many advantages still fail at creating new products.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: The company has a tendency to over-invest. Startups have the benefit of starving, and so you need to create scarcity. What we try to do is remind everyone things are going to fail, let's not drag the rest of the company into it.
moments.summary: Creating artificial scarcity inside a large company replicates the startup advantage of constraint-driven focus and prevents over-investment in unproven bets.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: I needed the rest of the company to go away so we could get the autonomy to test the things that we needed, but it's not going to scale. That is not going to respect all design guidelines.
moments.summary: Zero-to-one teams inside large companies need radical autonomy, even if it means temporarily breaking corporate design guidelines and processes.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: This concept of incubate by working on something on the side, iterate until you've got it right and then integrate it back into the main product is something I would definitely do again.
moments.summary: The incubate-iterate-integrate framework lets teams build new products adjacent to the core product and merge them back once proven.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: The Safety Funnel is amazing. You basically put a hard stop and you limit the number of people who had bad experiences. And you do that for a while, up until you can prove it's amazing and then you invite more people.
moments.summary: The safety funnel approach limits early user exposure to prevent bad experiences, only scaling access once the product is proven.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: In my opinion everyone can be a product engineer. They just need to be exposed to the right user context. The right user context is 10 customers you know by name, you know their context, you know their problems.
moments.summary: Any engineer can become a product engineer when given direct exposure to a small set of customers they know by name and whose problems they deeply understand.

moments.speaker: Tanguy Crusson
moments.text: No one is going to read a research report that takes 30 minutes to read. Everyone is happy to watch a three-minute snippet with four customers talking about something.
moments.summary: Short customer video snippets shared weekly are far more effective at building internal momentum than lengthy research reports.

podcasts.title: The paths to power: How to grow your influence and advance your career | Jeffrey Pfeffer (author of 7 Rules of Power, professor at Stanford GSB)
podcasts.description: Grow your influence and advance your career, covering product design, career development, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: The reason why you should pay attention to this is because it leads to a lot of good things, salary, getting promoted, being happy in your career, being less stressed.
moments.summary: Developing power skills directly improves salary, promotions, career happiness, and stress levels.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: This is not about personality. These are skills they can be mastered.
moments.summary: Power and influence are learnable skills, not innate personality traits.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: You need to get over the idea that the world is a just world. The world is not a just world. Good performance by itself is not sufficient.
moments.summary: Relying solely on good performance is insufficient for career advancement because the world does not automatically reward merit.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: Network relentlessly. The data on this is overwhelming. The size of your network and the strength of your network ties predict your career outcomes more than almost anything else.
moments.summary: Network size and tie strength are among the strongest predictors of career outcomes, making relentless networking essential.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: Show up in a powerful fashion. Your body language, the way you speak, how much space you take up in a room. People make judgments about your power in milliseconds and those judgments stick.
moments.summary: People form lasting power judgments in milliseconds based on body language, speech patterns, and physical presence.

moments.speaker: Jeffrey Pfeffer
moments.text: Once you've acquired power, what you did to get there will be forgiven, forgotten, or both. The historical record is very clear on this.
moments.summary: History consistently shows that power acquisition methods are forgiven or forgotten once someone attains a powerful position.

podcasts.title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
podcasts.description: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member, covering startup building, product design, and career development.

moments.speaker: Uri Levine
moments.text: Fall in love, fall in love, fall in love, fall in love with the problem, and then actually what you're trying to do is engage everyone else to fall in love with the same problem, to go into this journey, into this path and follow your leadership there.
moments.summary: The most important startup lesson is falling in love with the problem and then enrolling everyone else to fall in love with it too.

moments.speaker: Uri Levine
moments.text: Every time that you hire someone new, mark your calendars for 30 days down the road and ask yourself one question, knowing what I know today, would I hire this person? If the answer is no, fire them immediately.
moments.summary: Set a 30-day calendar reminder after every hire to reassess the decision with fresh information, and act immediately if the answer is no.

moments.speaker: Uri Levine
moments.text: Most people are missing the most important slide of their presentation is the first slide. This slide is going to be presented for the longest period of time. This is the place that you're going to put your strongest point.
moments.summary: The first slide of a pitch deck gets the most viewing time and should contain the strongest point, yet most founders overlook its importance.

moments.speaker: Uri Levine
moments.text: The value is not about what the product does. The value is about the problem you solve. If you can describe the problem in a way that the person you're talking to says, 'oh my God, I have that problem,' you've won.
moments.summary: Product value comes from how well you articulate the problem being solved, not from describing product features.

moments.speaker: Uri Levine
moments.text: Product-market fit is a journey, not a destination. With Waze, we changed the product significantly three or four times before we got it right. The first version, nobody used. The second version, a few people used. The third version, people started using and telling their friends.
moments.summary: Product-market fit is an iterative journey, not a single moment; Waze went through three or four significant pivots before finding it.

podcasts.title: Inside Canva: Coaches not managers, giving away your Legos, and running profitably | Cameron Adams (co-founder and CPO)
podcasts.description: Coaches not managers, giving away your Legos, and running profitably, covering team leadership, product design, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Cameron Adams
moments.text: I run everyone through the culture of Canva. One of those sections is on giving away your Lego, finding joy in the other things of building a team, passing on your experience, helping other people do great writing or great product building or great engineering.
moments.summary: Canva's culture emphasizes 'giving away your Lego' by finding joy in building teams and passing on experience rather than hoarding ownership.

moments.speaker: Cameron Adams
moments.text: We don't really have managers, but everyone at Canva has a coach. They're constantly working with you to look at your skills, but also when it might be time to move on to the next level.
moments.summary: Canva replaces traditional managers with coaches who focus on skill development and career progression.

moments.speaker: Cameron Adams
moments.text: I didn't want to do product management like they did at Google, and that's because of the different cultures. I have seen product managers at other companies who are very independent of teams and that seems very weird to me. For us, product managers are really connected.
moments.summary: Canva rejected Google's product management model in favor of PMs who are deeply embedded and connected to their teams rather than operating independently.

moments.speaker: Cameron Adams
moments.text: We've been profitable from very early on. That changes everything about how you make decisions. You're not on someone else's timeline. You're on your own timeline.
moments.summary: Early profitability fundamentally changes decision-making by freeing a company from external timelines and investor pressure.

moments.speaker: Cameron Adams
moments.text: The biggest unlock for growth at Canva was making the product useful for teams, not just individuals. When one person brings Canva into a team, suddenly 10 people are using it.
moments.summary: Canva's biggest growth unlock was evolving from an individual tool to a team product, creating viral expansion within organizations.

podcasts.title: The surprising truth about what closes deals: Insights from 2.5m sales conversations | Matt Dixon (author of The Challenger Sale and The JOLT Effect)
podcasts.description: Insights from 2.5m sales conversations, covering B2B products, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Matt Dixon
moments.text: And that indecision stems from their fear of failure. Dialing up the FOMO backfires 87% of the time. They're not afraid of missing out, they're afraid of messing up.
moments.summary: Sales deals are lost primarily to buyer indecision driven by fear of failure, not competition; amplifying FOMO backfires 87% of the time.

moments.speaker: Matt Dixon
moments.text: JOLT them forward. The first thing is we've got to judge their level of indecision. The second thing is we've got to offer a recommendation. The third thing is we've got to get them to start trusting us and we call it limit the exploration, and the T is we've got to de-risk the deal.
moments.summary: The JOLT method for overcoming buyer indecision: Judge the indecision level, Offer a recommendation, Limit exploration, and Take risk off the table.

moments.speaker: Matt Dixon
moments.text: The Challenger Sale insight is that the best salespeople don't just build relationships. They actually push back on customers. They teach them something new about their business that reframes how they think about the problem.
moments.summary: Top salespeople challenge customers by teaching them something new about their business rather than just building relationships.

moments.speaker: Matt Dixon
moments.text: The best way to de-risk a deal is to take risk off the table proactively. Don't wait for the customer to ask. Offer a pilot, offer a guarantee, offer an easy way out. That confidence actually makes them more likely to buy.
moments.summary: Proactively offering risk-reduction options like pilots and guarantees signals confidence and paradoxically increases close rates.

moments.speaker: Matt Dixon
moments.text: When we looked at the highest performing reps, one thing stood out. They were very good at limiting the exploration. Too many choices paralyze people. The best reps would say, 'Based on what you've told me, here's what I'd recommend,' and just give them one option.
moments.summary: Top sales performers limit buyer exploration by offering a single confident recommendation rather than presenting multiple options that cause paralysis.

podcasts.title: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact | Bangaly Kaba (YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Instacart)
podcasts.description: Unorthodox frameworks for growing your product, career, and impact, covering team leadership, growth systems, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: What I call the anti-pattern of what we want to do. Someone says, 'Hey, you know what? This would be great to build.' And you go pull data to go justify why that would be great to build. Call that identify, justify, execute. First you have to really understand from first principles what is actually going on. So understand, identify, execute.
moments.summary: The growth anti-pattern is identify-justify-execute; the correct approach is understand-identify-execute, starting from first principles rather than retrofitting data to justify a preconceived idea.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: When you're in a hypergrowth product, it's really important to understand who your users are today and the persona of the user, what motivates them, why they're using it, but then also to understand who is the next user? Who is the user who could be using this product, but for some reason it doesn't work for them.
moments.summary: Hypergrowth products must continuously identify the adjacent user who could use the product but currently cannot, and build for them.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: We had to convince Kevin and Mikey that it was actually not the right thing to do to prioritize celebrities to everybody because we were basically biting our nose to spank our face. The regular person wasn't having a great experience.
moments.summary: Instagram's connections pivot from prioritizing celebrity follows to friend connections dramatically improved retention by ensuring regular users felt valued.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: Usually it's somewhere in the onboarding to habit-building experience. What does it take for someone to actually understand the value, that first moment, that first aha moment in the product? And a lot of teams, it's shocking how many teams don't really understand what that moment is for them.
moments.summary: Most growth opportunities lie in onboarding and habit formation, yet a shocking number of teams do not understand their product's aha moment.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: We were losing 10, 12 million people a year from what we called account access churn. So we worked on this problem... just being really thoughtful around what is actually the core job that people are trying to do.
moments.summary: Instagram recovered millions of churned users per year by fixing account access issues, proving that simple login friction can drive massive hidden churn.

moments.speaker: Bangaly Kaba
moments.text: Another part that goes unspoken, still critical to this day was the celebrity partnerships was critical, because basically they had this wonderful partnerships team that basically took Instagram and taught celebrities how to use it, how to make it work for them, how to tell their own story.
moments.summary: Instagram's largely unspoken early growth driver was a dedicated celebrity partnerships team that taught stars to use the platform as a personal branding tool.

podcasts.title: Why not asking for what you want is holding you back | Kenneth Berger (exec coach, first PM at Slack)
podcasts.description: Kenneth Berger coaches startup leaders on how to prevent burnout, advocate for their desired lifestyle, and make a meaningful impact on the world. He’s spent more than 20 years in the tech industry, is a former founder backed by top investors, and was the first product manager at Slack.

moments.speaker: Kenneth Berger
moments.text: The core idea is ask for what you want. Turns out when you actually ask for what you want out loud, you're much more likely to get it.
moments.summary: Simply articulating what you want out loud dramatically increases the likelihood of getting it, yet most people avoid doing so.

moments.speaker: Kenneth Berger
moments.text: For me, the impact was about making this work sustainable so that we're not burning out or selling out, but actually able to pursue these hard goals that we have in startups.
moments.summary: Sustainable pursuit of hard startup goals requires intentionally designing against burnout rather than treating it as an inevitable cost.

moments.speaker: Kenneth Berger
moments.text: The biggest sign is when you notice a gap between what you want and what you're asking for. You might want a promotion, but you're not saying it. You might want more resources, but you're hoping someone notices.
moments.summary: The gap between what you want and what you explicitly ask for is the primary thing holding most people back in their careers.

moments.speaker: Kenneth Berger
moments.text: People think asking for what they want is selfish. But actually, when you're clear about what you need, you make it easier for everyone around you. Ambiguity is what creates conflict.
moments.summary: Clearly stating your needs is not selfish but actually reduces conflict by eliminating the ambiguity that creates workplace tension.

moments.speaker: Kenneth Berger
moments.text: At Slack, one of the things I learned was that the best product decisions came from people who were willing to say what they actually thought, even when it was uncomfortable. The culture rewarded directness.
moments.summary: Slack's best product decisions came from a culture that rewarded directness and people willing to voice uncomfortable truths.

podcasts.title: Be fundamentally different, not incrementally better | Jag Duggal (Nubank, Facebook, Google, Quantcast)
podcasts.description: Be fundamentally different, not incrementally better, covering product design, go-to-market execution, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Jag Duggal
moments.text: We're not trying to be incrementally better, we are trying to be fundamentally different. We want our customers to love us fanatically.
moments.summary: Nubank's core strategy is to be fundamentally different rather than incrementally better, aiming for fanatical customer love.

moments.speaker: Jag Duggal
moments.text: We built a lending product, we built an investment product, we built an insurance product, we built a series of small business products. We rarely scale a project until we know the Sean Ellis score hit a threshold that we find really compelling.
moments.summary: Nubank uses the Sean Ellis score as a gate before scaling any new product line, ensuring strong product-market fit before investment.

moments.speaker: Jag Duggal
moments.text: Kevin Systrom in the early days of Instagram, I heard him say it at a conference. We may not be right, but at least we are clear. Even if your strategy isn't right, you have a very clear idea of what was supposed to be happening.
moments.summary: Strategic clarity matters more than strategic correctness because a clear wrong strategy can be tested and corrected while an ambiguous one cannot.

moments.speaker: Jag Duggal
moments.text: One of the things we do at Nubank is we ask every product team to articulate what we call a 'magic moment.' What is the moment when the customer goes from 'this is interesting' to 'I love this'?
moments.summary: Nubank requires every product team to define their 'magic moment' when customers cross from interest to love.

moments.speaker: Jag Duggal
moments.text: 80% to 90% of our growth is through word of mouth. That only happens if your product is fundamentally different. No one tells their friends about something that's slightly better. They tell their friends about something that blew their mind.
moments.summary: Nubank's 80-90% word-of-mouth growth proves that viral organic growth only comes from products that are fundamentally different, not slightly better.

podcasts.title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
podcasts.description: A framework for PM skill development, covering team leadership, strategic decision-making, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
moments.text: I created a career growth framework for product managers, which comprises of three things. What you produce, what you bring to the table, and what's your operating model.
moments.summary: PM career growth depends on three pillars: what you produce (artifacts), what you bring to the table (skills), and your operating model (how you work).

moments.speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
moments.text: Can you show me your last PRD? Can you show me the last product note that you sent? Can you show me the product strategy doc? You must have that impact through the artifacts that you work on.
moments.summary: PM impact is demonstrated through tangible artifacts like PRDs and strategy docs, and early-career PMs should focus on producing them consistently.

moments.speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
moments.text: How you view change, whether you are focusing on things you control, and third is how you see yourself. The moment you are able to correct those stories, you may be back on the growth path again.
moments.summary: Three mindset blocks impede career growth: how you view change, whether you focus on controllable factors, and your self-narrative.

moments.speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
moments.text: At Gojek, we had to build products for a market where people were coming online for the first time. You can't assume anything about user behavior. You have to go watch them use the product in their environment.
moments.summary: Building products for emerging markets where users are coming online for the first time requires directly observing users in their real environment.

moments.speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
moments.text: The operating model is actually the most underrated part. How do you make decisions? How do you communicate? How do you handle ambiguity? These are the things that separate senior PMs from everyone else.
moments.summary: A PM's operating model, covering decision-making, communication, and ambiguity handling, is the most underrated differentiator for senior-level growth.

podcasts.title: Business strategy with Hamilton Helmer (author of 7 Powers)
podcasts.description: Business strategy with Hamilton Helmer (author of 7 Powers), covering strategic decision-making, startup building, and product design.

moments.speaker: Hamilton Helmer
moments.text: Power requires a benefit and a barrier, so he's taking care of the benefit part by saying a castle, you have to have a pretty good understanding of why it's a castle and not a shack.
moments.summary: True business power requires both a benefit (the castle) and a barrier (the moat); most companies have only one or neither.

moments.speaker: Hamilton Helmer
moments.text: You're on a treadmill and if you stop running on that treadmill, you get creamed, but it's not power. The things that drive operational excellence can be mimicked.
moments.summary: Operational excellence is a treadmill, not a power; anything competitors can mimic does not constitute a durable strategic advantage.

moments.speaker: Hamilton Helmer
moments.text: There's a thing called power progression. There are times when certain types of power are available. The path to power is where the rubber meets the road.
moments.summary: Different types of strategic power become available at different company stages, and timing the pursuit of power correctly is critical.

moments.speaker: Hamilton Helmer
moments.text: Network effects are the most powerful of the seven powers. If you have a product where every incremental user makes the product more valuable for every other user, that's an extraordinarily durable position.
moments.summary: Network effects are the strongest of the seven powers because each new user increases value for all existing users, creating extraordinary durability.

moments.speaker: Hamilton Helmer
moments.text: Counter-positioning is when a newcomer adopts a new, superior business model that the incumbent can't adopt because it would damage their existing business. This is what Netflix did to Blockbuster.
moments.summary: Counter-positioning occurs when a newcomer adopts a superior business model that incumbents cannot copy without damaging their existing business.

podcasts.title: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke (author of “Thinking in Bets” and “Quit,” former pro poker player)
podcasts.description: This will make you a better decision-maker, covering product design, practical product lessons, and career development.

moments.speaker: Annie Duke
moments.text: It's so incredibly necessary in improving decision quality to take what's implicit and make it explicit. It's not that intuition is crap, your intuition is sometimes right. If you don't make it explicit, then you don't get to find out when it's wrong.
moments.summary: Making implicit beliefs explicit is essential for improving decisions because it is the only way to discover when your intuition is wrong.

moments.speaker: Annie Duke
moments.text: People generally think the purpose of a meeting is for three things, discover, discuss, decide. The only thing that's ever supposed to happen in a meeting is the discussion part.
moments.summary: Meetings should only be for discussion; discovery and decisions should happen before and after the meeting, respectively.

moments.speaker: Annie Duke
moments.text: So a pre-mortem, it's great only if you set up kill criteria. Commit to actions that you're going to take if you see those signals.
moments.summary: Pre-mortems are only effective when paired with predefined kill criteria that commit teams to specific actions upon seeing warning signals.

moments.speaker: Annie Duke
moments.text: When you're making a decision where the outcome is very long-term, you need to focus on the quality of the process, not the quality of the outcome. Because the outcome is going to be affected by so many things you can't control.
moments.summary: For long-term decisions, evaluate the quality of the decision process rather than the outcome since too many uncontrollable variables affect results.

moments.speaker: Annie Duke
moments.text: The most important thing about quitting is to set your criteria in advance. Decide when you will quit before you start. Once you're in it, your sunk cost bias will keep you going way too long.
moments.summary: Setting quit criteria before starting a project is essential because sunk cost bias makes it nearly impossible to make rational quit decisions in the moment.

podcasts.title: Twitter’s former Head of Product opens up: being fired, meeting Elon, changing stagnant culture, building consumer product, more | Kayvon Beykpour
podcasts.description: Being fired, meeting Elon, changing stagnant culture, building consumer product, more, covering team leadership, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
moments.text: We wanted to change the lack of ambition, the lack of creativity, the lack of customers feeling that the product had changed at all.
moments.summary: Kayvon's mission at Twitter was to cure a stagnant culture where customers felt the product never changed and the team lacked ambition.

moments.speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
moments.text: The sacred cows are like their own roadmap. What are all the things that you think we're not allowed to change? Let's start there.
moments.summary: Treating sacred cows as a roadmap for innovation by starting with the things everyone assumes cannot be changed.

moments.speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
moments.text: I was like, 'I might flame out completely, but Hell if I don't try.'
moments.summary: Taking on a massive culture-change challenge requires accepting the possibility of total failure while refusing not to try.

moments.speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
moments.text: The number one thing that changed Twitter's shipping velocity was just getting people to believe they were allowed to ship. The culture had become so risk-averse that people self-censored their own ambition.
moments.summary: Twitter's biggest shipping blocker was not technical but cultural: people self-censored their ambition due to extreme risk aversion.

moments.speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
moments.text: Live video taught me that you can build a product that people don't ask for and they don't know they want, and it can become something they can't live without. Periscope was that for me.
moments.summary: Products people never asked for can become indispensable, as Periscope proved that live video was a latent need no one could articulate in advance.

podcasts.title: How to build deeper, more robust relationships | Carole Robin (Stanford GSB professor, “Touchy Feely”)
podcasts.description: Building deeper, more robust relationships, covering product design, team leadership, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Carole Robin
moments.text: I feel that you don't care and I feel you're being insensitive are not feelings, and that's where we make our biggest mistakes when it comes to feedback.
moments.summary: Statements like 'I feel you don't care' disguise judgments as feelings, and this confusion is the root of most feedback failures.

moments.speaker: Carole Robin
moments.text: Questions that start with what, when, where, how. Stay away from why.
moments.summary: Asking 'why' triggers defensiveness; effective feedback uses questions starting with what, when, where, and how.

moments.speaker: Carole Robin
moments.text: We don't understand that we are only privy to two out of the three, so I know what's going on for me and I know what I did. I have no idea what happened on your end.
moments.summary: The three realities framework reveals we only see our own intent and our actions, but are blind to the other person's experience.

moments.speaker: Carole Robin
moments.text: Anger is a secondary emotion. Really what's going on is you're afraid or you're hurt.
moments.summary: Anger is always a secondary emotion masking fear or hurt, and addressing the root feeling transforms difficult conversations.

moments.speaker: Carole Robin
moments.text: The most robust relationships are the ones that have been through conflict and survived. If you've never had a hard conversation with someone, you don't actually have a deep relationship. You have a pleasant one.
moments.summary: Deep relationships are forged through survived conflict, not pleasant avoidance; never having a hard conversation signals superficiality.

podcasts.title: Vision, conviction, and hype: How to build 0 to 1 inside a company | Mihika Kapoor (Product at Figma)
podcasts.description: Building 0 to 1 inside a company, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: My take is that your scope is the world. Nothing should ever perceive as being out of bounds.
moments.summary: The best PMs treat nothing as out of bounds, viewing their scope as the entire world rather than a narrowly defined product area.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: We lean heavily into designing and prototyping even before a project gets a green light. If you and your team do your job correctly, what does the world look like?
moments.summary: Building prototypes and designs before getting formal project approval creates compelling visions that make approval much easier to obtain.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: We have this concept called Maker Week, which is our internal hackathon, giving people the breathing space to see ahead into the horizon and be wildly ambitious.
moments.summary: Figma's Maker Week hackathon gives teams breathing space to think ambitiously beyond day-to-day work and explore future possibilities.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: The way I think about hype is that it's a tool. You need internal hype to get resources and attention. But you have to back it up with substance. Hype without substance erodes trust very quickly.
moments.summary: Internal hype is a necessary tool for securing resources and attention, but it must be backed by substance or it quickly erodes trust.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: I think the best PMs are unreasonable. Not in a bad way, but they refuse to accept the constraints that everyone else accepts. They ask, what if we just didn't have that constraint?
moments.summary: The best PMs are productively unreasonable, questioning accepted constraints and asking what would be possible without them.

moments.speaker: Mihika Kapoor
moments.text: One of the biggest shortcomings of AI is that it's optimized for the demo or optimized for the tweet. It's not really useful to enter a prompt and get an output that you can't interact with.
moments.summary: Most AI products fail by optimizing for demo-worthy outputs rather than giving users the ability to interact with and manipulate results.

podcasts.title: Lessons from 1,000+ YC startups: Resilience, tar pit ideas, pivoting, more | Dalton Caldwell (Y Combinator, Managing Director)
podcasts.description: Resilience, tar pit ideas, pivoting, more, covering startup building, product design, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Dalton Caldwell
moments.text: One of my mantras is just don't die. Being coached and being reminded of the fundamentals and basics puts you in the right mindset.
moments.summary: The simplest and most important startup mantra is 'just don't die,' keeping founders focused on fundamentals over everything else.

moments.speaker: Dalton Caldwell
moments.text: Seems like an unsolved problem. You'll get all this positive feedback from the world and people have been starting that startup since the '90s.
moments.summary: Tarpit ideas appear as unsolved problems with encouraging market feedback, but they have been trapping founders for decades without success.

moments.speaker: Dalton Caldwell
moments.text: A good pivot is like going home. It's warmer, it's closer to something that you're an expert at.
moments.summary: The best pivots feel like coming home because they move founders closer to their domain expertise and authentic interests.

moments.speaker: Dalton Caldwell
moments.text: We're trying to mix up some of the information diet about what kind of ideas people might be contemplating they are currently.
moments.summary: YC's request for startups is designed to broaden the idea diet of prospective founders beyond the narrow set of concepts they naturally encounter.

moments.speaker: Dalton Caldwell
moments.text: The founders who do best at YC are the ones who are resilient. They take the feedback, they iterate quickly, and they don't get emotionally attached to any single version of the product. They're attached to the problem.
moments.summary: Top YC founders are resilient, iterate rapidly on feedback, and remain attached to the problem rather than any specific product iteration.

podcasts.title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
podcasts.description: A framework for finding product-market fit, covering startup building, strategic decision-making, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: Finding product-market fit is the single most important thing that your startup does in the first three years, and it's just underexplored and it's just underexplained as a topic.
moments.summary: Product-market fit is the single most important startup milestone yet remains surprisingly underexplored and poorly explained.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: We've published dozens of articles on the First Round Review, and we have found a very consistent set of patterns, demand satisfaction, and efficiency. But the interesting thing is that you don't go for all three of them from the very beginning.
moments.summary: Product-market fit follows three sequential patterns: demand, satisfaction, and efficiency, which should not all be pursued simultaneously.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: You've got the persona, the problem, the promise, and the product. Lattice kept the first one but changed the others. Vanta changed all four.
moments.summary: When stuck on product-market fit, the four Ps framework (persona, problem, promise, product) helps identify which elements to change.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: Roughly, 60% are never going to get past L2.
moments.summary: About 60% of startups never progress past the second level of product-market fit, making it a critical inflection point.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: The best signal that you have nascent product-market fit is when customers start pulling the product from you. You don't have to push it anymore. They're asking you for it. That's a qualitative signal that data won't show you.
moments.summary: The strongest early signal of product-market fit is when customers start pulling the product from you instead of you pushing it.

moments.speaker: Todd Jackson
moments.text: At the developing stage, the biggest mistake I see is premature scaling. You have a few happy customers and you think you should hire a sales team and start marketing. But you haven't really nailed what makes those customers happy.
moments.summary: The most common product-market fit mistake is premature scaling before deeply understanding what makes early customers successful.

podcasts.title: Bending the universe in your favor | Claire Vo (LaunchDarkly, Color, Optimizely, ChatPRD)
podcasts.description: Bending the universe in your favor, covering career development, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Claire Vo
moments.text: People often think that I get hired into later stage companies because I'm supposed to teach them how to operate like a big company, and in fact, I say I'm hired to remind them they can operate like a startup.
moments.summary: Experienced operators are hired into later-stage companies not to add process but to remind them they can still operate with startup speed and scrappiness.

moments.speaker: Claire Vo
moments.text: I communicate to my leaders that my expectation is they bring in the clock speed one click faster. If you think something needs to be done this year, it needs to be done this half.
moments.summary: A simple leadership technique for increasing velocity: tell teams to compress every timeline by one increment.

moments.speaker: Claire Vo
moments.text: I'm using CPTO for short code of running product and engineering design functionally together. There should be no debates over what's best for product or what's best for engineering, what's best for design speed. What is best for the organization?
moments.summary: The CPTO model combines product, engineering, and design under one leader, eliminating cross-functional debates in favor of organizational-level optimization.

moments.speaker: Claire Vo
moments.text: Is it going to eliminate PMs next year? Probably not. Are the skills required going to shift? Yes. Could they shift much faster than we all anticipate? Probably.
moments.summary: AI will not eliminate PMs soon but will shift required skills faster than most people anticipate.

moments.speaker: Claire Vo
moments.text: The thing about bending the universe in your favor is it's not about luck. It's about creating as many surface areas for good things to happen as possible. Say yes to things, be visible, put yourself out there, and over time, the opportunities compound.
moments.summary: Creating career luck is about maximizing surface area for serendipity through visibility, saying yes, and letting opportunities compound over time.

podcasts.title: Zigging vs. zagging: How HubSpot built a $30B company | Dharmesh Shah (co-founder/CTO)
podcasts.description: How HubSpot built a $30B company, covering product design, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Dharmesh Shah
moments.text: Some of the best startup advice I've heard is startups should focus on one thing and be really, really exceptionally world-class at that one thing. And one of our early zigs is we are going to do exactly the opposite of that.
moments.summary: HubSpot deliberately defied the conventional startup wisdom of focusing on one thing, choosing instead to build a broad platform.

moments.speaker: Dharmesh Shah
moments.text: I could become passively okay at management with some training, with some coaching. I don't want to spend any years of my life becoming passively okay at something.
moments.summary: Dharmesh chose to have zero direct reports at HubSpot because he'd rather invest time in his strengths than become mediocre at management.

moments.speaker: Dharmesh Shah
moments.text: My co-founder and I were having one of our founders meetings and he said, 'Oh, Dharmesh, I hear this culture thing is really important. By the way, can you go do that?' I'm like, 'Okay. Brian, of all the people in all the company, is like, I am the worst possible person.' It's not that I don't like people, I just don't like being around them a whole lot.
moments.summary: Despite being self-described as not a people person, Dharmesh was tasked with defining HubSpot's culture, leading to their famous culture code.

podcasts.title: How to speak more confidently and persuasively | Matt Abrahams (professor, podcast host, author, speaker)
podcasts.description: Speak more confidently and persuasively, covering career development, operator lessons, and product design.

moments.speaker: Matt Abrahams
moments.text: You actually have to prepare to be spontaneous. And that's counterintuitive, but when you think about it in athletics or jazz music, it's like of course you would prepare and practice.
moments.summary: Being good at spontaneous speaking requires deliberate preparation, just like athletes and jazz musicians practice for improvisation.

moments.speaker: Matt Abrahams
moments.text: Strive for connection over perfection by daring to be dull. Just answer the question. Just give the feedback. Just be engaged in the small talk. By doing that, you dial down the volume of self-evaluation, freeing up resources that can be used to really help you succeed.
moments.summary: Reducing speaking anxiety means aiming for connection rather than perfection, which frees up mental energy for genuine engagement.

moments.speaker: Matt Abrahams
moments.text: Visualization is a really useful technique and you see yourself not just in the moment of speaking, but getting up to the stage, seeing it being well received, thinking about how you step off the stage. We see athletes do this kind of thing all the time, and there's good research to say that this desensitizes people.
moments.summary: Visualizing the entire arc of a speaking experience, not just the moment itself, helps desensitize anxiety the same way athletes use mental rehearsal.

podcasts.title: Kunal Shah on winning in India, second-order thinking, the philosophy of startups, and more
podcasts.description: Winning in India, second-order thinking, the philosophy of startups, and more, covering product design, startup building, and pricing decisions.

moments.speaker: Kunal Shah
moments.text: Every time you see that the product efficiency delta is greater than or equal to four, three things happen. It is irreversible. Second is that you have a very high tolerance for it to fail. And the third thing is what I call the UBP, 'Unique Brag-worthy Proposition.' Every time humans unlock a Delta 4 product or service, they cannot stop talking or sharing about it.
moments.summary: The Delta 4 framework states that when a new product is 4+ points more efficient than the old way on a 1-10 scale, it becomes irreversible, failure-tolerant, and organically viral.

moments.speaker: Kunal Shah
moments.text: A lot of CEOs have done well because they follow the 'dharma' of the founders quite well. They have not diluted the dharma of the founders who have started these companies, and managed to sustain that. Maintaining 'dharma', where I will say that 'These principles were given to me and I'm going to sustain this and make it even bigger,' comes from the humility of saying that there is something that was built with some pure form factor.
moments.summary: Indian-born CEOs succeed at major tech companies by maintaining the founder's original vision rather than imposing their own identity, drawing from a cultural archetype of stewardship.

moments.speaker: Kunal Shah
moments.text: No Indian has ever been paid an hourly salary in their entire life. So when you're not paid an hourly salary, the concept of time is not the same. And when the concept of time is not the same, paying for time is hard to do. Many Indian languages do not have a word for 'efficiency.'
moments.summary: The absence of hourly wages in India fundamentally changes how people value time, which has profound implications for product pricing and efficiency-based products.

moments.speaker: Kunal Shah
moments.text: When we give the highest status to risk-takers, the country appreciates it because ultimately we're all looking for markers of social validation. In Portugal, in the church normally only the Royalty are allowed to be buried or cremated. There is only one more category that is allowed -- the explorers.
moments.summary: Societies that elevate risk-takers to the highest social status produce more entrepreneurs, illustrated by Portugal honoring explorers alongside royalty.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to PR | Emilie Gerber (founder of Six Eastern)
podcasts.description: PR, covering B2B products, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Emilie Gerber
