moments.text: No one ever gets this tactical in PR. There's just so much theoretical conversation and examining the big blunders, but at the end of the day, if you're a startup that wants to get coverage for your company, you need to know actually the steps to take to do it.
moments.summary: Most PR advice is too theoretical; startups need concrete, tactical steps to actually get press coverage.

moments.speaker: Emilie Gerber
moments.text: You can get so in the weeds with your own messaging that you want to set up this massive problem statement, you want to make it a huge trend story, but if you're very straightforward and you're pattern matching, it's generally actually going to work.
moments.summary: Startup PR pitches work better when they are straightforward and pattern-match to what journalists already cover, rather than overcomplicating the story.

moments.speaker: Emilie Gerber
moments.text: I want to read one to you and then I want to see if you can tell me what this company does.
moments.summary: Emilie tests PR pitches by reading them aloud to see if the listener can even identify what the company does, a simple but revealing litmus test.

podcasts.title: The happiness and pain of product management | Noam Lovinsky (Grammarly, Facebook, YouTube, Thumbtack)
podcasts.description: The happiness and pain of product management, covering team leadership, career development, and product design.

moments.speaker: Noam Lovinsky
moments.text: There were many times where Google leadership reconsidered the acquisition and, 'Should we sell YouTube?' if you can believe it or not.
moments.summary: Google leadership seriously considered selling YouTube multiple times after the acquisition, revealing how uncertain even the most successful tech deals can be.

moments.speaker: Noam Lovinsky
moments.text: You're thinking on a different time horizon. If you're a large organization and you do some performance management process twice a year and you're 0 to 1 incubator, you've already killed it. It's the wrong incentive.
moments.summary: Large companies kill internal 0-to-1 incubation efforts by applying standard performance review cycles that are too short for innovation timelines.

moments.speaker: Noam Lovinsky
moments.text: I remember in a board meeting, the new model really started to show legs and one of the board members, Brian Schreier at Sequoia, said it was the prettiest smile graph that he had ever seen.
moments.summary: Thumbtack's turnaround from failure to success produced what a Sequoia board member called the prettiest smile graph he'd ever seen.

podcasts.title: Inside TikTok: Culture, strategy, monetization, and more | Ray Cao (Global Head of Monetization Product Strategy and Operations)
podcasts.description: Culture, strategy, monetization, and more, covering strategic decision-making, team leadership, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Ray Cao
moments.text: The number one thing is context, no control. That's the reason why we're always encouraging people to see themselves as a business owner.
moments.summary: TikTok's core operating principle is 'context, no control' -- giving employees full information and letting them make decisions like business owners.

moments.speaker: Ray Cao
moments.text: We always have this mentality we are a startup, we're a young company, we're always hungry for growth. And a very wacky way is like, 'How can I run my second half of my marathon faster than the first half?'
moments.summary: TikTok maintains a startup mentality by challenging teams to run the second half of the marathon faster than the first.

moments.speaker: Ray Cao
moments.text: How do you actually solve the puzzle by connecting all the dots together? Just like how I see some of my friends, their kids playing Legos, if you don't really see the full picture, you won't be able to make the Lego as one thing at the end of the day. You have to see the other pieces.
moments.summary: TikTok's context-driven approach means giving people the full picture so they can connect the dots like assembling Lego with the box cover visible.

podcasts.title: Making Meta | Andrew ‘Boz’ Bosworth (CTO)
podcasts.description: Making Meta, covering startup building, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Andrew 'Boz' Bosworth
moments.text: I didn't sleep for more than four hours at a time. I'd wake up every four hours and check the report and see if anyone was attacking the site. They don't tell you about that stuff in the movies.
moments.summary: As the 10th engineer at Facebook, Boz woke up every four hours to check for site attacks, illustrating the unglamorous reality behind startup success stories.

moments.speaker: Andrew 'Boz' Bosworth
moments.text: I don't want to take away from the romanticism of it. It's just that most often, we hear those romantic stories from the successes. It's a healthy thing for people to want to throw themselves into something and take that risk, but it is not glamorous at the time.
moments.summary: Startup romanticization comes from survivorship bias; the actual experience of building something from scratch is grueling, not glamorous.

moments.speaker: Andrew 'Boz' Bosworth
moments.text: Now, newsfeed was an easier case than people suspect. Everyone was outraged at the same time as they immediately doubled their usage of the product.
moments.summary: Despite massive public outrage when Facebook launched News Feed, usage immediately doubled, proving that user behavior matters more than user complaints.

podcasts.title: How to discover your superpowers, own your story, and unlock personal growth | Donna Lichaw (author of The Leader’s Journey)
podcasts.description: Discover your superpowers, own your story, and unlock personal growth, covering team leadership, product design, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Donna Lichaw
moments.text: When superheroes discover what their superpowers actually are, they wreak havoc and they make a mess, and it's uncomfortable. And even Superman tries to get rid of his superpowers. It's hard to know what you're really great at.
moments.summary: Discovering your strengths is inherently uncomfortable, much like superheroes initially struggling with and even rejecting their powers.

moments.speaker: Donna Lichaw
moments.text: Pull your superpowers out of your stories from your past, your present, and then eventually figure out how to apply them and transpose them to your future.
moments.summary: You can identify your strengths by mining patterns from your personal stories across past and present experiences.

moments.speaker: Donna Lichaw
moments.text: The most effective stories are the ones that we tell ourselves. They may or may not be true; our brain doesn't know the difference. Once you can really understand that, you may as well leverage it to be that hero.
moments.summary: Since our brains cannot distinguish between true and constructed self-narratives, we should deliberately craft empowering stories about ourselves.

podcasts.title: How Netflix builds a culture of excellence | Elizabeth Stone (CTO)
podcasts.description: How Netflix builds a culture of excellence, covering team leadership, engineering tradeoffs, and product design.

moments.speaker: Elizabeth Stone
moments.text: We can't really have any of the other aspects of the culture, including candor, learning, seeking excellence in improvement, freedom and responsibility if you don't start with high talent density. And in order to do that, you have to really hold yourself to a lot of stuff that doesn't feel like natural human behavior.
moments.summary: Netflix's entire culture of candor and freedom depends first on high talent density, which requires holding people to standards that feel unnatural.

moments.speaker: Elizabeth Stone
moments.text: It was with a belief that there could be a different approach to building a company that would make it a place that people thrived in and loved being and would feel different than other places, both in the quality of that talent density, but even more importantly, the excellence and the outcomes. And that that's where people would derive a lot of sense of fulfillment.
moments.summary: Netflix was built on Reed Hastings's belief that people derive fulfillment not from perks but from excellence and being surrounded by exceptional colleagues.

podcasts.title: Making time for what matters | Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky (authors of Sprint and Make Time, co-founders of Character Capital)
podcasts.description: Making time for what matters, covering product design, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Jake Knapp
moments.text: It's not really about productivity, it's not about time management. It's really just about, look, at any given day, we're lucky if we can have one great moment where we have our peak attention and we use it well. The notion with the highlight is imagine it's the end of the day if someone asks you, 'What was the highlight of your day,' what would you say? That's the anchor of everything.
moments.summary: The Make Time framework centers on choosing one daily highlight as an anchor, since we're lucky to get even one moment of peak attention per day.

moments.speaker: Jake Knapp
moments.text: Things can sometimes be a mess outside of that, and you still feel really good about your days. You still feel really good about the way you're spending your energy.
moments.summary: Even when everything else is chaotic, having one intentional highlight each day is enough to feel good about how you spent your time.

moments.speaker: Jake Knapp
moments.text: It's not going to happen every day, but if we have some intention around it, it can happen more often than not.
moments.summary: Consistent intentionality around focus does not guarantee perfection but meaningfully increases the frequency of productive, fulfilling days.

podcasts.title: Inside OpenAI | Logan Kilpatrick (head of developer relations)
podcasts.description: Inside OpenAI, covering AI product work, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Logan Kilpatrick
moments.text: Finding people who are high agency and work with urgency, if I was hiring five people today, those are some of the top two characteristics that I would look for in people because you can take on the world if you have people who have high agency and not needing to get 50 people's different consensus.
moments.summary: At OpenAI, the two most valued hiring traits are high agency and urgency, enabling people to solve problems without waiting for consensus.

moments.speaker: Logan Kilpatrick
moments.text: They hear something from our customers about a challenge that they're having, and they're already pushing on what the solution for them is and not waiting for all the other things to happen. People just go and do it and solve the problem, and I love that.
moments.summary: The best people at OpenAI hear a customer problem and immediately start building the solution rather than waiting for process or permission.

podcasts.title: Lessons from Atlassian: Launching new products, getting buy-in, and staying ahead of the competition | Megan Cook (head of product, Jira)
podcasts.description: Launching new products, getting buy-in, and staying ahead of the competition, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and product design.

moments.speaker: Megan Cook
moments.text: What we put into place is something we call Fight Club. It's 30 minutes every week, and it's just for myself, my engineering, and my design leader; and we get together, and we know that we're going there to have a conflict.
moments.summary: Jira's product leadership team holds a weekly 30-minute 'Fight Club' specifically dedicated to surfacing and resolving disagreements early.

moments.speaker: Megan Cook
moments.text: I think often when there's difficult conversations, or those conflicts come up, you can put them off until they become much bigger. Or if somebody is conflict adverse, they can try to avoid having it at all. But by having a specific slot of time in your week for something like that, then you're sort of in that mindset.
moments.summary: Scheduling a regular time for conflict prevents disagreements from festering and normalizes healthy debate by putting everyone in the right mindset.

moments.speaker: Megan Cook
moments.text: The relationship we all have is so much better because we get on top of these things early.
moments.summary: Proactively addressing conflicts through structured sessions strengthens professional relationships rather than weakening them.

podcasts.title: Managing nerves, anxiety, and burnout | Jonny Miller (Nervous System Mastery)
podcasts.description: Jonny’s five-week boot camp, Nervous System Mastery, will equip you with evidence-backed protocols to cultivate greater calm and agency over your internal state. Learn to rewire maladaptive stress responses and improve your sleep ( use code LENNY for $250 discount ). Apply here .

moments.speaker: Jonny Miller
moments.text: I have this idea that I call the feather brick dump truck phenomenon. When we are showing early signs of burnout, our body will give us feedback usually in subtle ways in the beginning. The feather might be waking up in the morning and feeling a little bit tired. The brick, maybe you have a fight with someone. And then the dump truck is a full-blown health crisis.
moments.summary: The 'feather, brick, dump truck' framework describes how burnout signals escalate from subtle fatigue to interpersonal conflict to full health crises if ignored.

moments.speaker: Jonny Miller
moments.text: Ideally, you want to notice when it's the feather and then make adjustments or shift then and not have to wait until you experience the full-blown pain of the dump truck, which unfortunately is what happens to a lot of people, especially when they experience burnout for the first time.
moments.summary: Most people only recognize burnout at the dump truck stage because they never learned to notice the early feather-like signals from their body.

podcasts.title: Geoffrey Moore on finding your beachhead, crossing the chasm, and dominating a market
podcasts.description: The “chasm” is the gap between success with early adopters and success with the mainstream market. Crossing the chasm requires selling to “pragmatist customers” who, unlike early adopters, will not buy on the basis of a vision.

moments.speaker: Geoffrey Moore
moments.text: The tendency when you're in the chasm is, 'I just need more customers. I should take any customer I could find,' because we need revenue. It's like taking a match and running it back and forth under a log. It's not going to light the log.
moments.summary: Companies in the chasm mistakenly chase any customer for revenue, but scattered efforts are like waving a match under a log -- you need focused kindling first.

moments.speaker: Geoffrey Moore
moments.text: How do you start a fire? Well, you start it by putting a little kindling, little crumpled up paper, and you hold the match in one place until the fire starts. That's why adjacency is so important. If you light the fire, the piece of kindling is here, but the log is in the other room. That doesn't work.
moments.summary: Crossing the chasm requires holding the match in one place with adjacent kindling, meaning dominating one tight beachhead before expanding to adjacent segments.

moments.speaker: Geoffrey Moore
moments.text: So how do you start a fire? Well, you start it by putting a little kindling, little crumpled up paper, and you hold the match in one place until the fire starts.
moments.summary: Market domination begins with concentrated effort on a narrow segment that can catch fire and spread to adjacent markets.

podcasts.title: Good Strategy, Bad Strategy | Richard Rumelt
podcasts.description: Good Strategy, Bad Strategy, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Richard Rumelt
moments.text: Don't call it strategy, call it an action agenda. Begin to try to identify the one or two key challenges that can actually be addressed and what are we going to do about it? What are the coherent actions we're going to do to take these on?
moments.summary: Real strategy is simply identifying the one or two key addressable challenges and defining coherent actions to tackle them, not crafting mission and vision statements.

moments.speaker: Richard Rumelt
moments.text: It's huge numbers of people out there willing to sell you advice on mission and your vision and your values, all these things that have to be in place before you can have strategy. That's not true.
moments.summary: The strategy industry has created a false prerequisite that you need mission, vision, and values before you can think strategically.

moments.speaker: Richard Rumelt
moments.text: We're going to go after this and here's the action steps we're going to take to do that. That's the essence of what you're doing when you're thinking strategically.
moments.summary: Strategic thinking at its core is choosing what to go after and defining the specific steps to get there.

podcasts.title: The art and wisdom of changing teams | Heidi Helfand (author of Dynamic Reteaming)
podcasts.description: Change and growth are natural parts of company evolution. Don’t fear reteaming or reorgs; instead, embrace these changes, actively plan for them, and leverage the five reteaming patterns to create a dynamic and adaptive organizational culture.

moments.speaker: Heidi Helfand
moments.text: Reteaming is hard. Reorgs are hard. You can't lump them all into one thing with oh, it's all great all the time. No, it's not. If we could just build the software, deliver to the customer, get the product market fit, hey, have we delighted them or not? If only it could be that easy. No, we have the people layer, so let's focus there too.
moments.summary: Companies focus on product and market fit but often neglect the people layer, where reteaming and reorgs are unavoidable and deserve equal attention.

moments.speaker: Heidi Helfand
moments.text: Change and growth are natural parts of company evolution. Don't fear reteaming or reorgs; instead, embrace these changes, actively plan for them, and leverage the five reteaming patterns to create a dynamic and adaptive organizational culture.
moments.summary: Rather than fearing reorgs, leaders should proactively plan for team changes using structured reteaming patterns.

podcasts.title: Taking control of your career | Ethan Evans (Amazon)
podcasts.description: The Magic Loop framework: a five-step process to grow your career.

moments.speaker: Ethan Evans
moments.text: People think invention takes all this time, but you only need two hours once a month. The thing is, once you have one good idea, it often takes years to express that.
moments.summary: Innovation requires surprisingly little ideation time; the real work is the years of execution needed to fully express a single good idea.

moments.speaker: Ethan Evans
moments.text: Jeff and Amazon had ideas like, 'Let's have Prime shipping.' Prime is still getting better and still being worked on. It's a 20 some year old idea. The Kindle, a decades old idea now still getting better. The point here is you don't need very many good ideas to be seen as tremendously inventive.
moments.summary: Amazon Prime and Kindle are decades-old ideas still being refined, proving that being inventive requires very few ideas but deep, sustained execution.

moments.speaker: Ethan Evans
moments.text: You had the idea to have a newsletter. You've been working on the expression of that idea for years now.
moments.summary: Even Lenny's newsletter is an example of a single idea that took years to fully express and develop into a business.

podcasts.title: How to be more innovative | Sam Schillace (Microsoft deputy CTO, creator of Google Docs)
podcasts.description: The importance of taking risks, embracing failure, and finding joy in your work.

moments.speaker: Sam Schillace
moments.text: We tend to undervalue the things we're good at. We tend to think work has to be unpleasant. And so if something is easy and fun, we don't tend to think it's valuable. You should go do the thing that you feel guilty to get paid for, if there's a thing like that, and do the hell out of it.
moments.summary: People undervalue their natural talents because easy work feels illegitimate; the best career strategy is to maximize what feels effortless and fun.

moments.speaker: Sam Schillace
moments.text: Lots of people gravitate in this direction of like, let's go do unpleasant things and grind our way through the career because that's the way to make it. But the reality is... if you get pleasure from doing something that people want to pay you for, do it the best you can do it, as hard as you can do it. Work doesn't necessarily have to be hard.
moments.summary: The 'grind through unpleasant work' mentality is a trap; the creator of Google Docs argues that leaning into what brings you joy creates the most value.

moments.speaker: Sam Schillace
moments.text: If that's messing around and playing around with cool ideas, do the hell out of that.
moments.summary: Playful exploration and experimentation with ideas is a legitimate and often superior path to career success and innovation.

podcasts.title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
podcasts.description: The engineering mindset, covering team leadership, career development, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Will Larson
moments.text: I think that we often treat engineers a little bit like children instead of giving them the responsibilities and ability to actually thrive as adults. 'Oh, the engineers won't want to do that work.' Well, that's actually not good for the engineers to be sheltered from what is important.
moments.summary: Product organizations inadvertently stunt engineer growth by shielding them from real business problems instead of treating them as capable adults.

moments.speaker: Will Larson
moments.text: One of the highlights is that I think we're coming back this moment where we can actually treat engineers like our peers and put them into really senior leadership roles and not have this kind of baseline assumption of like, 'Oh, we have to coddle them or hide them from the real problems.'
moments.summary: The industry is returning to a moment where engineers can be placed in senior leadership roles rather than being protected from business realities.

moments.speaker: Will Larson
moments.text: This is how they're going to get the opportunity to grow as well.
moments.summary: Exposing engineers to real business problems is not just organizationally beneficial but essential for their personal career growth.

podcasts.title: The UX research reckoning is here | Judd Antin (Airbnb, Meta)
podcasts.description: The UX research reckoning is here, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Judd Antin
moments.text: User-centered performance refers to customer obsession or user-centered practice that is symbolic rather than focused on learning. It's hugely common. It's work we do to signal to each other how customer obsessed we are, not because we want to make a different decision.
moments.summary: Much of what companies call 'user research' is actually performative -- done to signal customer obsession rather than to genuinely inform decisions.

moments.speaker: Judd Antin
moments.text: Every time a PM comes to a researcher at the end of a product process and says, 'Can you just run a quick user study just to validate our assumptions,' that's user-centered performance. It's too late to matter. We got to ship it. What they want is to check the box.
moments.summary: Late-stage validation studies are the most common form of user-centered performance -- they exist to check a box, not to change a decision.

moments.speaker: Judd Antin
moments.text: One of my big mantras was, 'We don't validate, we falsify. We are looking to be wrong.' Many PMs, many designers are not in that place. They do not want to be wrong. They're looking to validate, and that's user-centered performance.
moments.summary: True user research seeks to falsify assumptions rather than validate them, but most product teams are psychologically wired to confirm what they already believe.

podcasts.title: Strategies for becoming less distracted and improving focus | Nir Eyal (author of Indistractable and Hooked)
podcasts.description: Strategies for becoming less distracted and improving focus, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Nir Eyal
moments.text: I went to Alibaba and I bought myself one of these flip phones from China like we used to have in the 1990s with no apps, no internet connection. And then I got myself a word processor off of eBay so that I could just sit down and write. And even when I stopped using all the technology... I kept getting distracted because the problem is not our technology. The problem is our inability to deal with discomfort.
moments.summary: Even after eliminating all technology, Nir Eyal still got distracted, proving that the root cause of distraction is emotional discomfort, not devices.

moments.speaker: Nir Eyal
moments.text: Always identify what is that internal trigger, what is that itch that you are looking to escape when you get distracted, because that is the source of 90% of our distractions.
moments.summary: 90% of distraction stems from internal triggers -- emotional itches we try to escape -- not from external notifications or technology.

moments.speaker: Nir Eyal
moments.text: Time management is pain management. The reason we get distracted is not because of what's happening outside of us but what's happening inside of us.
moments.summary: The core insight of Indistractable is that managing your time is really about managing your relationship with discomfort.

podcasts.title: The hierarchy of engagement | Sarah Tavel (Benchmark, Greylock, Pinterest)
podcasts.description: Sarah’s Hierarchy of Engagement framework for growing a consumer startup.

moments.speaker: Sarah Tavel
moments.text: I think a lot of people think about markets almost like these bodies of water, it's like it's this big body of water that we're going after. I actually think that the most interesting markets, you have to think of them like currents where there's something happening in the market that's creating this current where you can have a plank of wood that you've put on the river and it's going to pull you forward.
moments.summary: The best markets are not static pools of opportunity but dynamic currents that pull companies forward, making market momentum more important than market size.

moments.speaker: Sarah Tavel
moments.text: That's why we care less about market size because really, what you're looking for when you're looking at a market, are what are the dynamics of change, what's the current and momentum that's going to pull the company and make the job easier for the founders to actually build something that endures.
moments.summary: Benchmark cares less about TAM and more about the dynamics of change in a market, because momentum does the heavy lifting for founders.

moments.speaker: Sarah Tavel
moments.text: Versus a market that doesn't really have that momentum to it, you're going to have to build something really big and fancy to make any progress.
moments.summary: In markets without natural momentum, even the best product requires enormous effort to make progress, while dynamic markets amplify modest efforts.

podcasts.title: The essence of product management | Christian Idiodi (SVPG)
podcasts.description: The essence of product management, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and product design.

moments.speaker: Christian Idiodi
moments.text: I try to explain to people that the real essence of this job is that you wake up on behalf of someone else to solve a problem for them, and you have to do it well enough that they give you something back in return. That's kind of the real essence of it, and that's, I always call it a certificate of appreciation.
moments.summary: Product management at its core is waking up to solve someone else's problem well enough that they reward you with revenue, engagement, or loyalty.

moments.speaker: Christian Idiodi
moments.text: If it's not fun, you're probably not doing it right. If it's not hard, you're probably also not doing it right.
moments.summary: Good product management should be both fun and hard; if it's missing either quality, something is off about how you're approaching the role.

moments.speaker: Christian Idiodi
moments.text: And it could be in the form of revenue, engagement, loyalty, reference, all of those things.
moments.summary: The 'certificate of appreciation' from customers manifests as revenue, engagement, loyalty, or referrals -- all signals that you've solved their problem well.

podcasts.title: Jason Fried challenges your thinking on fundraising, goals, growth, and more
podcasts.description: Fundraising, goals, growth, and more, covering product design, startup building, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Jason Fried
moments.text: The reason I think it's great for entrepreneurs to start bootstrapping is because they just have more practice making money, and they get better, and better, and better at the fundamental skill you need to have ultimately to run a successful business, which is to make money.
moments.summary: Bootstrapping forces founders to practice the fundamental skill of making money from day one, while funded founders often defer this critical learning.

moments.speaker: Jason Fried
moments.text: Basically almost nobody makes it that way. Really, almost nobody really makes it that way, and there's a lot more room to make it, and to build a successful business, if you throw out that outlier and look at all the other places you can land as a business.
moments.summary: The venture-funded unicorn path has an astronomically low success rate; there's far more room to build successful businesses outside that narrow model.

moments.speaker: Jason Fried
moments.text: Hopefully, I don't come off as encouraging everyone to be like me. I'm not saying that at all. What I'm saying is, this is a way to be. It's an alternative to what you're often hearing in our industry, which is, 'Go big or go home.'
moments.summary: Jason Fried presents bootstrapping not as the only way but as a legitimate alternative to the dominant Silicon Valley narrative of go-big-or-go-home.

podcasts.title: How to tell better stories | Matthew Dicks (Storyworthy)
podcasts.description: Tell better stories, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Matthew Dicks
moments.text: Everyone loves the word storytelling in business. It's a huge buzzword. They love to think of themselves as storytellers, but when they come to me, they don't really want to be storytellers. Because to be a storyteller means you have to separate yourself from the herd, and in their mind, that risks them getting picked off.
moments.summary: Business professionals want the label of storyteller without the vulnerability it requires -- true storytelling means standing out from the herd.

moments.speaker: Matthew Dicks
moments.text: How many times have you gone to a conference, listened to someone speak, and by the time you're pulling into the driveway, you really can't remember anything that they said? Because that's what happens if we don't speak in story. Our minds are not designed to remember a pie chart or facts or statistics or platitudes or ideas that are not attached to imagery.
moments.summary: Without story, presentations are forgotten by the drive home because human brains are not wired to retain isolated facts, charts, or platitudes.

moments.speaker: Matthew Dicks
moments.text: The risk you take if you're not telling stories is that you will be forgotten. 100%. You will be forgotten.
moments.summary: The biggest risk in communication is not vulnerability from storytelling but the certainty of being forgotten without it.

podcasts.title: Failure
podcasts.description: In this special compilation episode, we delve into failure—an overlooked source of wisdom. From freezing onstage in front of thousands of people, to coworkers staging an intervention, to huge product investments that went to zero, we’ve pulled our favorite stories of failure from 100+ podcast episodes.

moments.speaker: Katie Dill
moments.text: Katie Dill shares her story of failure from the compilation episode on the overlooked wisdom that comes from product failures and personal setbacks.
moments.summary: The failure compilation episode gathers the most insightful stories of failure from over 100 podcast episodes to extract overlooked wisdom.

moments.speaker: Various Guests
moments.text: From freezing onstage in front of thousands of people, to coworkers staging an intervention, to huge product investments that went to zero, we've pulled our favorite stories of failure from 100+ podcast episodes.
moments.summary: The episode compiles raw failure stories ranging from public humiliation to billion-dollar product failures, normalizing setbacks as a source of wisdom.

moments.speaker: Various Guests
moments.text: You can learn a lot and often a lot more from failure.
moments.summary: Studying how the best companies fail often teaches more than studying how they succeed.

podcasts.title: Radical Candor: From theory to practice with author Kim Scott
podcasts.description: From theory to practice with author Kim Scott, covering team leadership, product design, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Kim Scott
moments.text: If you say, 'Do you have any feedback for me?' You're wasting your breath. The other person's going to say, 'Oh no, everything's fine.' The question that I like to ask is, 'What could I do or stop doing that would make it easier to work with me?'
moments.summary: Generic feedback requests fail because they're too easy to dodge; asking what to do or stop doing that would make working together easier forces a real answer.

moments.speaker: Kim Scott
moments.text: Do not write down my question because if you sound like Kim Scott and not like yourself, then other people are not going to believe you want the answer. It needs to sound authentic to you.
moments.summary: Feedback questions must be phrased in your own authentic voice, not copied from a book, or people will not believe you genuinely want the answer.

moments.speaker: Kim Scott
moments.text: If everybody can write down their question, who they're going to ask it of and then pop it into their calendar right now, this will be one of the most productive podcasts in all of podcast land.
moments.summary: Kim Scott challenges listeners to immediately schedule asking their personalized feedback question to someone specific, turning insight into action.

podcasts.title: The full-stack PM | Anuj Rathi (Swiggy, Jupiter Money, Flipkart)
podcasts.description: The full-stack PM, covering product design, product strategy and execution, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Anuj Rathi
moments.text: There are only three reasons why things do not happen the way you want them to happen as a leader. Either that person can't do, which is a capability issue, or they won't do, which is a motivation or an alignment issue, or they were not set up to do, which is really your problem that you didn't set up the ways of working properly.
moments.summary: When things go wrong as a leader, the root cause is always one of three things: capability gap, motivation gap, or a systemic setup failure on your part.

moments.speaker: Anuj Rathi
moments.text: If they won't do, why won't they? Are they not aligned to you? Do they not agree with your vision? Do they not just have enough time? You need to really go deeper there.
moments.summary: When someone 'won't do' the work, leaders must dig deeper to understand whether it's misalignment, disagreement with the vision, or simply a capacity issue.

moments.speaker: Anuj Rathi
moments.text: As a leader, do you have the right people in terms of capability? If not, is the right answer for us to coach them or to really put them... or mentor them, or move them to some other place because maybe their capability is suited elsewhere?
moments.summary: A full-stack PM leader must constantly assess whether to coach, mentor, or redeploy people based on where their capabilities are best suited.

podcasts.title: Crafting a compelling product vision | Ebi Atawodi (YouTube, Netflix, Uber)
podcasts.description: Crafting a compelling product vision, covering strategic decision-making, team leadership, and product design.

moments.speaker: Ebi Atawodi
moments.text: I do not believe in being liked. I believe in being loved. And love is the choice to extend yourself for the spiritual growth of oneself or another. When you are extending yourself, you're not nice. It sometimes is having hard conversations.
moments.summary: True leadership is about being loved, not liked, which means extending yourself for others' growth even when it requires uncomfortable honesty.

moments.speaker: Ebi Atawodi
moments.text: It's knowing that, oh, there's a human, they know I care about them. So when the feedback is coming like raw, they know that it's in their best interest because I've shown enough times that I genuinely care about the person behind the role.
moments.summary: Raw, honest feedback is only received well when you've built a foundation of genuine care for the person behind the professional role.

moments.speaker: Ebi Atawodi
moments.text: And that's love. And when you are extending yourself, you're not nice. It's not always nice or like, it sometimes is having hard conversations.
moments.summary: Caring deeply about someone's growth often looks like having difficult conversations rather than being pleasant and agreeable.

podcasts.title: The future of AI in software development | Inbal Shani (CPO of GitHub)
podcasts.description: The future of AI in software development, covering engineering tradeoffs, AI product work, and product design.

moments.speaker: Inbal Shani
moments.text: The user of the AI tools to develop software needs to form a different thinking. You need to start figuring out how are you using these AI tools to help you be successful. And it's no longer just the actual code writing, it's really evolving your thinking to the big picture, to the connected experience, to connected systems.
moments.summary: AI coding tools require developers to shift from code-writing thinking to systems-level thinking about connected experiences and architecture.

moments.speaker: Inbal Shani
moments.text: The junior developers, when they start, usually we expect them to be able to write simple code. But if now there is an AI system that is helping them writing code, they can spend more time from the get-go understanding the system, understanding the environment that they're building, or understanding that product that they're building, which today they don't have time because they're still learning how to code.
moments.summary: AI coding tools could leapfrog junior developers past basic code-writing to focus on understanding systems and products from their first day.

moments.speaker: Inbal Shani
moments.text: Which today we just find it more in the world of more senior developers and less and less in the junior developers.
moments.summary: Systems-level thinking has traditionally been a senior developer skill, but AI tools may make it accessible to developers at all levels.

podcasts.title: Interview Q Compilation
podcasts.description: Because I think humble people will always say "luck" in some way, and I always kind of want to know, how self-aware are you, basically, and how curious are you, covering career development, measurement and analysis, and product design.

moments.speaker: Shishir Mehrotra
moments.text: A group of scientists have invented a teleportation device. They've hired you to be their business counterpart, bring this to market. You could be a product manager for this thing. What do you do? Usually people will start asking a bunch of questions. At some point I'll say, they will answer only two of your questions. What two questions do you ask?
moments.summary: Shishir Mehrotra's teleportation device interview question tests whether candidates can identify the few critical questions that drive all downstream decisions.

moments.speaker: Eeke De Miliano
moments.text: To what do you attribute your success? And you can't say luck. Because I think humble people will always say luck in some way, and I always kind of want to know, how self-aware are you, basically, and how curious are you?
moments.summary: Asking candidates to explain their success without citing luck reveals their self-awareness, curiosity, and ability to reflect on what actually drove their achievements.

moments.speaker: Nikhyl Singhal
moments.text: What's something that everyone takes for granted that you think is essentially hogwash or inaccurate? I'm always looking for people to break this sort of interview mindset. There's no way to answer that question without being genuinely opinionated.
moments.summary: Asking candidates what conventional wisdom they think is wrong forces authenticity and breaks through the rehearsed interview performance.

moments.speaker: Paul Adams
moments.text: What feedback will I be giving this person in their first performance review? It's an amazing question, because the person can't dodge it. There's an answer, and it's incredibly enlightening.
moments.summary: The best reference call question is asking what feedback you'll give in their first review, because it's impossible to dodge and reveals genuine development areas.

podcasts.title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Snap, Zero)
podcasts.description: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success, covering product design, team leadership, and career development.

moments.speaker: Tom Conrad
moments.text: There's this belief that everybody needs to be a founder. I think, in some ways, our industry would be much better off if there were fewer founders. There's an entire category of smart, creative, hardworking, talented, borderline visionary people who can raise that $2 million seed and go off and build some stupid company that's never going to go anywhere.
moments.summary: The tech industry's founder worship wastes talented people on doomed startups when they'd create more impact joining existing teams with real traction.

moments.speaker: Tom Conrad
moments.text: That would be so much better off finding a team that needs their skillset and working on a problem that has a mathematical formula that's going to win on any metric. Whatever metric you care about. You want the acclaim of your peers, you want financial reward, you want outside impact on culture, whatever the thing is that gets you out of bed every morning, you can achieve that in collaboration with others.
moments.summary: The best career outcomes often come from joining the right team rather than founding your own company, regardless of what metric of success you care about.

moments.speaker: Tom Conrad
moments.text: You don't have to be the person that raises the seed round.
moments.summary: Being a founder is not the only path to outsized impact, financial reward, or fulfillment in tech.

podcasts.title: Redefining success, money, and belonging | Paul Millerd (The Pathless Path)
podcasts.description: Redefining success, money, and belonging, covering consumer products, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Paul Millerd
moments.text: Take three hours during a workday. Has to be during a workday. Block off your schedule, sneak out. Go for a walk without a destination or do something from your childhood that you used to do all the time. And just pay attention. What is emerging? Do you feel bad for sneaking out of work? Where does that bad feeling come from?
moments.summary: Taking three hours off during a workday to do something unstructured reveals your deepest assumptions about work, identity, and self-worth.

moments.speaker: Paul Millerd
moments.text: A lot of people have never really thought about, why do you work? People say money. Okay, that's fine. But what else? Why? Are you trying to be a good person? Do you see a good person as somebody that works every day? Maybe. These are a lot of scripts people grow up with.
moments.summary: Most people have never examined why they work beyond money, operating on unquestioned scripts about work equaling virtue that were inherited from childhood.

moments.speaker: Paul Millerd
moments.text: You're really just creating this space to get in touch with, how do I actually feel about work? How do I get to know myself more? Are there things that I've lost touch with that really bring me alive?
moments.summary: Creating space away from work is not about laziness but about reconnecting with what genuinely energizes you beyond your professional identity.

podcasts.title: Marketplace lessons from Uber, Airbnb, Bumble, and more | Ramesh Johari (Stanford professor, startup advisor)
podcasts.description: Marketplace lessons from Uber, Airbnb, Bumble, and more, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Ramesh Johari
moments.text: Marketplaces are a little bit like a game of whac-a-mole. We decided to build some custom bespoke features that were really going to direct new supply to more experienced folks on the other side of the market. Good. And then lo and behold, now the existing folks on the other side are having a worse experience.
moments.summary: Marketplace management is a constant whac-a-mole game where improving one side's experience inevitably degrades the other side's metrics.

moments.speaker: Ramesh Johari
moments.text: A lot of marketplace management is moving attention and inventory around. Many of the changes that are most consequential create winners and losers. And rolling with those changes is about recognizing whether the winners you've created are more important to your business than the losers you've created in the process.
moments.summary: The hardest marketplace decisions involve accepting that consequential changes always create losers, and judging whether the winners matter more to your business.

moments.speaker: Ramesh Johari
moments.text: Your metrics just keep moving around. And that's because the whac-a-mole game here is ultimately about moving attention and inventory around.
moments.summary: Marketplace metrics constantly shift because every intervention redistributes attention and inventory, creating new problems as it solves old ones.

podcasts.title: Mastering product strategy and growing as a PM | Maggie Crowley (Toast, Drift, Tripadvisor)
podcasts.description: Mastering product strategy and growing as a PM, covering strategic decision-making, product strategy and execution, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Maggie Crowley
moments.text: If you ever find yourself saying something like, that's not my job, that's probably a thing you should do. You can spend your life getting frustrated at that or you can just get over and get the work done. And people who are willing to just get the work done will move faster.
moments.summary: When a PM thinks 'that's not my job,' that's usually the exact thing they should do, because ownership over unglamorous work is what separates great PMs.

moments.speaker: Maggie Crowley
moments.text: As a PM, for better or for worse, you're oftentimes the emotional center of the team and it's your job to keep people motivated, keep people excited, keep them bought into the project, and you just have to keep that optimism going and it's hard work.
moments.summary: PMs are the emotional center of their team, responsible for maintaining motivation and optimism even when the work is unglamorous.

moments.speaker: Maggie Crowley
moments.text: Their products will be more successful and they probably aren't carrying around all that anger and crappy emotion because... you just have to do whatever it takes.
moments.summary: PMs who embrace a 'whatever it takes' mentality not only ship more successful products but also feel less frustrated than those guarding their job boundaries.

podcasts.title: Unpacking Amazon’s unique ways of working | Bill Carr (author of Working Backwards)
podcasts.description: Unpacking Amazon’s unique ways of working, covering team leadership, measurement and analysis, and product design.

moments.speaker: Bill Carr
moments.text: Jeff would say, we took it as an article of faith. If we served customers well, if we prioritized customers and delivered for them, things like sales, things like revenue and active customers and things like the share price and free cash flow would follow.
moments.summary: Amazon's foundational belief was that obsessing over customer outcomes would cause revenue, stock price, and free cash flow to follow as natural consequences.

moments.speaker: Bill Carr
moments.text: When we're making a decision thinking about a problem, we're going to start with what's best for the customer and then come backward from there. That informs what's the work you have to do to then create this new solution for customers.
moments.summary: Amazon's 'working backwards' method starts every decision with what's best for the customer and reverse-engineers the required work from there.

moments.speaker: Bill Carr
moments.text: So therefore, when we're making a decision thinking about a problem, we're going to start with what's best for the customer and then come backward from there.
moments.summary: The 'working backwards' philosophy makes customer benefit the starting point of every product decision rather than business metrics.

podcasts.title: Reflections on a movement | Eric Ries (creator of the Lean Startup methodology)
podcasts.description: Reflections on a movement, covering startup building, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Eric Ries
moments.text: People act like having a startup fail is the worst thing that can happen to you. And man, that's not even in the top 10. Far worse is to be in a company that won't die, a zombie, undead company that you hate, but you can't leave.
moments.summary: A clean startup failure is far less damaging than being trapped in a zombie company that won't die but that you hate -- the psychological toll of the latter is much worse.

moments.speaker: Eric Ries
moments.text: We're having a mental health crisis among founders that's not talked about enough. When you build a company and you sell it out and it becomes something that you find abhorrent, man, maybe you get rich, but it's not good.
moments.summary: The untold founder mental health crisis comes not from failure but from success that creates something the founder finds morally abhorrent.

moments.speaker: Eric Ries
moments.text: Building a company you hate, that becomes a maligned force in the world, that you have to go pretend you weren't involved with or like you feel complicit in... That's way, way worse, and I wish more founders would take that more seriously early on.
moments.summary: Eric Ries urges founders to take seriously the risk of building something that succeeds but becomes a force they're ashamed to be associated with.

podcasts.title: What AI means for your product strategy | Paul Adams (CPO of Intercom)
podcasts.description: What AI means for your product strategy, covering product design, AI product work, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Paul Adams
moments.text: This is a meteor coming towards you. This is going to radically transform society. And I think if people don't explore AI properly, it will leave them behind.
moments.summary: Paul Adams frames AI as an extinction-level event for unprepared products, urging teams to treat it with the urgency of a meteor heading toward them.

moments.speaker: Paul Adams
moments.text: I'd start with the thing your product does. 'What's the core premise behind it? Why do people use it? What problem does it solve for them?' Go back to basics. And then ask, 'Can AI do that?' And for a lot, the answer is going to be, 'Yes, it can.'
moments.summary: Intercom's CPO recommends starting your AI strategy by asking whether AI can do the core thing your product does, and many will find the answer is yes.

moments.speaker: Paul Adams
moments.text: For some it'll be replacement, AI would replace, it'll just do it. And, in other places, it'll be augmentation. It'll augment. It'll help people. But yeah, I think that you've got to match your product, and what AI can do, and what it will be able to do, and then ask yourself, 'Okay, what are we going to do?'
moments.summary: AI will either replace or augment different parts of every product, and companies must map their features against AI capabilities to decide their response.

podcasts.title: Scaling Duolingo, embracing failure, and insight into Latin America’s tech scene | Gina Gotthilf (Latitud, Duolingo)
podcasts.description: Scaling Duolingo, embracing failure, and insight into Latin America’s tech scene, covering startup building, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Gina Gotthilf
moments.text: Communication is constantly underrated. And communication isn't about being able to convey a message, it's about being able to convey a message in a way that the listener receives it, and understands it, and remembers it. And that's really hard to do.
moments.summary: Effective communication is not about sending messages but about ensuring they are received, understood, and remembered, which is a fundamentally different skill.

moments.speaker: Gina Gotthilf
moments.text: One of the things I've helped employ at Duolingo is a unique voice. There is always a quirk, like it's unexpected. The way we talk to you is a little bit funny. It doesn't take ourselves too seriously and it makes the person receiving this message feel something.
moments.summary: Duolingo's distinctive brand voice works because it makes users feel something through humor and unexpectedness rather than generic corporate messaging.

moments.speaker: Gina Gotthilf
moments.text: Again, it's about how you make people feel. And you feel like either you giggle, or you're like, wait what? They just did what? And using that to your benefit.
moments.summary: The secret to Duolingo's growth was making people feel something, whether through laughter or surprise, creating organic sharing.

podcasts.title: Building beautiful products with Stripe’s Head of Design | Katie Dill (Stripe, Airbnb, Lyft)
podcasts.description: Building beautiful products with Stripe’s Head of Design, covering product design, team leadership, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Katie Dill
moments.text: The use of the word beauty in books that have been digitized by Google has decreased, like pretty dramatically. And it's aligned with this idea of, 'Well, functionality is king.' As if people think about functionality and beauty as like two opposite things. No, they're not two opposite things. Beauty enhances functionality.
moments.summary: The false dichotomy between beauty and functionality has caused a measurable decline in valuing aesthetics, when in fact beauty directly enhances usability.

moments.speaker: Katie Dill
moments.text: Things that are more beautiful, increase trust. You see that we've put painstaking detail into this, and we care about the details of how something works, and that gives you assurance that we care about other details that you can't see too.
moments.summary: Beautiful design builds trust by signaling that a company cares about visible details, which implies they also care about the invisible ones.

moments.speaker: Katie Dill
moments.text: The other piece of it that is not talked about in business as often is just the importance of how people feel. Things that are more beautiful, increase trust.
moments.summary: Business undervalues how products make people feel, yet beauty is one of the most powerful drivers of user trust and product adoption.

podcasts.title: How to get press for your product | Jason Feifer (editor in chief of Entrepreneur magazine)
podcasts.description: Getting press for your product, covering startup building, practical product lessons, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Jason Feifer
moments.text: The editor, the writer, I'll just say it as plainly as possible. They don't care about you. They don't care about you. They care about their reader or their listener or their viewer. That's who they care about.
moments.summary: Media gatekeepers serve their audience, not you, so approach them by understanding what value you bring to their readers.

moments.speaker: Jason Feifer
moments.text: Before you ever pitch, just get me to recognize your name. Just engage in social media in a very casual way, such that when you email me, I think, do I know who that person is? It just makes it more likely that I'll open the email.
moments.summary: Building name recognition through casual social media engagement before pitching dramatically increases your chances of getting a response from editors.

moments.speaker: Jason Feifer
moments.text: Sometimes you are not the story, but you can be part of the story. Sometimes that means you could just be a writer. You might try to just pitch authoritative articles by you to different publications.
moments.summary: If your company is hard to write about directly, position yourself as part of a bigger trend or offer expert commentary to get press indirectly.

moments.speaker: Jason Feifer
moments.text: Opportunity set B is what's available to you, even though nobody's asking you to do it. What I have found throughout my own career is that opportunity set B is always more important, infinitely more important. Opportunity set A, everything that's asked of you. Opportunity set B is where growth happens.
moments.summary: Career growth comes not from excelling at what is asked of you, but from pursuing opportunities that are available to you that nobody is requesting.

podcasts.title: Inside Linear: Building with taste, craft, and focus | Karri Saarinen (co-founder, designer, CEO)
podcasts.description: Building with taste, craft, and focus, covering product design, team leadership, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Karri Saarinen
moments.text: The more it matters, the more the design matters. Whenever there's a new paradigm, the first iterations of those products don't have to be super well designed. Then, as you build the 100,000 different email clients, any email client now has to be pretty good to be even considered.
moments.summary: As product categories mature, the bar for design quality rises dramatically and becomes table stakes for being taken seriously.

moments.speaker: Karri Saarinen
moments.text: Today it's almost a very basic thing now. Pretty much from the very beginning, you need pretty high level design for people to even pay attention or consider you seriously.
moments.summary: In today's saturated market, high-quality design is no longer a differentiator but a baseline requirement to earn user attention.

moments.speaker: Karri Saarinen
moments.text: My belief is that, like any domain or industry, the more it matters, the more the design matters. What happens is whenever there's a new paradigm, I don't know, like the mobile or the web or something the first iterations of those products existing there, they don't have to be super well designed necessarily because they are the first.
moments.summary: New paradigms create temporary windows where design standards are low, but those windows close as competition increases.

podcasts.title: Brian Balfour: 10 lessons on career, growth, and life
podcasts.description: Career, growth, and life, covering AI product work, growth systems, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Brian Balfour
moments.text: All of the ingredients for new distribution platform are essentially happening. My prediction, the new distribution platform will be ChatGPT. There's a bunch of signals that they're about to launch that.
moments.summary: ChatGPT is poised to become the next major distribution platform for products, similar to how Facebook and mobile app stores were in their era.

moments.speaker: Brian Balfour
moments.text: It ends up being a prisoner's dilemma. Don't trick yourself into thinking that you can't play the game. The cycles seem to be getting shorter and shorter, so you actually have a smaller amount of time. If you don't do it, your competitors are going to go to the new platform and your customer expectations change. There is no opting out of the game.
moments.summary: Distribution platform shifts are a prisoner's dilemma where cycles are accelerating, and companies cannot afford to opt out.

moments.speaker: Brian Balfour
moments.text: If you're a late-stage company, you place multiple bets. For startups, it's a totally different ballgame. You have to choose one and go all in.
moments.summary: Late-stage companies should diversify distribution bets, but startups must concentrate all resources on a single channel to win.

podcasts.title: What sets great teams apart | Lane Shackleton (CPO of Coda)
podcasts.description: What sets great teams apart, covering product design, team leadership, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Lane Shackleton
moments.text: Moments that stretch you or moments that you feel uncomfortable in or you find yourself saying, 'Oh shit. I shouldn't be here,' or, 'I'm under qualified to be here,' those are the moments you should be seeking out. A much sharper way is like, 'Hey, how many oh shit moments have you had in the last six months?'
moments.summary: The best measure of career growth is counting how many 'oh shit' moments of discomfort and stretch you have experienced recently.

moments.speaker: Lane Shackleton
moments.text: If you ask yourself that question and the answer is, 'It's been a really long time since I've been stretched in some meaningful way or I've felt like I'm under qualified to be there,' then it may be worth digging into.
moments.summary: A prolonged absence of discomfort and stretch is a red flag signaling career stagnation that deserves serious introspection.

moments.speaker: Lane Shackleton
moments.text: Those are the moments that stretch you and give you a new foundation. So oftentimes you'll hear a career question like, 'Hey, do you feel like you're growing in your role?' And that's a very ambiguous, in my opinion, way to ask this question.
moments.summary: Asking 'are you growing?' is too vague; tracking specific moments of discomfort gives a sharper read on actual development.

podcasts.title: Building Anchor, selling to Spotify, and lessons learned | Maya Prohovnik (Spotify’s Head of Podcast Product)
podcasts.description: Building Anchor, selling to Spotify, and lessons learned, covering team leadership, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Maya Prohovnik
moments.text: We hired a couple of college interns and we brought them in and we were like, people are going to push this magical one button in the Anchor app and they're going to say, I want to distribute my podcast, and your job is going to be to do all that same manual stuff manually, but to them it's going to feel magical.
moments.summary: Anchor used a Wizard of Oz approach with college interns manually submitting podcasts to Apple to create a seemingly magical one-click distribution experience.

moments.speaker: Maya Prohovnik
moments.text: We were obsessed with reducing friction, this was our constant battle. I still don't know how many people know this. I think people think that we had some secret backdoor deal with Apple for distribution, but we just had college students making Apple podcast accounts and then submitting hundreds of thousands of podcasts.
moments.summary: Anchor's explosive podcast hosting growth came not from secret deals but from relentlessly reducing friction through manual human-powered operations.

moments.speaker: Maya Prohovnik
moments.text: That was a really big part of why we got so much hosting market share so quickly because it was such an insane benefit over the other platforms which otherwise had been commoditized at that point.
moments.summary: Doing things that don't scale, like manual podcast submission, created a massive competitive advantage in an otherwise commoditized hosting market.

podcasts.title: How to drive word of mouth | Nilan Peiris (CPO of Wise)
podcasts.description: Driving word of mouth, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Nilan Peiris
moments.text: Some people focus on conversion rate, like, 'I'm going to make this really, really slick.' And that's cool. You get a bit more growth. But to get to recommendation, you're going to blow your user socks off. You have to give them an experience they didn't know was previously possible.
moments.summary: Optimizing conversion rates produces incremental growth, but creating experiences users never knew were possible is what drives organic word of mouth.

moments.speaker: Nilan Peiris
moments.text: When you are in that place of doing something that no one has ever done before, that's when you get it.
moments.summary: True word-of-mouth growth only happens when your product does something genuinely unprecedented in the user's eyes.

moments.speaker: Nilan Peiris
moments.text: To get to recommendation, you're going to blow your user socks off. You have to give them an experience they didn't know was previously possible. And when you are in that place of doing something that no one has ever done before, that's when you get it.
moments.summary: The threshold for earning user recommendations is delivering an experience so novel that users feel compelled to share it.

podcasts.title: Becoming evidence-guided | Itamar Gilad (Gmail, YouTube, Microsoft)
podcasts.description: Becoming evidence-guided, covering product design, team leadership, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Itamar Gilad
moments.text: You fake it, you do a fake door test, you do a smoke test, Wizard of Oz tests. We used a lot of those in the tabbed inbox by the way, one of the first early versions was actually we showed the tabbed inbox working to people. But it wasn't really Gmail, it was just a facade of HTML.
moments.summary: Gmail's tabbed inbox was validated early through Wizard of Oz testing where the team manually sorted emails behind a facade to test the concept.

moments.speaker: Itamar Gilad
moments.text: Behind the scenes and according to the permissions that the users gave us some of us moved just the subject and the sender into the right place. So initially the interviewer kind of distracted them and then showed them their inbox and then the top 50 messages were sorted to the right place. And people were like, 'Wow, this is actually very cool.'
moments.summary: The Gmail team manually sorted 50 emails to simulate the tabbed inbox, proving the concept was compelling before investing in building it.

moments.speaker: Itamar Gilad
moments.text: But it gave us some evidence to go and say, 'Hey, we should try and build this thing.'
moments.summary: Lightweight evidence from scrappy prototypes was enough to justify building what became one of Gmail's most important features.

podcasts.title: How to become a category pirate | Christopher Lochhead (author of Play Bigger, Niche Down, Category Pirates, more)
podcasts.description: Becoming a category pirate, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Christopher Lochhead
moments.text: Now is the greatest time in history to be a creator, to be an entrepreneur, to be a marketer. I've been a marketer for my entire adult life. It's never been greater than it is right now.
moments.summary: The current era offers unprecedented opportunities for creators and entrepreneurs, making this the best time in history to build something.

moments.speaker: Christopher Lochhead
moments.text: If you are somebody for whom you want to make an exponential difference, you want to innovate, you want to create new value where there wasn't, you want to have a legendary career where you could look back on your career and go, you know what? I was part of this and that, and I was on this team, now's the greatest time in history. The future needs you.
moments.summary: Those who aspire to exponential impact and legendary careers should seize this moment because the opportunity window has never been wider.

moments.speaker: Christopher Lochhead
moments.text: The future needs you.
moments.summary: A rallying cry for innovators and entrepreneurs to step into their potential and shape what comes next.

podcasts.title: Picking sharp problems, increasing virality, and unique product frameworks | Oji Udezue (Typeform, Twitter, Calendly, Atlassian)
podcasts.description: Picking sharp problems, increasing virality, and unique product frameworks, covering B2B products, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Oji Udezue
moments.text: Products who try to be viral just for what I call synthetic virality that fail. Because in the end, if you're synthetically viral and people get to the product and it sucks, that's it.
moments.summary: Synthetic virality tactics fail because users who arrive at a bad product never come back, regardless of how clever the distribution hack was.

moments.speaker: Oji Udezue
moments.text: Slack wasn't even viral, there was no synthetic virality. Slack couldn't even connect to organizations for the longest time. But what happens when you went to lunch? People are like, 'We got Slack and this is amazing.' This is the bedrock of virality. Build a great product that solves a sharp problem.
moments.summary: Slack's organic virality came not from growth tricks but from being so good that people couldn't help talking about it over lunch.

moments.speaker: Oji Udezue
moments.text: This is the bedrock of virality. Build a great product that solves a sharp problem.
moments.summary: True virality is rooted in solving a sharp, well-defined problem so well that users become evangelists naturally.

podcasts.title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
podcasts.description: When enough is enough, covering career development, product design, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Andy Johns
moments.text: There are day-to-day stresses that are normal and we just have to put up with, but then there's the other stuff that's the flashing red alarm. You can go back to animals. When their fundamental functions, their core behaviors of diet, exercise, playfulness, socialization, sleep, when those things get disrupted, it's a sign that there is something going on.
moments.summary: When your basic human functions like sleep, relationships, and health start deteriorating, your body is sending unmistakable signals that something needs to change.

moments.speaker: Andy Johns
moments.text: If your sleep always sucks, if your relationships are constantly strained or frequently strained, if your physical health is failing, there's so many ways that that can be measured. So there's really no excuse for that to say, 'Oh, I just didn't know.'
moments.summary: Physical health, sleep, and relationship quality are measurable indicators of unsustainable work patterns that we have no excuse to ignore.

moments.speaker: Andy Johns
moments.text: When those are suffering or when they're really out of whack, it's undeniable that there is something that is detrimental to your wellbeing that's going on right now, and your body is telling you, 'Stop. Something needs to change.'
moments.summary: Persistent dysfunction in core life areas is your body's undeniable signal to stop and make a fundamental change.

podcasts.title: An inside look at Figma’s unique GTM motion | Claire Butler (first GTM hire)
podcasts.description: An inside look at Figma’s unique GTM motion, covering product design, go-to-market execution, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Claire Butler
moments.text: We had Coda. They were our first user, and they were based in Palo Alto. Dylan and I drove down and demoed the product to them, and they were the first ones. Their designer, Jeremy was like, 'Yes, we'll take this on full time.' And I remember we were both like, 'What? Really? You will?'
moments.summary: Figma's first real user adoption came from a hands-on demo where the founders personally drove to Palo Alto to show the product.

moments.speaker: Claire Butler
moments.text: When we launched Designer News, which was a popular forum back in the day, the first response was, 'If this is the future of design, I'm changing careers.' Because designers do not want to be collaborative. It was a massive shift in getting them to think in a different way.
moments.summary: Figma's collaborative design concept was initially rejected by designers who saw solo work as sacred, requiring a major mindset shift.

moments.speaker: Claire Butler
moments.text: Being technical is important. And caring about your tools, that's another one. Designers, specifically, and I think engineers are the same way, have deep passions for their tools. Probably because they're in it eight hours a day. So, it certainly helps if your target audience already cares deeply about what their tools are.
moments.summary: Product-led growth works best when your target users are deeply passionate about their tools and spend significant time in them daily.

moments.speaker: Claire Butler
moments.text: Consistent pressure over time. Taking some of the pressure off of having to do things immediately. You just have to keep working at it, and not giving up. Having that grit to keep going and keep pushing over time, is way more important than any immediate accomplishments.
moments.summary: Sustained consistent effort over time outweighs any single accomplishment, and grit matters more than speed.

podcasts.title: Inside Etsy’s product, growth, and marketplace evolution | Tim Holley (VP of Product)
podcasts.description: Inside Etsy’s product, growth, and marketplace evolution, covering product design, B2B products, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Tim Holley
moments.text: When the CDC mandated face masks in early April 2020, that's when essentially we went to sleep one day with our typical April traffic, typical April sales, and then it was Black Friday overnight.
moments.summary: Etsy experienced overnight Black Friday-level traffic when COVID mask mandates hit because sellers rapidly pivoted to making masks.

moments.speaker: Tim Holley
moments.text: Our sellers are incredibly astute business people. And if you had been making wedding dresses, and you know how to sew, and you've got material, and you've got a bit of time, making a mask is quite a simple task. And so we just saw this huge surge of demand, and then supply rising to meet it.
moments.summary: Etsy's marketplace resilience came from sellers who could rapidly pivot skills like sewing wedding dresses to meet sudden mask demand.

moments.speaker: Tim Holley
moments.text: We did something that as far as I know, we've never done in Etsy's past, which is we put out a call to our sellers to say, 'Now's the time. Now's the time to make face masks if you can.'
moments.summary: Etsy took the unprecedented step of actively calling on sellers to produce masks, leaning into the crisis as a moment to demonstrate marketplace value.

podcasts.title: An inside look at Deel’s unprecedented growth | Meltem Kuran Berkowitz (Head of Growth)
podcasts.description: An inside look at Deel’s unprecedented growth, covering growth systems, team leadership, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Meltem Kuran Berkowitz
moments.text: In the early days, it's very important to just go back to the basics, build the skeleton before you put on the makeup. The first question I would ask is, do you have a website? Is it fast? Do the search engines know that it exists?
moments.summary: Before investing in paid growth, ensure fundamentals are solid: fast website, search engine discoverability, and a strong baseline user experience.

moments.speaker: Meltem Kuran Berkowitz
moments.text: You can't run a successful paid ads program if you have a website that's loading in four plus seconds. So really going back to the basics and starting from a good experience at the core and then expanding step-by-step from there.
moments.summary: Paid advertising fails when the underlying product experience is broken, so fix the basics before scaling spend.

moments.speaker: Meltem Kuran Berkowitz
moments.text: Only after all of those questions are answered, should you then consider, do I have money? Can I put it behind some paid ads to make sure people come to my website?
moments.summary: Paid acquisition should be the last step in growth, not the first, after organic foundations are established.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to JTBD | Bob Moesta (co-creator of the framework)
podcasts.description: A guide to JTBD, covering product design, B2B products, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Bob Moesta
moments.text: I think one of the biggest misconceptions around Jobs to Be Done is this notion that it's pain and gain as opposed to context and outcome. When you hear somebody's story and it seems irrational, like we'll have people go, 'Oh my God, that's an anomaly.' But what you realize is that the context makes the irrational rational.
moments.summary: Jobs to Be Done is fundamentally about context and outcome, not pain and gain, and understanding context is what makes seemingly irrational user behavior make sense.

moments.speaker: Bob Moesta
moments.text: The moment you hear a story, you go, 'I can't believe that,' nine times out of 10, it's because you don't have the rest of the story. And so part of it's being able to understand the rest of that context that would drive somebody to say, 'Why would somebody cut their arm off?'
moments.summary: When user behavior seems irrational, it almost always means you are missing critical context about their situation.

moments.speaker: Bob Moesta
moments.text: That's what we're trying to do is find, where will people change behavior?
moments.summary: The core goal of Jobs to Be Done research is identifying the precise conditions under which people will actually change their behavior.

podcasts.title: Monetizing passions, scaling marketplaces, and stories from a creator economy vet | Camille Hearst (Spotify, Patreon, Apple, YouTube)
podcasts.description: Monetizing passions, scaling marketplaces, and stories from a creator economy vet, covering consumer products, pricing decisions, and product design.

moments.speaker: Camille Hearst
moments.text: Those Steve Jobs' lore was that if you were in an elevator with him, you better be prepared to talk about what you do at the company because he had a habit of getting in the elevator and looking at you and saying, 'What do you do? What do you do here?' And there were also rumors that people who had not given him a good answer, that ended up being their last day at Apple.
moments.summary: Steve Jobs' legendary elevator test at Apple demanded every employee clearly articulate their contribution, setting a high bar for organizational clarity.

moments.speaker: Camille Hearst
