moments.text: So there was someone who worked in my department before I got there who got in an elevator and looked up and Steve was approaching him and so he went to press the button to open the door and accidentally pressed the one to close the door. And it was like frantically pressing the button, trying to open the door, but accidentally pressing the closed door button and the elevator going to its destination.
moments.summary: The terror of Steve Jobs' elevator encounters was so intense that one Apple employee accidentally trapped himself in the elevator trying to escape.

moments.speaker: Camille Hearst
moments.text: And apparently he got off and just bolted straight up, ran down the hallway.
moments.summary: The anecdote of an Apple employee fleeing down the hallway after an accidental elevator ride with Steve Jobs captures the intensity of Apple's culture.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to Martech | Austin Hay (Reforge, Ramp, Runway)
podcasts.description: Martech, covering growth systems, strategic decision-making, and product design.

moments.speaker: Austin Hay
moments.text: From 2010 to 2020, we had the golden years of deterministic matching where it was very easy to run an ad and understand with precision who installed the app. You can't do that anymore. So, what that means is these ad networks are becoming more complex, sophisticated, and interesting, right at the same time that it's harder for marketers to really understand how they're spending money.
moments.summary: The golden age of deterministic ad attribution is over, forcing marketers to work with probabilistic data and more complex decision-making.

moments.speaker: Austin Hay
moments.text: I am paying a lot of attention to how marketers make decisions with probabilistic data because most of the work that I'm doing now is actually saying, well, given that we don't have deterministic data about a certain audience or where somebody came from, how can I find other information that will create a model for 30% of the population and we can use that to extrapolate to a hundred.
moments.summary: Modern marketing requires building probabilistic models from partial data to make decisions, a fundamentally different skill than deterministic tracking.

moments.speaker: Austin Hay
moments.text: On our creative group, we can come up with mockups, in literally, 1% of the time that it took. And so you still have to understand what questions to ask of the AI and be capable of iterating, but these rough drafts that you might show the artwork of to a client to say, 'Do we like this more or do we like this more?' That's AI generated.
moments.summary: AI has reduced creative mockup time to 1% of what it was, shifting the marketer's role from production to strategic questioning and iteration.

podcasts.title: Relentless curiosity, radical accountability, and HubSpot’s winning growth formula | Christopher Miller (VP of Product, Growth and AI)
podcasts.description: Relentless curiosity, radical accountability, and HubSpot’s winning growth formula, covering growth systems, team leadership, and pricing decisions.

moments.speaker: Christopher Miller
moments.text: We approached the team who owned it and we were like, 'Are you all working on this?' They were like, 'Nah, we're working on a bunch of other stuff.' We were like, 'Can we take this?' They were like, 'Sure, if you want it.' And so, we took it and immediately blew it up.
moments.summary: HubSpot's growth team found its biggest early win by taking ownership of a self-service revenue line that nobody else wanted.

moments.speaker: Christopher Miller
moments.text: That attitude of saying that every problem is our problem and radical accountability and ownership mentality helped us find opportunities that maybe the business wasn't explicitly asking us to solve, but we were able to triangulate why it might be important for the business for us to solve it.
moments.summary: Radical accountability and treating every problem as your own helps growth teams discover high-impact opportunities the business hasn't identified yet.

moments.speaker: Christopher Miller
moments.text: When you do that, we look hungry, so let's keep feeding us, right?
moments.summary: Growth teams that proactively grab and solve unclaimed problems earn more resources and trust from the organization.

podcasts.title: Velocity over everything: How Ramp became the fastest-growing SaaS startup of all time | Geoff Charles (VP of Product)
podcasts.description: How Ramp became the fastest-growing SaaS startup of all time, covering team leadership, product design, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Geoff Charles
moments.text: When I joined, we were about 10-ish folks, about eight engineers, and in three months, we built a competitor to Amex. Six months after that, we built a competitor to Expensify, both publicly traded companies.
moments.summary: Ramp built competitors to Amex and Expensify in under nine months with fewer than 10 people, demonstrating extreme product velocity.

moments.speaker: Geoff Charles
moments.text: We hit a hundred million in annual revenue. I think we were under at that point, 50 total in the R&D department, less than four engineers and three PMs. And then we started expanding into accounts payable. It was three engineers, one designer, one PM three months, and they hit out of the park.
moments.summary: Ramp reached $100M ARR with under 50 R&D people, and launched a billion-dollar accounts payable product with just five people in three months.

moments.speaker: Geoff Charles
moments.text: And that product is moving in billions of dollars a year.
moments.summary: Ramp's accounts payable product, built by a five-person team in three months, grew to process billions annually.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to A/B testing | Ronny Kohavi (Airbnb, Microsoft, Amazon)
podcasts.description: A/B testing, covering measurement and analysis, pricing decisions, and product design.

moments.speaker: Ronny Kohavi
moments.text: I'm very clear that I'm a big fan of test everything, which is any code change that you make, any feature that you introduce has to be in some experiment. Because again, I've observed this sort of surprising result that even small bug fixes, even small changes can sometimes have surprising, unexpected impact.
moments.summary: Every code change, no matter how small, should be tested through experimentation because even minor bug fixes can have surprising and unexpected impacts.

moments.speaker: Ronny Kohavi
moments.text: You have to allocate sometimes to these high risk, high reward ideas. We're going to try something that's most likely to fail. But if it does win, it's going to be a home run. And you have to be ready to understand and agree that most will fail.
moments.summary: Organizations must deliberately allocate experimentation capacity to high-risk, high-reward ideas while accepting that 80% will fail.

moments.speaker: Ronny Kohavi
moments.text: It's amazing how many times I've seen people come up with new designs or a radical new idea. And they believe in it, and that's okay. I'm just cautioning them all the time to say, 'If you go for something big, try it out, but be ready to fail 80% of the time.'
moments.summary: Even the most brilliant product people should expect their big, bold ideas to fail 80% of the time in A/B tests.

podcasts.title: The 10 traits of great PMs, how AI will impact your product, and Slack’s product development process | Noah Weiss (Slack, Foursquare, Google)
podcasts.description: The 10 traits of great PMs, how AI will impact your product, and Slack’s product development process, covering product design, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Noah Weiss
moments.text: We have this mental metaphor that we talk a lot about, getting to the next hill. The actual wording is 'Take bigger boulder bets.' I think teams can often get lost crawling up that hill, not realizing that there's a huge, incredibly beautiful range behind it.
moments.summary: Slack encourages teams to take bigger boulder bets and look beyond incremental hill-climbing to find transformative opportunities on the horizon.

moments.speaker: Noah Weiss
moments.text: We did that with a lot of these native audiovisual products like huddles and clips really in the pandemic because our customers were demanding it from us. I think in the AI space, we're trying to hear from customers, what do you wish Slack could do if it had these new superpowers?
moments.summary: Slack incubates new product areas by listening to customer demands and spinning up small teams to prototype before scaling.

moments.speaker: Noah Weiss
moments.text: Let's incubate a couple teams or prototype, give them space to run and pilot and then get something to launch that's amazing. Blows people away. That's the formula that we've seen.
moments.summary: Slack's formula for breakthrough products is to incubate small teams with space to prototype and pilot until they build something that blows people away.

podcasts.title: LinkedIn’s product evolution and the art of building complex systems | Hari Srinivasan (LinkedIn)
podcasts.description: LinkedIn’s product evolution and the art of building complex systems, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and career development.

moments.speaker: Hari Srinivasan
moments.text: It was March 2020 and we were just watching COVID hit. It was just this heartbreaking moment where in the feed you were seeing all these people, by no fault of their own, starting to post that they've lost their job.
moments.summary: LinkedIn witnessed the human impact of COVID in real time through its feed as millions of people posted about sudden job losses.

moments.speaker: Hari Srinivasan
moments.text: People are used to looking for certain particular titles, and they didn't start realizing other people could do this job. We made a pretty big push in something we call skills-first hiring. This was the idea that we could translate people's experiences into a set of skills.
moments.summary: LinkedIn pioneered skills-first hiring to help the job market rebalance by matching people on capabilities rather than just previous job titles.

moments.speaker: Hari Srinivasan
moments.text: I think that the job market is rebalancing, but it's being done, the pathways are being done in a very different way that seems to be maybe a change that holds through these ups and downs. That'll be very interesting to see.
moments.summary: The shift to skills-first hiring may represent a permanent structural change in how the job market matches talent to opportunities.

podcasts.title: M&A, competition, pricing, and investing | Julia Schottenstein (dbt Labs)
podcasts.description: M&A, competition, pricing, and investing, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Julia Schottenstein
moments.text: M&A is always about creating plan Bs. And the way I would think about it is for any one company, there's only ever two to three buyers that find what you're building to be extremely strategic.
moments.summary: Successful M&A strategy recognizes that only two to three potential buyers will ever find your company truly strategic, so focus on them.

moments.speaker: Julia Schottenstein
moments.text: The strategy that I would do in how do you get noticed is I would figure out the area that you bring a competitive advantage. And I would inflict pain on that potential buyer. Make it impossible for them to not notice you.
moments.summary: To attract acquisition interest, identify your competitive advantage and use it to inflict competitive pain on potential buyers until they cannot ignore you.

moments.speaker: Julia Schottenstein
moments.text: I see a lot of founders get this wrong and they prematurely will shut down a conversation or they won't talk to an incumbent or a potential future buyer because they take too competitive of a stance. But that's a mistake because M&A is all about creating plan Bs and you don't want to shut that door down prematurely.
moments.summary: Founders err by being too adversarial with potential acquirers, when keeping doors open and maintaining optionality is essential for M&A success.

podcasts.title: How to ask the right questions, project confidence, and win over skeptics | Paige Costello (Asana, Intercom, Intuit)
podcasts.description: Ask the right questions, project confidence, and win over skeptics, covering team leadership, product design, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Paige Costello
moments.text: The thing I would say is bring the insight. Know thy customer. Know thy market. Know thy competitors. Know thy numbers. Know thy product.
moments.summary: Winning trust as a young product leader comes down to one thing: showing up with deep knowledge of customers, market, competitors, numbers, and product.

moments.speaker: Paige Costello
moments.text: Your brain is so accustomed to having a scarcity mindset as opposed to creating alternative options or seeing a different path. Effectively, there's this notion of, 'How might the opposite be true?' The moment I challenged myself and said, 'How might the opposite be true?' my shoulders dropped.
moments.summary: Asking 'how might the opposite be true?' breaks through scarcity mindset and opens up creative solutions that reduce anxiety.

moments.speaker: Paige Costello
moments.text: I felt more relaxed. I was like, 'Oh, yeah, I can do both. It will be fine.'
moments.summary: Challenging your default assumptions with their opposite creates space for seeing that both paths can coexist.

podcasts.title: Building minimum lovable products, stories from WeWork and Airbnb, and thriving as a PM | Jiaona Zhang (Webflow, WeWork, Airbnb, Dropbox)
podcasts.description: Building minimum lovable products, stories from WeWork and Airbnb, and thriving as a PM, covering team leadership, product design, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Jiaona Zhang
moments.text: I think it's really important to become really good at and also known for something. You could be known for shepherding the most complex launches because you're just so good at quarterbacking.
moments.summary: Building a recognizable specialty as a PM creates a flywheel where your reputation attracts increasingly important and challenging projects.

moments.speaker: Jiaona Zhang
moments.text: Find something that you can be really, really good at. And the reason I give that advice is because when you do that, you can crush the projects that you get because you're making a name for yourself, reputation, and then you are giving more responsibility.
moments.summary: Specializing deeply in one area creates a virtuous cycle of reputation, trust, and increasing responsibility.

moments.speaker: Jiaona Zhang
moments.text: People tend to flock and give responsibility to the people that are known for being excellent at something.
moments.summary: In organizations, responsibility flows to those who have established excellence in a specific domain.

podcasts.title: Lessons from scaling Ramp | Sri Batchu (Ramp, Instacart, Opendoor)
podcasts.description: Lessons from scaling Ramp, covering team leadership, growth systems, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Sri Batchu
moments.text: I do think there's actually a general path that most B2B companies take and should take. My view is you start off with founder-led sales, the early team needs to know how to actually sell.
moments.summary: B2B growth follows a predictable path from founder-led sales through low-cost marketing to paid acquisition, with each phase building on the last.

moments.speaker: Sri Batchu
moments.text: Then you hire your first couple of salespeople, then you start some very low cost targeted marketing efforts. So whether it's content, community, small scale events, and then PR, after all of that is when you start paid and brand effort.
moments.summary: The ideal B2B growth progression is founder sales, first salespeople, content and community, then PR, and only then paid marketing and brand.

moments.speaker: Sri Batchu
moments.text: The reason for the progression the way I've described it is the channels get more expensive as you go farther along and they get more effective as you understand more about your customers.
moments.summary: Growth channels should be adopted in order of increasing cost because customer understanding compounds over time, making expensive channels more effective later.

podcasts.title: Building high-performing teams | Melissa Tan (Webflow, Dropbox, Canva)
podcasts.description: Building high-performing teams, covering growth systems, team leadership, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Melissa Tan
moments.text: My aha moment of the value of first principles thinking was when I was at Dropbox. We would hire a ton of really smart people that had never done sales and had them do sales.
moments.summary: Dropbox found innovation by hiring brilliant people with zero sales experience, who invented novel go-to-market approaches because they had no preconceptions.

moments.speaker: Melissa Tan
moments.text: That's how we got our very innovative go-to-market motions because a lot of those people then moved into different functions at the company. They had all this context on who the user was.
moments.summary: Dropbox's innovative GTM motions emerged because inexperienced salespeople who learned the user deeply brought that context into other roles.

moments.speaker: Melissa Tan
moments.text: If you take people that are just super smart, they've never done it before, one advantage of that is they can innovate because I think they come in with, I don't know anything. Let me just figure this out.
moments.summary: Hiring smart people with no domain experience can drive innovation because they approach problems without inherited assumptions.

podcasts.title: Leveraging growth advisors, hiring well, mastering SEO, and honing your craft | Luc Levesque (Shopify, Meta, TripAdvisor)
podcasts.description: Leveraging growth advisors, hiring well, mastering SEO, and honing your craft, covering team leadership, product design, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Luc Levesque
moments.text: We talk about the 10X engineer and we don't really talk about the 10X growth advisor or 10X growth person, but the same dynamic applies. You could argue it applies even more because the right growth advisor can have literally company changing impact.
moments.summary: A 10X growth advisor can be even more impactful than a 10X engineer because a single insight can change a company's entire trajectory.

moments.speaker: Luc Levesque
moments.text: Something I've experienced several times in hindsight when you're like, 'Okay, here's the needle in the haystack.' And then it's implemented and you can see hundreds of percentages, sometimes over a thousand percent lift when you get it right.
moments.summary: The right growth advisor finding the right lever can produce 100-1000% lifts, making it one of the highest-leverage hires a company can make.

moments.speaker: Luc Levesque
moments.text: It's one of those weird disciplines where the right person at the right time can literally say a sentence that changes the trajectory of your company. You can't say that for a lot of different disciplines, but this is one of them.
moments.summary: Growth is unique among disciplines in that a single sentence from the right person at the right time can redirect an entire company.

podcasts.title: Building a long and meaningful career | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
podcasts.description: Building a long and meaningful career, covering career development, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Nikhyl Singhal
moments.text: When I was a kid and I was growing up in the Midwest, entertainment was like going to the dog tracks. The way that they motivated the dogs was they had these fake rabbits. These tails would go around faster than the dogs, which would then motivate the dogs to go around in circles. And what was interesting is the moment that the dogs, if they accidentally touched the rabbit, they would never run again.
moments.summary: Like greyhounds chasing a mechanical rabbit, people who achieve a singular career goal without a next one often lose their drive entirely.

moments.speaker: Nikhyl Singhal
moments.text: Your listeners are spending time focused on like, 'Well, one day I will be X. I will be that vice president. I will have more money.' But they don't think about what happens next. What's the second thing? What's your career next look like?
moments.summary: Most ambitious people fixate on their next milestone without planning what comes after, setting themselves up for a motivation crisis when they arrive.

moments.speaker: Nikhyl Singhal
moments.text: Otherwise, you'll keep working because you know nothing else to do, but you'll be sadder or you'll find ways to create war when peace is needed.
moments.summary: Without a sense of purpose beyond the next promotion, high achievers either stagnate in joyless work or manufacture unnecessary conflict.

podcasts.title: Moving fast and navigating uncertainty | Jeremy Henrickson (Rippling, Coinbase)
podcasts.description: Moving fast and navigating uncertainty, covering product design, team leadership, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Jeremy Henrickson
moments.text: It's very, very tempting to float up here as a leader and say, 'Hey, you take that hill over there. You guys do this over here.' When in fact, where you really learn where the challenges are, or the problems or the successes is by just being there with the people in the trenches on one of the things.
moments.summary: Leaders learn the most by getting into the trenches on the hardest problem rather than directing from above.

moments.speaker: Jeremy Henrickson
moments.text: Whichever one seems hardest or most complicated. And so I try to do that as often as I can, and I found that I always learn a lot by going through that detailed exercise.
moments.summary: Deliberately choosing to dive into the hardest, most complicated problem yields the most learning for a leader.

moments.speaker: Jeremy Henrickson
moments.text: Where you really learn where the challenges are, or the problems or the successes is by just being there with the people in the trenches.
moments.summary: Real organizational understanding comes from being present in the work alongside your team, not from reviewing dashboards and status updates.

podcasts.title: Storytelling with Nancy Duarte: How to craft compelling presentations and tell a story that sticks
podcasts.description: Craft compelling presentations and tell a story that sticks, covering product design, team leadership, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Nancy Duarte
moments.text: A lot of people think that the only time you really need to present well is when you have a big stage talk and you make the big investment in the script. I'll tell you a dirty little secret. I can get my husband to do chores for me on the weekends with a real quick, what is, what could be new bliss.
moments.summary: The 'what is vs. what could be' storytelling framework is not just for big presentations; it works in any moment of influence, even casual conversations.

moments.speaker: Nancy Duarte
moments.text: The ability to just have that contrast as a framework in your brain during a meeting, on a phone call, any moment of influence, like literally it works. It works in any format.
moments.summary: Contrasting the current state with a compelling future state is a universal persuasion framework that works in meetings, calls, and everyday interactions.

moments.speaker: Nancy Duarte
moments.text: People know I'll take a swag at data and pretend it's real. I had a president who took us a whack at that number in 2014, and he said at that time it was 225,000, and that was almost 10 years ago. So I can't even tell you, in 35 years we have thousands of projects.
moments.summary: Duarte's firm has helped craft over 225,000 presentations across 35 years, giving her unparalleled pattern recognition in what makes stories stick.

podcasts.title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
podcasts.description: The power of strategic narrative, covering B2B products, strategic decision-making, and product design.

moments.speaker: Andy Raskin
moments.text: The way I learned how to pitch in business school, and I think the way most people did is what I call the arrogant doctor. So you have a problem, a pain, I have a solution, a treatment, and I'm going to tell you why it's better than all the other treatments.
moments.summary: The traditional pitch structure of 'you have a problem, I have a solution' is flawed because it positions you as an arrogant doctor prescribing treatment.

moments.speaker: Andy Raskin
moments.text: Every movie starts with some kind of shift in the world, and I call this shift the shift from the old game to a new game. The archetypal example of this in the business world is what Benioff did with Salesforce. He comes in and he says, 'Hey, software is over and there's this new world called the cloud.'
moments.summary: Great strategic narratives start by naming a shift from an old game to a new game, like Salesforce declaring 'software is over' and the cloud is the future.

moments.speaker: Andy Raskin
moments.text: This structure really is about defining a movement, and that's very different from, 'Hey, I'm going to solve your problem.'
moments.summary: The most powerful pitch structure defines a movement people want to join rather than a problem they need solved.

podcasts.title: Gustav Söderström
podcasts.description: Next question, what's something relatively minor you've changed in your product development process that's had a tremendous impact on your team's ability to execute, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Gustav Soderstrom
moments.text: The internet started with curation, often user curation. So you took something, some good like people or books or music, and you digitize it and you put it online and then you ask users to curate it. And that was your Facebook, Spotify, and so forth.
moments.summary: The internet evolved through three eras: user curation, algorithmic recommendation, and now AI-powered generation, each requiring fundamental product rethinking.

moments.speaker: Gustav Soderstrom
moments.text: After a while, the world switched from curation to recommendation, where instead of people doing that work, you had algorithms. And that was a big change that required us and others to actually rethink the entire user experience and sometimes the business model as well.
moments.summary: The shift from curation to recommendation forced companies like Spotify to fundamentally redesign their user experience and business models.

moments.speaker: Gustav Soderstrom
moments.text: I think what we're entering now is we're going from curation to recommendation to generation. And I suspect it will be as big of a shift that you will eventually have to rethink your products. No one really knows yet.
moments.summary: The generative AI era will force product teams to rethink interfaces and experiences as fundamentally as the recommendation era did.

podcasts.title: Lessons on building product sense, navigating AI, optimizing the first mile, and making it through the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe, Behance)
podcasts.description: Building product sense, navigating AI, optimizing the first mile, and making it through the messy middle, covering product design, growth systems, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Scott Belsky
moments.text: I basically say, 'How much conviction do you have in the solution you're building?' I know in the beginning, before you knew all you know now, you had tons of conviction. That's what caused you to leave your job. Now knowing all you know, do you have more or less conviction in the problem and the solution you're building?
moments.summary: The best test for whether to continue a struggling startup is whether your conviction has increased or decreased with everything you have learned.

moments.speaker: Scott Belsky
moments.text: Some people are like, 'Oh, Scott, I mean, I have more conviction. All that I've learned, all the validation I've received from customers, we just haven't figured it out yet.' For those people, I'm like, 'You know what? You're just in the messy middle. Stick with it.'
moments.summary: If your conviction has grown despite setbacks, you are in the messy middle and should keep going; if it has shrunk, it may be time to move on.

moments.speaker: Scott Belsky
moments.text: Oftentimes, I'll hear, 'Honestly, if I knew then what I know now, I would not have done this. Holy shit.'
moments.summary: Founders who admit they would not start again knowing what they know now have their answer about whether to continue.

podcasts.title: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles | Ayo Omojola (Carbon Health, Cash App)
podcasts.description: Frameworks for product differentiation, team building, and thinking from first principles, covering product design, consumer products, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Ayo Omojola
moments.text: Cash App, as a team we really cared about what we could do that was different and better than what else existed in-market. Being different is not enough, because it's very easy to build a thing that's different from what exists today, because you just have to look at what exists today and build something else.
moments.summary: Being different alone is easy and being better alone is insufficient; a product must be different and better in ways that genuinely matter to end users.

moments.speaker: Ayo Omojola
moments.text: Being better is not enough, because it's also easy to say, 'Hey, I'm going to make this thing better, and just charge you more money for it.' It has to be better than what exists today in a way that matters to the end user.
moments.summary: Incremental improvement that costs more is not real differentiation; the improvement must matter viscerally to the user in their daily experience.

moments.speaker: Ayo Omojola
moments.text: For us for a long time it was when someone says, 'Hey, why are you betting on Venmo?' I'd be like, 'Try and send me a dollar that I can use now,' and there was only one app you could do it with.
moments.summary: Cash App's killer differentiator was instant usability of received money, a simple demo that no competitor could match.

podcasts.title: Mastering paid growth | Jonathan Becker (Thrive Digital)
podcasts.description: Mastering paid growth, covering strategic decision-making, growth systems, and product design.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Becker
moments.text: There's a lot of different ways that we are beginning to use AI to do more with less. The effect ultimately that we've seen from a human capital point of view is displacement. We have more people now than we've ever had, but the nature of the work that they do is more strategic.
moments.summary: AI in paid marketing has not reduced headcount but displaced tactical work, shifting human effort toward strategy, modeling, and creative thinking.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Becker
moments.text: It's more about modeling, validation, asking the right questions, being focused around creative levers. And less so the trench work of implementation and bid modifiers at the keyword level on Google search.
moments.summary: The modern paid growth marketer's job has shifted from manual bid optimization to strategic modeling and creative experimentation.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Becker
moments.text: On our creative group, we can come up with mockups, in literally, 1% of the time that it took. And so you still have to understand what questions to ask of the AI and be capable of iterating.
moments.summary: AI reduces creative mockup production time by 99%, but the human skill of asking the right questions and iterating remains irreplaceable.

podcasts.title: Building a culture of excellence | David Singleton (CTO of Stripe)
podcasts.description: Building a culture of excellence, covering product design, team leadership, and career development.

moments.speaker: David Singleton
moments.text: The way we think about product development at Stripe, it really is to find the correct set of early users to kind of co-create the product with. Maybe the best example of that is Stripe billing.
moments.summary: Stripe builds products by co-creating with carefully selected early users through shared Slack channels and regular feedback loops.

moments.speaker: David Singleton
moments.text: We decided to co-create the product with them. So we had shared Slack channels, we'd actually show them product on a very regular basis, get their feedback on it. And only when that original Alpha group was super, super happy with the product did we then think it might be ready to go to a broader audience.
moments.summary: Stripe only ships products broadly when the alpha co-creation group is genuinely delighted, ensuring quality before scale.

moments.speaker: David Singleton
moments.text: That means that every engineer building product at Stripe really has many of the attributes and will exercise many of the attributes that you'll often find in PMs in other companies.
moments.summary: Stripe's co-creation model means engineers develop deep product sense and customer empathy that other companies typically only expect from PMs.

podcasts.title: How to achieve hypergrowth in your business and career | Carilu Dietrich (Atlassian, Miro, Segment, 1Password)
podcasts.description: Achieve hypergrowth in your business and career, covering career development, growth systems, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Carilu Dietrich
moments.text: In order to get hypergrowth, you have to have organic, inbound, and viral word of mouth. You can't pay enough to grow at those rates and have a viable company. The biggest thing is an amazing product that people love to use.
moments.summary: Hypergrowth is impossible through paid acquisition alone; it requires organic word of mouth driven by a genuinely amazing product.

moments.speaker: Carilu Dietrich
moments.text: Hypergrowth companies go through the stages of growth that would take other companies five years or 10 years. They're jumping. And so they really need to keep hiring 2X and 3X leaders who have seen the next stage of growth, because it's going to be here before you know it.
moments.summary: Hypergrowth companies must continuously hire leaders who have already seen the next stage of scale because growth stages compress from years to months.

moments.speaker: Carilu Dietrich
moments.text: ChatGPT is the most hypergrowth product that we've seen in history potentially, because people are so excited to use it and it's working in interesting ways.
moments.summary: ChatGPT exemplifies hypergrowth driven purely by product excitement and utility, becoming potentially the fastest-growing product in history.

podcasts.title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
podcasts.description: Outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Varun Parmar
moments.text: Every single day, every single time somebody is pushing your code to production and you're releasing a feature or an enhancement, you are making the product better or you're making the product worse, but the products never remain same.
moments.summary: Every release is either a positive or negative chess move against competitors; products never stay the same, they always get better or worse.

moments.speaker: Varun Parmar
moments.text: With every release that your competitor is making and every release that you're making, you are either making chess points, moves against them, positive points, or you're going negative.
moments.summary: Product development is a daily chess match where every deployment either gains or loses ground against your competition.

moments.speaker: Varun Parmar
moments.text: I think that framework, it actually drives an insane amount of clarity in terms of what you're doing and what the impact is going to be.
moments.summary: Framing every release as a chess move against competitors creates intense clarity about priorities and expected impact.

podcasts.title: How to make better decisions and build a joyful career | Ada Chen Rekhi (Notejoy, LinkedIn, SurveyMonkey)
podcasts.description: Making better decisions and building a joyful career, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and career development.

moments.speaker: Ada Chen Rekhi
moments.text: It's a terrible outcome to wake up one day and be late career and feel trapped because you have a certain lifestyle or certain expectations of the people around you that you have to go work this job, but then you look at yourself in the mirror and you're not happy going in there.
moments.summary: The worst career outcome is reaching late-career feeling trapped by lifestyle expectations while being deeply unhappy with your work.

moments.speaker: Ada Chen Rekhi
moments.text: I think that's a terrible trap that we should all try to avoid as we navigate our career paths and find the thing that's most optimal for us. Which is usually a mix of career success, but also meaningfulness and alignment in the work that we're doing with our values.
moments.summary: Optimal career paths blend success with meaningfulness and values alignment, and navigating toward that mix requires intentional effort.

moments.speaker: Ada Chen Rekhi
moments.text: Which is usually a mix of career success, but also meaningfulness and alignment in the work that we're doing with our values.
moments.summary: Career optimization is finding the intersection of external success and internal alignment with your personal values.

podcasts.title: How to build a cult-like brand | Laura Modi (Bobbie)
podcasts.description: Building a cult-like brand, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Laura Modi
moments.text: She goes, 'Here's the dilemma. We are depleting inventory far quicker than our ability to replenish and the customers keep coming.' You sit there and your first reaction is 'This is great. We're growing.' And she's like, 'It's not that great, actually.'
moments.summary: Bobbie faced the paradox of dangerous hypergrowth where customer acquisition outpaced inventory, threatening existing customers who depended on the product.

moments.speaker: Laura Modi
moments.text: We are going to run out of product for the babies that are on Bobbie today. We have about six days before we get to a place where we won't be able to serve those who've already made a commitment to Bobbie.
moments.summary: Bobbie had six days before they would fail existing customers whose babies depended on their formula, forcing a wrenching decision.

moments.speaker: Laura Modi
moments.text: So we need to turn off our site and stop growing the business.
moments.summary: Bobbie made the counterintuitive decision to shut down their website and halt all growth to protect babies already relying on their formula.

podcasts.title: Product lessons from Waymo | Shweta Shrivastava (Waymo, Amazon, Cisco)
podcasts.description: Product lessons from Waymo, covering product design, product strategy and execution, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Shweta Shrivastava
moments.text: We're using a lot of human driving data to train our deep models. So it's important to make sure that the behavior of the car doesn't seem robotic, it can feel quite unnatural. And from the get-go, we focused on building a fully autonomous system. So it's important to have that familiarity, that trust, building with the riders where they're not daunted by technology, they don't feel like they're sitting in our walk. It has to feel very human-like, but in a good way.
moments.summary: Waymo trains on human driving data to make autonomous vehicles feel natural and trustworthy rather than robotic.

moments.speaker: Shweta Shrivastava
moments.text: The concept of MVP, which is so widely popular in the SaaS product management world or product management world, in general, has a whole new meaning here at Waymo when you work in a product like this because safety is on top of mind for all of us and can't really cut corners on safety. The MVP bar itself for safety is extremely high for us.
moments.summary: At Waymo, the MVP concept still applies but the safety bar is fundamentally higher than in typical software products.

moments.speaker: Shweta Shrivastava
moments.text: It's also very important to know what you're not building. And this one is not only in big companies, I would say even in startups, it's extremely important to know what you're not building because you could very easily get swayed by customer X telling you to do this, customer Y telling you to do that. And a product that tries to be all things to all people usually doesn't end up going anywhere.
moments.summary: Knowing what not to build is just as critical as knowing what to build, because a product that tries to serve everyone ends up serving no one.

moments.speaker: Shweta Shrivastava
moments.text: Challenging your own assumptions. Listening with an open mind but then are you proactively trying to challenge your own assumptions is extremely important. As a big enough product manager as well as a seasoned product leader, if you're not doing enough of that, then I think you might not be listening well. If there's no conflict, if there's no contention, then something is missing.
moments.summary: Product leaders must proactively challenge their own assumptions -- if there is no conflict or contention, something important is being missed.

moments.speaker: Shweta Shrivastava
moments.text: The way to get promoted is to not want it too badly. It is about you have to focus on the impact. It's about having an impact and then doing what is right for the business. So not sort of optimizing things for your promotion. If you try to maneuver that too much, it becomes visible and it's not a positive signal to the organization.
moments.summary: The best path to promotion is focusing on business impact rather than optimizing for the promotion itself.

podcasts.title: Driving alignment and urgency within teams, work-life balance, and the changing PM landscape | Nikita Miller (The Knot, Trello)
podcasts.description: Driving alignment and urgency within teams, work-life balance, and the changing PM landscape, covering team leadership, product design, and cross-functional alignment.

moments.speaker: Nikita Miller
moments.text: And many of the companies that I've either worked with or advised, coached over the past few years, it was all about outcomes. Everyone was, 'Outcomes, outcomes, outcomes,' which is right. But some folks, myself included at certain points, swung way too far on the outcomes train and forgot that output is an indicator of that. If you're also not shipping a lot of things to market quickly enough, then it just doesn't matter that much.
moments.summary: Teams often over-index on outcome-focused thinking and forget that shipping velocity and output are essential indicators of achieving those outcomes.

moments.speaker: Nikita Miller
moments.text: It usually starts with individuals identifying very clearly early on roles and responsibilities. The exercise I often do is I generally have an idea of what I think the roles and responsibilities are, but the exercise especially with leaders in an org is to have them sit down and write them for each other. I as a product leader, I'm going to write down what I think the expectations of my engineering manager are, of my designer, of my data. And then we look at it together and arrive at essentially a contract with one another.
moments.summary: Cross-functional leaders should write down their expectations of each other's roles, then align on a shared contract of responsibilities.

moments.speaker: Nikita Miller
moments.text: A lot of that now sits with engineering managers, which is a little bit different from how it was when I started in product where actually, a lot of that was put on PMs. And some of you might recall, it caused a lot of issues with product managers because they were the ones that were constantly like, 'What's happening in the sprints? What didn't make it? Why didn't it?' Engineering managers are increasingly expected to be the ones that are actively making sure that sprint goals are met.
moments.summary: Project management responsibilities have shifted from PMs to engineering managers, a recent evolution that reduces PM burden and improves execution ownership.

moments.speaker: Nikita Miller
moments.text: Folks planning their weddings, think of it as an emotional, high stakes thing that you're hopefully going to do once, and so the pressure is really there. Not unlike product managers, ultimately wedding planning is this huge project where you have a bunch of stakeholders, friends, family. You need to manage multiple vendors, and the time horizon for a wedding once you're engaged is anywhere from 12 to 18 months.
moments.summary: Wedding planning and product management share surprising parallels: high-stakes projects with multiple stakeholders, vendors, and long time horizons.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to adding a PLG motion | Hila Qu (Reforge, GitLab)
podcasts.description: Adding a PLG motion, covering B2B products, growth systems, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Hila Qu
moments.text: PLG I always say is actually fundamentally DLG, data led growth. So when you give away your free product, what you want to get in exchange are two things. One is a broader reach because free product spread itself is lower barrier to entry. Two, you want to understand usage behavior of those free users, which features do they use and which features kind of correlates with a higher conversion rate, retention rate. If you don't have a foundation of data and understanding of how to analyze those data, you are giving away a free product for nothing.
moments.summary: Product-led growth is fundamentally data-led growth: without a data foundation to understand free user behavior, giving away a free product is worthless.

moments.speaker: Hila Qu
moments.text: A lot of the companies, if you go to their website, especially B2B companies, you'll see the biggest CTA is called book demo. They don't have anything else. The first step for you to do is submit a form and basically explain yourself to this company. That means the entry point to PLG is cut off. Instead, the first step is you need to either have a free product, free trial, some sort of a low barrier entry for anyone who stumble upon this product to give it a try.
moments.summary: Most B2B companies block their own PLG potential by making 'book a demo' the only entry point instead of offering a self-serve free trial.

moments.speaker: Hila Qu
moments.text: Think of it as the first time a user experienced value of your product. Facebook has this example from the early growth days -- you added 10 friends in seven days, you hit your aha moment. At GitLab we ended up with something along the line of two users, two features used in the first 14 days. Two users is talking about the team component -- whatever the first user is trying is so valuable, he or she is confident to invite another coworker to come in.
moments.summary: Every PLG product needs a defined aha moment metric; GitLab's was two users using two features in 14 days, indicating team collaboration value.

moments.speaker: Hila Qu
moments.text: Think about Miro, as an example. If you go through their activation experience and sign up to usage, they ask very limited questions, very targeted. They drop you, they ask about your use case, what are you here for? And they quickly gave you templates to get started. In maybe five minutes, you finish the entire journey from go to the website and sign up, answer a few questions, and you are already using the template they provided. That's time to value. That's a success.
moments.summary: Miro exemplifies great PLG activation by getting users from signup to using a relevant template in under five minutes.

podcasts.title: Taxi mafias, cash vaults, and 100% MoM growth: The story behind Southeast Asia’s biggest startup | Kevin Aluwi (Gojek)
podcasts.description: The story behind Southeast Asia’s biggest startup, covering product design, growth systems, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Kevin Aluwi
moments.text: In the early days of Gojek, there was a lot of resistance to our services. The most common form of that resistance was actually by motorcycle taxi mafia. These people would actually physically assault our drivers. We've had everything from bricks thrown at our drivers to knives and machetes being brandished at them. But instead of saying they're all contractors, let them sort it out, we actually hired private security to help extract them out of these sticky situations.
moments.summary: Gojek faced physical violence from motorcycle taxi mafias but invested in private security for drivers, building deep loyalty in their community.

moments.speaker: Kevin Aluwi
moments.text: I am kind of annoyed at how much super apps are being mentioned these days. All of the things that are really appealing -- lower customer acquisition costs, higher attach rates, higher retention, the ease of cross-selling -- all of these things sound great, but in reality, a lot of those benefits don't pan out. There needs to be a unifying concept across all of your services for your users to think about your product in a sensible way.
moments.summary: The super app strategy sounds great on pitch decks but often fails because services need a unifying concept that customers can intuitively understand.

moments.speaker: Kevin Aluwi
moments.text: I'm a very big believer that the two most important things in a consumer business are product and brand in that order. Brand as an afterthought is definitely one of the areas where I think there's a giant missed opportunity. For the first six months after launching our app, we had only raised about $2 million and our regional competitor had already raised 250. A big reason why we survived was that we built a great brand.
moments.summary: Brand is the second most important thing in consumer business after product, and Gojek's strong brand helped them survive against a competitor with 100x more funding.

moments.speaker: Kevin Aluwi
moments.text: I really don't like the idea of moats. I don't believe that any moats are durable over time. Eventually with enough time all moats can be crossed. One so-called moat that doesn't get talked about enough is the fact that you're able to do hard things because hard things are hard, and just simply doing things that are hard, as long as they create value to your customers, actually makes it harder for your competitors to win.
moments.summary: No moat is truly durable, but consistently doing hard things that create customer value is an underrated competitive advantage.

podcasts.title: Navigating comms and PR | Lulu Cheng Meservey (Substack, Activision Blizzard)
podcasts.description: Navigating comms and PR, covering product design, startup building, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Lulu Cheng Meservey
moments.text: I often say to find your audience's cultural erogenous zones. People have things that they either care about or don't, and you're not going to change that. It's a huge lift to try to change someone's worldview or their passions. It's a light lift to take the thing you want to talk about and just shape it to fit into their worldview. It's so much easier to take what they're passionate about and convince them that if they care about that then they should care about your thing.
moments.summary: Instead of trying to change what people care about, shape your message to connect with their existing passions and worldview.

moments.speaker: Lulu Cheng Meservey
moments.text: If you're a startup your enemy is the status quo. When you don't take risks, when you minimize risk by doing nothing, you're letting the status quo win. I always encourage people to try to make mistakes of commission rather than omission. Because if you make a mistake of commission you can observe it, you can learn from it. Whereas if you make mistakes of omission you're not observing, you're not learning, and you're maybe not even noticing opportunities slip by.
moments.summary: For startups, the real risk is inaction; mistakes of commission are far more valuable than mistakes of omission because they generate learning.

moments.speaker: Lulu Cheng Meservey
moments.text: If you decrease the surface area then with the same amount of force you can apply more pressure. This is a basic equation for physics, but it's also true of communications. If you decrease the surface area and don't try to appeal to everybody with everything, and you're targeting exactly whom you're talking to and sharpening your message to a point, then with the same amount of effort you're able to make more of an impact.
moments.summary: The physics equation for pressure applies to communications: narrow your audience and sharpen your message to maximize impact with limited resources.

moments.speaker: Lulu Cheng Meservey
moments.text: Something that the Substack founders have always done so well is propagate product updates out in concentric circles. There's a general who said if there's a problem I look for it in concentric circles going back to my own desk. If you want to spread something you go out in concentric circles starting from your own desk. Employees, co-founders, executives, investors, power users, and out from there. You can't skip a circle.
moments.summary: Spread messages in concentric circles from employees outward to power users and beyond, because each circle takes cues from the one inside it.

podcasts.title: Competing with giants: An inside look at how The Browser Company builds product | Josh Miller (CEO)
podcasts.description: Josh Miller is the CEO and co-founder of The Browser Company, where he helped build Arc, my go-to web browser. In today’s episode, we get an inside look at the unique structure and values of The Browser Company and how their company culture has helped them land some of the best talent in tech.

moments.speaker: Josh Miller
moments.text: We want people that show up to our company with some fire in their belly, something that they are out to do. For some people it may be UI Craft details. For other people, it may be achieving double the performance with a quarter of the engineering headcount. Everyone has something, but they show up with this heartfelt intensity. If you have a team that has heartfelt intensity and is there for a purpose, you give them a very exciting, ambitious product and get out of their way and they will do remarkable work.
moments.summary: The key to building great teams is hiring people with heartfelt intensity and personal purpose, then giving them ambitious work and getting out of their way.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to OKRs | Christina Wodtke (Stanford)
podcasts.description: OKRs, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and practical product lessons.

moments.speaker: Christina Wodtke
moments.text: People do not value celebrations enough. I've had CEOs who said, 'Well, it was the middle of the quarter, so we didn't start OKRs, but we did start Friday celebrations and oh my God, things are already changing.' The simple act of getting together and saying, 'What was the most awesome thing that happened this week?' It makes people feel like they're part of something really special.
moments.summary: Weekly celebrations where teams share wins are an undervalued practice that can transform team morale even before formal OKRs are implemented.

podcasts.title: Hot takes and techno-optimism from tech’s top power couple | Sriram and Aarthi
podcasts.description: Hot takes and techno-optimism from tech’s top power couple, covering growth systems, consumer products, and product design.

moments.speaker: Sriram Krishnan
moments.text: I hate Jobs-to-be-Done, I think it is a terrible framework. When you sign up for Facebook, Facebook knew it needed to get you to 10 friends in 14 days. So if you signed up, you'd get People You May Know. Then you'll have this person who just signed up, you go, 'Why am I seeing this person?' It's not because you need a friend, because they need a friend. Facebook made your experience slightly worse to make that person's experience slightly better. This was performing no job for you.
moments.summary: Jobs-to-be-Done fails to explain growth tactics like Facebook's People You May Know, which intentionally degraded one user's experience to improve a new user's retention.

moments.speaker: Sriram Krishnan
moments.text: I do think a lot of people trying to get ahead in their career, especially in technology, should just write cold emails, cold DMs, notes, put out content, and that leads to good things. In Elon's case, what wound up happening was he DMed me out of the blue. He saw something I'd written and wanted something from the company and I think he went through the org chart and he DMed me. I called him and we built up a relationship after that.
moments.summary: Career breakthroughs often come from putting content out into the world and being open to cold outreach, as Sriram's relationship with Elon started from a simple DM.

podcasts.title: Career frameworks, A/B testing mistakes, counterintuitive onboarding tips, selling to developers | Laura Schaffer (VP of Growth at Amplitude)
podcasts.description: Career frameworks, A/B testing mistakes, counterintuitive onboarding tips, selling to developers, covering team leadership, growth systems, and career development.

moments.speaker: Laura Schaffer
moments.text: We just asked for forgiveness and put these questions into the signup flow and ran as a test with a small group. And I'm fully expecting, 'Okay, this is going to hurt our numbers.' And we start to get the data for this thing. I'm not kidding, it improved conversion by like 5%, just improved signups. And it was one of those like, 'What? Okay, what is going on here?'
moments.summary: Adding onboarding questions to the signup flow counterintuitively improved conversion by 5%, challenging the assumption that more friction always hurts.

podcasts.title: Lessons from scaling Stripe | Claire Hughes Johnson (former COO of Stripe)
podcasts.description: Lessons from scaling Stripe, covering product design, team leadership, and career development.

moments.speaker: Claire Hughes Johnson
moments.text: If you're not sure who the decision maker is, one, it's probably you. And I'd rather you act that way than not because you're going to slow the whole company down. Follow a process and get it done, and don't forget to actually make a decision. Too many people get stuck and it makes your work terrible. What do we all care about? Progress, impact, momentum. Be a force for positive momentum and it will be actually a real career maker.
moments.summary: When you are unsure who the decision maker is, assume it is you and act accordingly, because getting stuck is far worse than making an imperfect decision.

podcasts.title: Gustaf Alstromer
podcasts.description: It's sort of like when you ask a founder the question, "How are things going?", it's so emotional for them to answer that question because it's never going well, right, covering startup building, career development, and product design.

moments.speaker: Gustaf Alstromer
moments.text: If I drill down what makes companies fail, it's quite simple. It's just like they don't talk to users, which means they don't find product market fit. And if they don't find product market fit, nothing else really matters. YC's headline is make things people want, and it's still true and it's always going to be true.
moments.summary: The primary reason companies fail is simple: they do not talk to users, which prevents them from finding product-market fit.

podcasts.title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
podcasts.description: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth, covering product strategy and execution, team leadership, and product design.

moments.speaker: Annie Pearl
moments.text: Strategy is really just an integrated set of choices that outline how you're going to win in whatever marketplace you choose. A good product strategy is going to answer questions like what's your winning aspiration? But maybe more importantly, where are you going to play? What are the markets you're going to go after? What are the segments? What are the personas? And then, how are you going to win with a target audience?
moments.summary: Product strategy is an integrated set of choices about where to play and how to win, not just aspirations but specific market, segment, and persona decisions.

podcasts.title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
podcasts.description: An inside look at how CNN builds product, covering product design, team leadership, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Upasna Gautam
moments.text: It happens all the time. That is the nature of breaking news. You have to be ready to pivot at the drop of a hat. I had a big working session planned with my users to do research with them, and breaking news breaks. When breaking news happens, they have to prioritize that over everything. You can be frustrated, absolutely it's frustrating. But we always have to have the ability to pivot and also have backup and buffers in those types of scenarios.
moments.summary: Building product at CNN requires constant readiness to pivot when breaking news strikes, demanding built-in buffers for research and development work.

podcasts.title: 10 lessons on bootstrapping a $200m business | Patrick Campbell (ProfitWell)
podcasts.description: Bootstrapping a $200m business, covering team leadership, product design, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Patrick Campbell
moments.text: Real professionals ship. At the end of the day, real... I don't care if you're a marketer or a product person, engineer, ops person, people ops, real professionals ship, and they ship at a pretty high frequency. In my opinion, your tempo framework is more important than your org design. If you've ever had a team that seems really smart but they're always planning or don't really ship a lot, you probably don't have enough alignment on what good looks like in terms of tempo.
moments.summary: Shipping tempo is more important than organizational design; if smart teams are always planning but rarely shipping, there is a tempo alignment problem.

podcasts.title: Understanding the role of product ops | Christine Itwaru (Pendo)
podcasts.description: Understanding the role of product ops, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Christine Itwaru
moments.text: Speaking as a former PM, I would not ever give up spending time with customers and watching their pain. That's how I fell in love with product was I saw my internal customer 12 years back now fighting with the keyboard, fighting with the mouse, and I was just like, 'Oh, my gosh. What's this guy doing?'
moments.summary: Direct observation of customer pain is irreplaceable; watching a user physically struggle with a product is what sparks the passion for building better solutions.

podcasts.title: Mastering onboarding | Lauryn Isford (Head of Growth at Airtable)
podcasts.description: Mastering onboarding, covering growth systems, measurement and analysis, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Lauryn Isford
moments.text: An activation rate that falls in a lower percentage range, maybe for most companies five to 15%, is better than one that falls in a higher percentage range because it means that there's likely much higher correlation with long-term retention and you're really working hard to get most of your users to reach a state that they're not reaching today.
moments.summary: A lower activation rate (5-15%) that strongly correlates with retention is better than a high one, because it means the metric is meaningfully predictive.

podcasts.title: Leading with empathy | Keith Yandell (DoorDash, Uber)
podcasts.description: Leading with empathy, covering team leadership, startup building, and career development.

moments.speaker: Keith Yandell
moments.text: Every business that you have heard of has gotten rejected by at least a handful of venture capitalists at one point or another. We were weeks of runway situation and had been told no by everyone. And it was just Tony's drive to keep going. The way he explains it is it's just the difference between a founder and a non-founder. If you're really a founder, you just have to find a way. It only takes one yes. But you got to keep going.
moments.summary: Every successful company has been rejected by VCs; the difference between founders and non-founders is the drive to keep going when everyone says no.

podcasts.title: AI and product management | Marily Nika (Meta, Google)
podcasts.description: AI and product management, covering AI product work, product design, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Marily Nika
moments.text: There is something called the shiny object trap, and I'm always telling people, 'Hey, don't do AI for the sake of doing AI.' Make sure there is a problem there. Make sure there is a pain point that needs to be solved in a smart way. Once you have identified what that problem is and what that very high-level solution is, then reach out and try to figure out how to actually implement it.
moments.summary: Avoid the shiny object trap with AI: start with a real customer pain point, not the technology, then figure out implementation.

podcasts.title: How to foster innovation and big thinking | Eeke de Milliano (Retool, Stripe)
podcasts.description: Foster innovation and big thinking, covering product design, team leadership, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Eeke de Milliano
moments.text: Process, by definition, is variance reducing. You're introducing it because you worry that the variance in your org is too high. The cost of that is while you are reducing the standard and bringing folks up to the average, you're also bringing other folks down to the average. And oftentimes, the folks you're bringing down are your highest performers, your most creative thinkers.
moments.summary: Process inherently reduces variance, which means it lifts underperformers but also constrains your highest performers and most creative thinkers.

podcasts.title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
podcasts.description: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy, covering product design, strategic decision-making, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Zoelle Egner
moments.text: Make sure someone rereads your email so that it sounds good. Invest in having a decent photo or illustration. If you have sample content for your productivity app, take the time to not have it be Jane Doe 12 times. Have it be references to your industry so that people are like, 'Oh, hey. That's a joke about Steve Jobs. I'm a designer. This person is thinking about me.' It tells that person the people who worked on this were thinking about me as a customer.
moments.summary: Small details like industry-specific sample content and polished copy signal to users that a product was built with them in mind, building brand trust.

podcasts.title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
podcasts.description: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey, covering product design, team leadership, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Vijay Iyengar
moments.text: The issue for us at the time was that we took people away from the investment in our core product to go do those other things. The trap there is that you leave yourself ripe for disruption in your core because someone else can outinvest you in that core. If you are the leader in some core product, you should continue to outinvest everyone else in that core and then invest the profits that come out of that core into the next venture. Invest profits and not people.
moments.summary: Companies should invest profits, not people, from their core product into new ventures; pulling talent away from the core leaves it vulnerable to disruption.

podcasts.title: What differentiates the highest-performing product teams | John Cutler (Amplitude, The Beautiful Mess)
podcasts.description: What differentiates the highest-performing product teams, covering team leadership, strategic decision-making, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: John Cutler
moments.text: Let's say you're a founder and you're trying to decide, should I invest more on processes, or should I invest more in people. The first thing is introspection. What do you believe in, really? What do you believe in, and what do the people around you believe in, and how can you be a coherent leader? You can nudge yourself a little bit away from your happy place, but you're not going to go super far. I think it starts with self-awareness and then that's how people form their authentic leadership vibe.
moments.summary: Choosing between investing in process vs people starts with honest introspection about your own beliefs, which forms the foundation of authentic leadership.

podcasts.title: How to price your product | Naomi Ionita (Menlo Ventures)
podcasts.description: Price your product, covering product design, pricing decisions, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Naomi Ionita
moments.text: Do not set it and forget it. I see companies do this, where they labor over designs and features and build this perfect product that's delightful to use. And then pricing's sort of plucked out of thin air, and then they don't revisit it. This was Evernote. Think about your pricing just like you do your roadmap. Every 6 to 12 months, there's probably something meaningful that you're launching. Treat that as an opportunity to revisit your monetization strategy.
moments.summary: Pricing should be revisited every 6-12 months alongside the product roadmap, not set once and forgotten like many companies including Evernote did.

podcasts.title: Yuhki Yamashata
podcasts.description: And so it's our quick way of being like, we're doing a product review, we on a pulse check, we drop it in and we're like, how are people feeling aligned, not aligned, covering product design, team leadership, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Yuhki Yamashita
moments.text: There's something controversial about this idea that everyone can see what you're doing or that multiple designers can be in the file at the same time. One of the first responses we saw to Figma was, 'If this is the future of design, I'm quitting.' And there's that tension of that narrative tension, but that is signal that you're part of this revolution and you're trying to change something.
moments.summary: Strong negative reactions to Figma's collaborative design were actually a positive signal -- narrative tension means you are changing something meaningful.

podcasts.title: Leveraging mentors to uplevel your career | Jules Walter (YouTube, Slack)
podcasts.description: Leveraging mentors to uplevel your career, covering career development, product strategy and execution, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Jules Walter
moments.text: If you give me feedback, I'll be like, 'Hey, thank you so much. This is super helpful,' because people are like, 'Oh, he actually likes the feedback.' Now, inside my heart might be melting. I'm like, 'Oh, I thought I got better at this.' But externally, I'm like, 'Hey, thank you,' and I mean it. I think that's the key that most people don't focus on. And if you get more feedback, then you'll just get better at the things.
moments.summary: Graciously receiving feedback externally, even when it stings internally, creates a virtuous cycle where people give you more feedback and you improve faster.

podcasts.title: Launching and growing a podcast | Chris Hutchins (All the Hacks, Wealthfront, Google)
podcasts.description: Launching and growing a podcast, covering product design, startup building, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Chris Hutchins
moments.text: Yes, there are four million podcasts. However, there are only about 150,000 podcasts that have had 10 episodes and have published in the last 10 days. So the easiest way to be in the top 5% is to just stick to it. If you just do an episode a week for 10 weeks, you're now in the top 4% of all podcasts that anyone has created.
moments.summary: Most podcasts never make it past a few episodes; simply publishing consistently for 10 weeks puts you in the top 4% of all podcasts ever created.

podcasts.title: Founder-led sales | Pete Kazanjy (Founding Sales, Atrium)
podcasts.description: Founder-led sales, covering B2B products, startup building, and product design.

moments.speaker: Pete Kazanjy
moments.text: The thing that I just like to encourage founders and product managers is just don't be afraid of sales. There's a lot of people out there who would love to tell you a story that it's magical or like, 'Oh, you've got to be a born seller,' and it's really not. Those people are just talking their book. Just getting good at those behaviors, it's going to benefit you in a myriad of ways.
moments.summary: Sales is a learnable skill, not an innate talent; founders and PMs should not be intimidated by the myth of the born salesperson.

podcasts.title: How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts (Notion, First Round Capital)
podcasts.description: How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business, covering product design, consumer products, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Camille Ricketts
moments.text: The way that you think about product market fit, you have to think about content market fit. What is it that they really need in their lives? What is it that they need to get promoted? What is it that they need to avoid failure? What causes them anxiety in the day-to-day of their lives or their work? And can you create some type of content product that is going to address this for them?
moments.summary: Content strategy requires the same rigor as product-market fit: understand what your audience needs to succeed, avoid failure, and reduce anxiety.

podcasts.title: How to hit revenue targets in a recession | Sahil Mansuri (Bravado)
podcasts.description: Hit revenue targets in a recession, covering B2B products, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Sahil Mansuri
moments.text: The way I think about setting up a plan when you have limited visibility and some major headwinds is setting up a really conservative plan and then having milestones, short term milestones that unlock the ability to lean into growth and spend based on hitting those targets.
moments.summary: In uncertain times, set a conservative base plan with short-term milestones that unlock permission to increase spending as targets are hit.

podcasts.title: How to be the best coach to product people | Petra Wille (Strong Product People)
podcasts.description: Be the best coach to product people, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and career development.

moments.speaker: Petra Wille
moments.text: Getting promoted is way harder if you're not good at telling stories and rallying the team behind a shared goal. You usually achieve this through good storytelling techniques. I would consider it a bit of a career staller if you don't get to a decent level of storytelling and to a decent level of public speaking.
moments.summary: Storytelling and public speaking are not optional soft skills for product leaders; they are essential for promotion and rallying teams behind shared goals.

podcasts.title: What it takes to become a top 1% PM | Ian McAllister (Uber, Amazon, Airbnb)
podcasts.description: What it takes to become a top 1% PM, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and career development.

moments.speaker: Ian McAllister
moments.text: If you forget about everything else, forget about politics, forget about promotion, or having a bigger org or whatever, if you simply wake up every day trying to have the biggest impact you can, or if you're a leader trying to use your team to have the biggest impact you can in the company, how you do every part of your day, that's a really good guiding light.
moments.summary: The simplest guiding principle for a top PM is to wake up every day focused on maximizing impact, ignoring politics and promotion.

podcasts.title: Leaving big tech to build the #1 technology newsletter | Gergely Orosz (The Pragmatic Engineer)
podcasts.description: Leaving big tech to build the #1 technology newsletter, covering practical product lessons, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Gergely Orosz
moments.text: In my best year at Uber, I made about 320 or $330,000 in total compensation. And when I quit my job, I was actually thinking, am I crazy? But now I am making more in compensation than I made at Uber. And the difference is that now my earnings keep going up as long as the newsletter is growing, so there's no theoretical cap on this.
moments.summary: Leaving a high-paying engineering role at Uber for an independent newsletter now generates more income with no theoretical cap on earnings.

podcasts.title: Alex Hardimen
podcasts.description: Is it our homes?" You have to help people understand, "Okay, when we talk product, we mean the website and app." What do you think of the product at The New York Times, covering product design, career development, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Alex Hardiman
moments.text: Our impact and our business goals are in service of our mission, which is to seek the truth and help people understand the world, not the other way around. When you're a product manager, you're involved in driving specific metrics like engagement or subscribers, but you're also trying to help stories find their real audience in ways that trigger this whole different side of mission and purpose driven impact. I didn't feel that when I was at a place like Facebook.
moments.summary: At The New York Times, product work serves mission-driven journalism first and business metrics second, creating a fundamentally different PM experience than at Facebook.

podcasts.title: How to fire people with grace, work through fear, and nurture innovation | Matt Mochary (CEO coach)
podcasts.description: Fire people with grace, work through fear, and nurture innovation, covering team leadership, startup building, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Matt Mochary
moments.text: The biggest marker that I've seen between a botched layoff and a successful layoff is at the moment someone hears that they no longer have a job, did they hear it from their manager in a one-on-one? If that's when they heard it, it'll be okay. But if they heard it in an email, in a group chat, in any kind of thing where they were sitting next to other people, it wasn't personalized, that is terrible. That's when people get really angry.
